strengths: A coherent latent diffusion framework over motion primitives with scheduled training enables real-time, online text-driven generation using only 10 diffusion steps, achieving strong efficiency and quality (e.g., temporal composition: segment FID 3.79 vs. FlowMDM 5.81; speed 334 fps vs. 31 fps; latency 0.02 s vs. 161.29 s; memory ~2394 MiB vs. ~11892 MiB).

Versatile spatial control via two mechanisms: latent noise optimization and RL in the latent action space, with compelling results (in-betweening: goal error 0.59 cm vs. DNO 4.24 cm, lower jerk/skate; goal-reaching: walk skill 17.08 s vs. GAMMA 31.44 s, higher success and less skating/floor distance; RL runs at ~240 fps).

The rebuttal provides concrete, substantive additions: systematic weak-label studies (pseudo frame labeling, hierarchical conditioning, curriculum mixing), quantitative scene-interaction metrics and broader benchmarks, optimization accelerations (warm starts, receding horizon, multi-resolution SDF, parallel multi-start), RL extensions to uneven terrains/3D goals/obstacles, comparisons to diffusion controllers (Shi et al., 2024), and a corrected denoiser formulation.

Methodological transparency with explicit algorithms, losses, architectures, and ablations (VAE effect, scheduled training, diffusion steps, primitive horizons) that justify design choices and contribute to long-horizon stability and controllability.

Autoregressive primitive design aligns well with atomic action semantics, supports smooth transitions, and yields favorable human preference outcomes on realism and semantics despite natural online transition delays.
weaknesses: Reliance on fine-grained frame-level labels remains a practical limitation; while the authors plan pseudo-labeling and hierarchical conditioning, comprehensive quantitative evidence across weakly labeled datasets (e.g., HML3D) is still pending.

Scene-conditioned control currently hinges on per-instance optimization that can be slow for longer sequences; although accelerations and broader quantitative metrics/baselines are promised, these results are not yet included in the current submission.
RL-based evaluations are still constrained in scope; richer 3D goals, complex environments, and broader baseline comparisons are planned but not yet present.
reviewers.summary_comment: DART presents a latent diffusion model over motion primitives with scheduled training that delivers real-time, online text-driven motion generation and flexible spatial control via optimization and RL. The paper demonstrates strong efficiency and quality improvements over competitive baselines (e.g., FlowMDM) and provides convincing results on in-betweening and goal-reaching. The authors’ rebuttal constructively addresses prior concerns, committing to systematic weak-label studies, quantitative scene-interaction metrics with broader benchmarks and speed–quality tradeoffs, RL extensions to uneven terrains and 3D goals with obstacle navigation, and additional baseline comparisons, while also clarifying the denoiser formulation. Although these additions are largely prospective and some limitations persist, the core method is sound, impactful, and now more clearly presented. I recommend acceptance, with the expectation that the promised expansions further strengthen the final version.

strengths: The authors provide concrete, well-scoped plans to handle weak labels and reduce ambiguity: pseudo frame labeling with calibrated confidence and thresholding, hierarchical conditioning over CLIP embeddings to mix local and global semantics, and curriculum mixing of strong/weak labels during scheduled training (Sec. 5, Appendix D.2, Appendix J).

Substantial expansion of spatial control evaluation and practicality: committing to quantitative SDF-based scene metrics (penetration depth/frequency, contact precision/recall, contact ratio, skating, goal-joint error) across multiple scenes with randomized trials, plus detailed speed–quality analyses and accelerations (warm starts, receding horizon/chunking, multi-resolution SDF, parallel multi-start).

Improved physical plausibility assessment: adding before/after physics-tracking metrics (penetration, skating, contact statistics, motion fidelity) and context via comparisons to physically simulated controllers where feasible, while clarifying that RL policies already include penalties for skating and penetration.

Clarity and technical correctness are addressed: fixing the x0-parameterized DDPM mean equation and symbol consistency, and extending representation ablations to analyze redundancy vs. controllability and effects on jitter/skating.

Retention of core strengths without altering the method: an efficient autoregressive latent primitive model (H=2, F=8) with scheduled training and 10-step diffusion enabling real-time generation, complemented by versatile control via latent optimization and RL policies.
weaknesses: Generalization beyond frame-aligned labels remains partly prospective: pseudo-labeling and hierarchical conditioning may introduce tagger bias, and results at scale for open or long-tail vocabularies are not yet demonstrated.

Optimization-based control can still be compute-intensive; while accelerations and trade-off analyses are planned, robustness across diverse scenes, longer horizons, and tight real-time constraints remains to be validated.

Quantitative comparisons to fully physics-based controllers are limited in scope and may be challenging to make strictly comparable; the approach remains inherently kinematic without integrated dynamics.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal effectively addresses the remaining concerns with concrete commitments: rigorous weak-label experiments (pseudo labeling with calibration/thresholding, hierarchical conditioning, curriculum mixing), expanded quantitative scene-control metrics and speed–quality trade-offs, and a thorough physical plausibility assessment before/after physics tracking, alongside broader RL comparisons and terrain variations. They also correct notation and enhance ablations on representation choices. While open-vocabulary generalization and fully real-time optimization under diverse scenes remain partially open, the planned additions strengthen the evaluation and clarity without changing the core method. Given the strong core contributions, efficiency, controllability, and the thoroughness of the planned revisions, I maintain my recommendation to accept.

strengths: Presents a latent diffusion-based autoregressive motion primitive model enabling real-time, online text-driven generation (~300 fps with 10 diffusion steps) and long, seamless rollouts.

Offers a unified and practical control toolkit: precise spatial control via latent noise optimization and efficient goal-reaching via RL policies, validated across in-betweening, human-scene interaction, and waypoint navigation.

Strong empirical foundation: extensive benchmarks against competitive baselines, diverse metrics (FID, R-prec/MM-Dist, jerk, diversity), human preference studies, efficiency profiling, and informative ablations (VAE, scheduled training, diffusion steps).

Rebuttal substantially strengthens rigor and clarity: commitments to add quantitative scene/SDF metrics, fairness protocols for in-betweening, RL evaluations on uneven terrains and additional baselines, and pre-/post-physics tracking analyses.
Clarity improvements promised and specific: correcting the x0-parameterized DDPM mean/notation, reporting transition-onset latency and lag-aware semantic metrics to contextualize online trade-offs.
weaknesses: Core dependence on fine-grained frame-level labels persists; while pseudo labeling, hierarchical conditioning, and curriculum mixing are planned, open-vocabulary generalization remains future work.

Several important additions are prospective (quantitative scene metrics, extended RL scenarios, physics-based comparisons, speed–quality studies); current main results do not yet reflect these extensions.

Optimization-based spatial control can be computationally heavy for long horizons; although proposed accelerations and hybrid RL initialization are sensible, end-to-end scalability and robustness remain to be demonstrated empirically.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper introduces DART, a latent diffusion-based autoregressive motion primitive model for real-time text-driven motion control, complemented by two effective spatial control strategies (latent optimization and RL). The technical approach is sound and well-motivated; current experiments already show high realism, efficiency, and favorable human preferences versus strong baselines. The rebuttal convincingly addresses prior concerns by committing to concrete additions: quantitative scene-interaction metrics, fairness in in-betweening, broader RL evaluations and baselines, physics-based tracking analyses, and notation fixes with latency-aware semantic evaluation. While reliance on fine-grained labels and the prospective nature of some extensions remain, the overall contribution is strong, practically impactful, and clearly presented. I recommend acceptance.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: The work sits at the intersection of deep generative models (latent diffusion), autoregressive modeling, and reinforcement learning for control—core ICML areas (deep learning, probabilistic methods, RL, optimization, and ML systems).

Contribution fit: A compact VAE + diffusion over motion primitives with scheduled training achieves real-time generation (10 steps, ~300 fps) and integrates two control mechanisms (latent noise optimization and RL policies). These are general ML contributions on efficient generative modeling and control.

Rigor fit: Reviewers highlight strong empirical evaluation and ablations (VAE effect, scheduled training, diffusion steps, primitive horizons), extensive metrics and human studies, and clarified/corrected denoiser formulation—matching ICML’s rigor expectations.

Novelty fit: While not purely theoretical, reviewers score novelty and significance well (4/5), noting a coherent framework that clearly advances state of the art in online, controllable generative modeling.

Presentation fit: Clarity is strong (5/5) with explicit algorithms and losses; authors addressed notation issues and added latency-aware context for online generation, aligning with ICML presentation standards.
why_it_does_not_fit: Topic emphasis is application-heavy (text-to-motion and human-scene interaction), which can be seen as more CV/graphics/embodied AI than the broader, less domain-specific focus often favored at ICML.

Contribution scope is largely engineering and systems for a specific domain (human motion); reviewers note reliance on fine-grained frame labels and that several evaluation extensions (scene-level metrics, broader RL scenarios, optimization speed-ups) are prospective, increasing acceptance risk at a general ML venue.
Novelty positioning may be perceived as incremental relative to prior diffusion-based motion generation/control work; limited theoretical insight or general ML principles may weaken ICML fit.

Reviewer risk: Despite positive reviews (accept, strong evaluation/clarity), remaining concerns (weak-label generalization, compute for long-horizon optimization, kinematic vs physical limits) suggest moderate risk for ICML’s higher bar on generality.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: NeurIPS explicitly welcomes interdisciplinary AI and embodied systems, human interaction, creatives, and decision-making/RL—this paper directly targets text-driven motion, embodied control, and creative/interactive applications.

Contribution fit: A unified framework that marries latent diffusion with autoregressive primitives and RL controllers matches NeurIPS’ appetite for practical yet principled ML systems spanning generative modeling, control, and data-centric training (scheduled training, label mixing).

Novelty fit: Reviewers rate novelty/significance/soundness high (mostly 4/5), noting real-time online generation with only 10 diffusion steps and superior controllability (e.g., in-betweening, goal-reaching), which advances state-of-the-practice in diffusion+control.

Rigor fit: Extensive benchmarks (FID, R-Prec/MM-Dist, jerk, diversity), human preference studies, speed/memory profiling, and ablations demonstrate strong empirical rigor—well aligned with NeurIPS standards for empirical ML.
Presentation fit: Clear algorithms/architectures/losses; reviewers note fixes to the x0-parameterized DDPM mean and plans for latency-aware semantic metrics, aiding community reproducibility.
Strategic fit: NeurIPS’ multi-track, interdisciplinary scope and acceptance of strong applied ML with solid empirical evidence makes it a strong venue for this work.
why_it_does_not_fit: Several key extensions are prospective (comprehensive scene/SDF metrics, broader RL baselines and terrains, optimization speed–quality trade-offs); some reviewers flag compute for long-horizon optimization and kinematic limitations, which add risk.

Dependence on fine-grained frame labels (BABEL) and limited open-vocabulary generalization may raise concerns for scalability/generalization among NeurIPS reviewers focused on data-centric robustness.

Minor semantic lag due to online transitions (acknowledged and natural) could affect some perception-based metrics; although human studies favor DART, this may invite discussion on evaluation choices.
Reviewer risk is moderate: overall positive reviews with accepts, but reliance on domain-specific datasets and prospective additions may still be scrutinized by NeurIPS’ broad audience.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Core ICLR themes—representation learning and deep generative models—are central: the paper learns a compact latent motion primitive space via a VAE and trains a latent diffusion denoiser with scheduled training for stable long-horizon rollout.

Contribution fit: Methodologically grounded DL contributions (autoregressive primitive representation, latent diffusion conditioned on motion history and text, scheduled training regime) and an RL controller in latent action space map well to ICLR’s focus on architectures and learning mechanisms.

Novelty fit: Reviewers score novelty/significance/soundness at 4/5 and praise the efficiency (10-step diffusion) and online controllability; the representation choice (H=2, F=8) and ablations provide insight into why the learned representation works, in the spirit of ICLR.
Rigor fit: Strong ablations and analysis (effects of VAE, training schedules, diffusion steps) and corrected denoiser formulation support ICLR’s standards for careful empirical methodology.
Presentation fit: Clear, thorough write-up (clarity 5/5) with algorithms and losses; added latency-aware metrics and notation fixes strengthen framing.
why_it_does_not_fit: ICLR often looks for broadly generalizable representation learning insights or theory; this work, while solid, is concentrated on a specific application (human motion) and may be viewed as engineering-heavy.

Reviewer-flagged limitations—reliance on frame-level labels, partial evidence for weak-label/open-vocabulary generalization, and kinematic vs physics constraints—could be seen as gaps relative to ICLR’s emphasis on generalizable representation learning.
Several evaluation expansions (scene metrics, broader RL settings, optimization accelerations) are prospective in the current version, adding some risk.

Strategic risk: While technically aligned, competing ICLR submissions may offer broader cross-domain demonstrations or deeper representation-learning analysis, slightly lowering the success likelihood versus NeurIPS.

strengths: Strong theoretical contributions with precise statements: Theorem 2 extends parity hardness to the finite-sample L2 setting; Theorem 5 shows one-step learning with teacher forcing under n = Ω(d^{2+ε}), oracle accuracy O(d^{-2−ε/8}), and η = Θ(d^{2+ε/16}); Theorem 7 shows logarithmic (log2 k) iterations without teacher forcing using self-consistency.

Mechanistic insight via interaction-term analysis (around Eqs. (6)–(10)) that isolates Θ(d^{-2}) gradient signals on the two correct positions, explaining how attention concentrates and how task decomposition emerges under the CoT objective (Eq. (2)) and its iterative scheme (Eqs. (3)–(4)).

Author rebuttal adds robustness and practicality: tolerance to small content leakage in K/Q; realizability and robustness of the link function φ by a small FFN with only O(δ) degradation; clarification that the block mask does not encode the unknown subset and an O(k)-step variant with standard causal masking is possible; a corollary replacing the adversarial oracle with minibatch concentration bounds; and a local Lipschitz argument to decompose the large update into a few small steps near initialization.

Qualitative experiments (d = 64; k ∈ {8,16,32}; ~100K samples) align with theory: the direct model stalls at ~0.5 error, CoT with teacher forcing converges, and self-consistency exhibits staged learning per level (Figures 4–5), matching the proofs’ phased optimization.

Planned empirical expansions are concrete and relevant: swapping φ for a trained 2-layer ReLU MLP, enabling content-dependent K/Q, removing the block mask (reporting O(k)-step behavior), sweeping filter thresholds and augmented data size, adding noise robustness, and testing 2-layer/multi-head variants with per-level accuracies.
weaknesses: The setting remains tailored to a stylized task: results hinge on a custom φ (even if realizable by a small FFN) and a self-consistency filter; generalization beyond parity to broader multi-step reasoning remains unproven and is only outlined as a roadmap.

Empirical evaluation in the submitted version is limited to synthetic parity without the promised ablations; sensitivity to architectural and training variations (e.g., alternative φ, content-dependent attention, masking schedules, noise) is not yet demonstrated.

Negative results still rely on a worst-case approximate-gradient framework; although the rebuttal provides a plan to connect to minibatch noise, the practical strength of the separation in real training remains partly interpretive.
reviewers.summary_comment: This paper delivers a clear theoretical account of how chain-of-thought style training can induce task decomposition in a one-layer transformer on the k-parity problem. It extends hardness to the finite-sample L2 setting (Theorem 2) and proves positive results: with teacher forcing, a single gradient step suffices (Theorem 5), and without teacher forcing, augmented self-consistency yields convergence in O(log k) iterations (Theorem 7). The analysis carefully isolates dominant Θ(d^{-2}) interaction terms that drive attention to the correct positions, and the experiments on d = 64, k ∈ {8,16,32} exhibit the predicted staged learning dynamics. The rebuttal substantively addresses realism concerns by arguing robustness to small content leakage in K/Q, realizability and tolerance of φ via a small FFN, clarifying that the hierarchical mask does not encode the solution (and providing an O(k)-step alternative), and sketching a corollary that replaces the adversarial oracle with minibatch noise along with a small-step variant of the one-step update. While the current empirical scope remains narrow and the setting is still tailored to parity, the theoretical contributions are solid and novel, and the planned additions will strengthen practical relevance. On balance, I recommend acceptance.

strengths: Clear theoretical separations: finite-sample hardness without intermediate supervision (Theorem 2), single-step learning with teacher forcing (Theorem 5), and logarithmic-step learning without teacher forcing via self-consistency (Theorem 7), each with precise quantitative regimes (e.g., n = Ω(d^{2+ε}), oracle tolerance O(d^{-2−ε/8}), learning rate η = Θ(d^{2+ε/16)).

Mechanistic analysis grounded in dominant interaction terms and gradient concentration on relevant positions (Eqs. (6)–(10)), with careful variance and concentration bounds supported by the appendices.

Author rebuttal substantively addresses robustness: small content leakage in K/Q (position-aware attention) shown to perturb only lower-order terms; φ realizability via a small FFN and a robustness lemma ensuring stability under δ-approximation; clarification that the block mask reduces propagation depth without encoding the target pairs; and an O(k)-step no-teacher-forcing variant under standard causal masking.

Concrete planned ablations will strengthen empirical evidence: replacing φ with a learned 2-layer ReLU MLP; enabling content channels in K/Q; removing the block mask and reporting per-level accuracies; sweeping self-consistency parameters and adding noise; testing 2-layer and multi-head variants; toggling quantization and using differentiable gates. These directly target previously noted concerns.

Experiments, though modest (e.g., d = 64, k ∈ {8,16,32}, ~100K samples), align with theory and show staged optimization and attention concentration (Figures 4–5), and quantization is not required in practice, suggesting it is mainly a proof convenience.
weaknesses: Positive results still rely on a stylized setup (one layer, single head, positional-only K/Q with pass-through V, bespoke φ with φ(0) = −1, φ(±1) = 1, and vanishing derivatives at these points); while the rebuttal promises robustness lemmas and ablations, these are not yet incorporated into the current manuscript.

Scope remains limited to parity; although a roadmap to generalization is provided (e.g., carry-chain arithmetic, bounded-depth language recognition), the paper does not yet demonstrate extensions empirically or theoretically to additional decomposable tasks.

The hardness result leverages an adversarial approximate gradient oracle; the authors add a minibatch gradient corollary to connect to practice and temper claims, but real-world relevance of the worst-case separation may still be perceived as limited until empirical corroboration is included.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal meaningfully strengthens the paper. It clarifies the role of the block mask (depth reduction without encoding pairs), provides robustness arguments for small content leakage in K/Q and for implementing or approximating the link function with a small FFN, and commits to concrete ablations that probe architectural and training variations (content-dependent attention, alternative φ via an MLP, masking/scheduling variants, self-consistency parameter sweeps, deeper/multi-head architectures, and quantization/gating choices). It also connects the hardness analysis to minibatch gradient noise and tempers claims about practical scope. While the current paper remains stylized and focused on parity, the theoretical contributions are solid, the empirical behavior (staged optimization) matches the mechanism, and the planned additions address key concerns. I recommend acceptance, with slightly elevated significance and evaluation scores reflecting the strengthened case.

strengths: The rebuttal substantively strengthens robustness and practicality: it commits to formal remarks that small content dependence in K/Q and learned V with bounded operator norm only perturb lower-order terms, preserving the dominant gradient signal identified in Eqs. (6)–(10) and the conclusions of Theorems 5 and 7.

The authors provide a clear path to relax the bespoke link function by using a small 2-layer ReLU MLP approximation, with a robustness lemma ensuring only O(δ) perturbations to the leading terms and preserving guarantees under modestly increased sample size; this ties back to Lemma 6 and the local quadratic behavior near {0, ±1}.

They clarify that the block causal mask (Figure 3 right) does not encode pair identities and that Theorem 5 holds with the standard causal mask (Figure 3 left); for no teacher forcing, the same mechanism applies without the block mask in O(k) steps, aligning the theory with more standard scheduling.

On the optimization side, the proposed small-step simulation lemma connects the one-step large-η update in Theorem 5 to practical training by showing several smaller steps near initialization approximate the same logits under a local Lipschitz bound (building on Lemma 4).

They address the oracle concern by adding a corollary for non-adversarial mini-batch SGD, using high-probability concentration to argue the tiny across-parity signal (Appendix A, near-diagonal empirical Gram) is still swamped, reinforcing Theorem 2’s practical relevance.

Concrete ablation plans enhance evaluation: learned φ, enabling content in K/Q, removing the block mask, differentiable gating for self-consistency (replacing non-differentiable filtering), toggling quantization, multi-head/2-layer variants, mini-batch SGD, and robustness to label/input noise, with per-level metrics to quantify staged learning (as seen in Figures 4–5 and Appendix D).
weaknesses: Even with robustness extensions, the setup remains stylized (position-dominant attention, pass-through V baseline, specialized φ, hierarchical scheduling), and the broader applicability to standard transformer stacks and tasks beyond parity remains to be demonstrated empirically.

Key empirical validations are still pending in the current submission: differentiable gating versus filtering, with/without quantization, learned φ and V, content-aware attention, multi-head or deeper architectures, and mini-batch SGD; acceptance hinges on including these ablations as promised.

The no–teacher-forcing guarantees rely on augmented data and a gating mechanism to enforce self-consistency (Eq. (3), Lemma 10); while practically motivated, the sensitivity to gate thresholds and augmented sample size n′ and the trade-offs in convergence are not yet quantified beyond parity.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper provides rigorous and insightful results on how chain-of-thought style training enables a one-layer transformer to solve k-parity: a finite-sample hardness result without intermediate supervision (Theorem 2), a single-update learning guarantee with teacher forcing (Theorem 5) under loss (2), and logarithmic-step learning without teacher forcing via a self-consistency mechanism (Theorem 7) under loss (4) and generation (3). The technical development—gradient signal isolation through interaction terms (Eqs. (6)–(10)), concentration bounds (Lemmas 9 and 10), and gradient norm control (Lemmas 4 and 6)—is careful and convincing, and the staged learning observed experimentally (Figures 4–5; d=64, k in {8,16,32}, ~100K samples, filter threshold 0.4) aligns with the theory. The rebuttal meaningfully addresses prior concerns by adding robustness to attention/content and φ, clarifying that the block mask does not hard-code the decomposition, proposing a small-step simulation lemma and a stochastic-gradient corollary, and committing to targeted ablations (learned φ/V, content-aware K/Q, masking variants, differentiable gating, quantization toggles, multi-head/deeper models, mini-batch SGD, and noise robustness). While the architecture remains stylized and several ablations are pending inclusion, the core theoretical contributions are sound and significant, and the planned additions should further strengthen empirical support and clarity. I recommend acceptance.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Strong alignment with ICML’s Theory of ML and Deep Learning tracks—provable training dynamics of transformers, gradient-based hardness, and CoT learning mechanisms are core ML topics.

Contribution fit: Primarily theoretical (Theorems 2/5/7) with light empirical confirmation, matching ICML’s appetite for solid theory with some validation (optimization, generalization, and architectural analysis).
Novelty fit: Reviewers rate novelty high (4/5) and emphasize clear separations (finite-sample hardness vs. CoT success, single-step vs. logarithmic-step), which is appropriate for ICML’s bar.
Rigor fit: Soundness is strong (scores 4–5/5) with careful variance/concentration bounds and mechanistic analysis (interaction-term isolation), typical of successful ICML theory papers.

Presentation fit: Clarity rated 4–5/5; the framing situates results in CoT literature and connects to practice via planned ablations and a minibatch corollary, easing accessibility for the ICML audience.
why_it_does_not_fit: Scope is stylized (one-layer, single-head, position-only K/Q, bespoke φ, synthetic parity), and empirical evaluation is limited—ICML reviewers sometimes expect broader applicability or stronger experiments.

Some positive results rely on a custom link function and self-consistency gate; despite rebuttal robustness arguments, the reliance on specialized components may be viewed as narrow for ICML’s broad ML community.
Hardness separation uses an approximate-gradient oracle (though tempered by a minibatch corollary); some ICML reviewers may question practical impact without the promised ablations in the submission.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: NeurIPS explicitly solicits Theory, Deep Learning, and General ML contributions; chain-of-thought reasoning in transformers is of broad current interest to the NeurIPS community.

Contribution fit: Clear theoretical separations and mechanisms (attention focusing via dominant Θ(d^{-2}) terms) align with NeurIPS theory track expectations; small experiments support staged learning.
Novelty fit: Reviewers judge novelty and significance as strong (4/5), and the work connects to contemporary CoT debates, increasing potential visibility.
Rigor fit: High soundness and careful appendices match NeurIPS standards for proofs and training dynamics analysis.
Presentation fit: Reviewers praise clarity (4–5/5) and the authors’ added practical connections (minibatch corollary, small-step simulation) improve accessibility for a diverse NeurIPS audience.
why_it_does_not_fit: NeurIPS often favors either broader empirical scope or clear impact beyond synthetic tasks; current experiments remain limited to parity and promised ablations are not yet included.
Architectural stylization (custom φ, positional-only attention, gating for self-consistency) may raise concerns about generality for a venue that prioritizes broad relevance and practical impact.

The hardness relies on an adversarial oracle for the primary statement; despite a corollary, some NeurIPS reviewers may view the practical separation as less compelling without additional empirical corroboration.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Directly in ICLR’s wheelhouse—deep learning theory and representation learning for transformers, with a focus on chain-of-thought and training dynamics.

Contribution fit: Theoretical analysis of how CoT emerges in transformers (teacher forcing vs. self-consistency) closely mirrors many recent ICLR works cited in the paper; small but illustrative experiments are acceptable for ICLR theory-oriented submissions.
Novelty fit: Reviewers rate novelty high (4/5) and highlight new finite-sample hardness plus efficient learning guarantees, matching ICLR’s bar for innovative deep-learning theory.
Rigor fit: Soundness 4–5/5 with detailed proofs and mechanistic insights is well aligned with ICLR’s acceptance of stylized, theory-focused deep learning analyses.
Presentation fit: Clarity 4–5/5 and strong positioning within CoT/transformer literature; the paper references and extends ICLR-cited works, making it legible and relevant to ICLR attendees.
why_it_does_not_fit: Empirical section remains narrow and some robustness/ablations are deferred; while often acceptable at ICLR for theory work, this could still be a minor weakness.
Results rely on a bespoke φ and gating mechanism; even with robustness arguments, some ICLR reviewers may ask for additional evidence that findings transfer to more standard transformer stacks.

strengths: The authors commit to a tagged camera-ready release with all new scripts/data and a validation checklist, including post-style invariance checks (unit tests for Code, ground-truth/verifier checks for Math, refusal detection for Safety, and stratified human spot checks for Chat), which directly addresses substance-drift concerns.

They will add a multi-source extension (e.g., Llama-3.1-405B, Gemini-1.5-Pro, Claude-3.5) with per-source metrics and cross-source robustness diagnostics, reducing single-LM bias and improving benchmark generality.

Safety confounds are addressed via style-neutralization and a same-family harmful-generation auxiliary subset, alongside lexical/style distance diagnostics—strengthening substance-vs-style isolation in the most sensitive domain.

Correlation analyses are recalibrated and enhanced with stronger statistics (bootstrap CIs, permutation tests, Spearman), sensitivity analyses (seeds, PPO hyperparameters), an additional base family, and inclusion of a DPO-aligned policy model; claims are explicitly toned to moderate under controlled conditions.

Clarity and technical precision will improve via consistent dataset counts, cleaned formatting, and a standard DPO reward formalization with citations; bias coverage is broadened through a Style-Control+ extension (e.g., code-only vs. code+explanation, cue-phrase biases, tone), with per-axis reporting and tooling.
weaknesses: A substantial portion of the improvements remain promises contingent on the camera-ready; the final strength of claims depends on execution quality and transparent reporting of pass rates and diagnostics.
Even with added statistics, correlations remain moderate and established under controlled settings; generalization across diverse training regimes and model families may still be limited.

The multi-source and safety same-family subsets may be limited in scope initially; residual stylistic or distributional gaps could persist without comprehensive coverage and quantitative thresholds (e.g., semantic similarity cutoffs, inter-annotator agreement for human checks).
Bias axes beyond length/markdown are only now being added; although welcome, broader adversarial coverage and artifact-rate reporting will be needed to reduce benchmark hacking.
reviewers.summary_comment: The second response provides concrete, verifiable commitments that substantially address prior concerns: substance-preserving style transforms with domain-specific validators and human checks, a multi-source data extension with cross-source diagnostics, safety ablations to minimize stylistic confounds, stronger and more cautiously framed correlation analyses, and improved clarity and DPO formalization. While several upgrades are pending execution and correlations remain moderate under controlled conditions, the planned tagged release and detailed reporting mitigate risk. Given the importance of rigorously probing style-over-substance in reward models and the authors’ concrete plans to deliver the requested validations and extensions, I maintain my acceptance recommendation.

strengths: The authors commit to concrete camera-ready deliverables that directly tackle stylistic confounds: multi-source data generation with per-source metrics and cross-source robustness tests, safety style-neutralization, and a same-family safety subset.

Statistical rigor is substantially improved with planned bootstrap confidence intervals, permutation tests, Spearman correlations, multi-seed and hyperparameter sweeps, a DPO-aligned policy setting, and expanded analysis on a second base family.

Data quality controls are strengthened via minimal-edit error injection, grammaticality and semantic similarity checks, and post-transformation invariance validations (unit tests for code, ground-truth/verifiers for math, refusal detectors for safety, human spot checks for chat), with flipped-label variants discarded and scripts/logs released.

Bias coverage is broadened through a Style-Control+ extension (code-only vs code+explanation, fenced code vs plain text, prompt-phrase cues, rhetorical formality) with per-axis matrices and generation/validation scripts, reducing avenues for benchmark hacking.

Transparency and interpretability are enhanced: documentation of annotator protocols and inter-annotator agreement, selection-bias diagnostics, clearer guidance on the Style–Substance matrix, and more careful DPO vs sequence-classifier comparisons with confidence intervals and outlier discussion.
weaknesses: Several remedies are slated for the camera-ready and not yet reflected in the current results; continued reliance on gpt-4o and mixed-LM generation in safety, plus a potentially small same-source safety subset, means residual stylistic confounds may persist.

Correlation evidence remains moderate and may vary across model families and training regimes; chat split size and jailbreak success constraints could continue to limit statistical power and ecological validity.

Quantitative thresholds and pass rates for the new validation steps (e.g., semantic similarity cutoffs, style-neutralization leakage metrics) are not yet reported, so the effectiveness of these safeguards remains to be demonstrated.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal thoughtfully addresses prior concerns with specific, verifiable additions: multi-source generation and safety neutralization to mitigate stylistic confounds, stronger statistical reporting and sensitivity analyses, expanded evaluation settings, improved error-injection and style-preservation checks, and broader style/bias axes with released scripts and data cards. While many enhancements are promised for the camera-ready and current results still reflect some limitations, the planned updates materially improve soundness and robustness without changing the core contribution. Given the benchmark’s clear design, broad evaluation, and strengthened methodology, I recommend acceptance, contingent on incorporating the promised artifacts and reporting refinements.

strengths: Authors commit to a tagged camera-ready release with a multi-source extension (e.g., Llama-3.1-405B, Gemini-1.5-Pro, Claude-3.5), per-source metrics, cross-source train→test swaps, and style-neutralization ablations, directly addressing single-source and stylistic confounds.

Data quality and transparency will be substantially improved via a documented annotation protocol (double annotation, adjudication), inter-annotator agreement reporting, post-transform invariance checks per domain, and representativeness diagnostics (e.g., JS divergence) with scripts and logs released.

Correlation claims will be better calibrated and statistically supported with bootstrapped confidence intervals, permutation and Spearman tests, multi-seed and hyperparameter sweeps, inclusion of DPO-aligned policies, and expanded base-family analysis (LLaMA-3-8B), plus CIs for Best-of-N.

Safety evaluation will be strengthened through same-family harmful/refusal generation, style-neutralization, and lexical/style-distance reporting, reducing separability driven by cross-model style differences.

Clear versioning and usability improvements (v1.0 single-source, v1.1 multi-source; style-control extensions; data cards; validation and analysis notebooks) enhance reproducibility and help users track changes.
Clarity issues are explicitly addressed: reconciling chat counts, standardized DPO reward notation, and typographical fixes, along with clearer interpretation guidance for the Style–Substance matrix.
weaknesses: Key enhancements remain contingent on the camera-ready/repository update; until v1.1 is integrated and results reported, residual GPT-4o artifacts and single-source bias may persist in the current evaluation.

Correlations are moderate and task-dependent; even with added rigor and broader settings, generalization across policy families and training regimes may remain limited and should be cautiously framed.

Chat coverage and selection bias from jailbreak-based perturbations, though mitigated by scaling and minimal-edit strategies, may still be constrained until expanded datasets and validations are delivered.
Expanding style-control axes risks benchmark fragmentation and user confusion if not carefully versioned and prioritized; clear guidance on which metrics to use for model selection will be important.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors provide detailed, verifiable plans that meaningfully address remaining concerns. The multi-source generation and cross-source robustness analyses, style-neutralization, and same-family controls directly target stylistic confounds. The enhanced annotation protocol, inter-annotator agreement, invariance validations, and representativeness diagnostics improve data integrity. Correlation analyses will be more rigorous and better scoped, with broader policy settings and uncertainty reporting. Clarity and usability improvements are specific and actionable, with clear versioning to preserve comparability. While some commitments are pending the camera-ready release and correlations will likely remain moderate, the proposed additions are feasible and substantially strengthen the contribution. I maintain acceptance with elevated evaluation and clarity scores.

why_it_fits: Strong alignment with ICML’s evaluation/replicability and trustworthy ML topics: the work is an evaluation-centric benchmark for RLHF reward models with explicit style–substance controls, matching ICML’s interests in evaluation methodology, validity, and human-in-the-loop ML.

Broad, ML-relevant coverage (Chat, Code, Math, Safety) and commitments to multi-source generation (e.g., Llama‑3.1‑405B, Gemini‑1.5‑Pro, Claude‑3.5) and cross-source diagnostics increase generality—attributes valued by ICML for community benchmarks.

Methodological rigor upgrades (bootstrap CIs, permutation tests, Spearman correlations, multi-seed/hyperparameter sweeps, inclusion of DPO-aligned policy) and domain-specific validators (unit tests for Code, ground truth/verifiers for Math, refusal detection for Safety, stratified human spot checks for Chat) address replicability and soundness concerns ICML reviewers often emphasize.

Trustworthy ML angle is explicit: isolating style bias and reporting bias axes (Style-Control+ such as code-only vs code+explanation, cue-phrase biases, tone) aligns with ICML’s focus on reliability and robustness.
why_it_does_not_fit: Primary contribution is a benchmark and diagnostics rather than a new algorithm/theory; ICML can favor stronger methodological or theoretical innovations, making acceptance more contingent on community impact.

Reviewer feedback notes that several key upgrades are promised for the camera-ready (multi-source data, validation thresholds, expanded bias axes), raising risk if any elements are not fully delivered; correlations remain moderate under controlled settings.

Residual confounds may persist initially (continued reliance on GPT‑4o in parts of the pipeline, mixed-LM safety generation, limited same-family safety subset), which could invite ICML critiques about external validity and generalization.
Chat split size and jailbreak-based perturbation constraints may limit statistical power and ecological validity; ICML reviewers often scrutinize dataset construction and selection bias.

why_it_fits: Excellent match to NeurIPS’ data-centric and evaluation focus (and its Datasets & Benchmarks framing): the paper contributes a carefully designed benchmark, a Style–Substance evaluation matrix, and Style-Control+ axes that are directly in scope.

Addresses socio-technical aspects (style bias, spurious correlations) and human interaction in AI systems, both explicitly highlighted in NeurIPS topics; transparency commitments (annotation protocols, inter-annotator agreement, scripts/logs) strengthen the data-centric contribution.

Broad empirical scope and strong statistical treatment (bootstrap CIs, permutation tests, Spearman) plus sensitivity analyses across seeds/hyperparameters and an additional base model family align with NeurIPS’ standards for rigorous empirical work.

Clear practical utility: moderate yet improved correlation with downstream policy performance and Best-of-N, plus per-source metrics and cross-source robustness tests make the resource actionable for the community.
why_it_does_not_fit: Reviewers emphasize that correlations are moderate and established in controlled settings; NeurIPS often looks for stronger evidence of generalization across model families/training regimes, which remains a caveat.

Some safeguards and multi-source expansions are camera‑ready commitments; NeurIPS Datasets & Benchmarks reviewers can be strict about artifacts being complete and documented (e.g., quantitative thresholds, pass rates).

Safety subsets and same‑family controls may still be small initially, and continued reliance on GPT‑4o in parts of the pipeline risks residual stylistic confounds—potential sticking points for NeurIPS’ rigorous dataset standards.

Comparisons like DPO vs sequence-classifier can be sensitive; incomplete or caveated results may temper enthusiasm among NeurIPS reviewers who expect comprehensive ablations with uncertainty estimates.

why_it_fits: Directly within ICLR’s focus on deep learning and alignment: evaluates reward models central to RLHF/DPO pipelines for LLMs, with concrete analyses connecting reward-model performance to policy outcomes.
Contribution type (benchmark + empirical methodology) is common at ICLR, especially for LLM alignment and evaluation; reviewers rated soundness/evaluation/clarity highly and recommended acceptance.

Clear technical framing (standardized DPO reward formalization, improved clarity, cleaned formatting) and strong empirical rigor (validators per domain, multi-source expansion, style-neutralization) align with ICLR’s expectations for careful empirical DL work.

Addresses prominent community concern (style-over-substance bias) with a principled design (Style–Substance matrix) and demonstrates moderate positive correlations with downstream tasks and style-controlled policy evaluations.
why_it_does_not_fit: Some enhancements were pending at camera-ready time (e.g., multi-source integration, expanded bias axes, quantitative thresholds/pass rates), creating limited residual risk if full delivery or reporting lags.

Correlations remain moderate and task/family dependent; while acceptable for ICLR’s empirical track, this can constrain the strength of claims and may draw pushback from reviewers seeking stronger generalization evidence.
As a benchmark rather than a new algorithmic advance, a minority of ICLR reviewers might prefer more methodological novelty; nonetheless, the paper’s acceptance indicates this was not a blocker.

strengths: Spider 2.0 offers a realistic, enterprise-grade benchmark spanning multi-dialect cloud/local databases, very large schemas, external documents, and project-level codebases (including DBT), far beyond prior text-to-SQL settings.

The authors provide a thorough construction and QC pipeline (surface/semantic rewrites, multi-annotator review, execution-based evaluators) and, in the rebuttal, clarify that the focused evaluator matches values on essential columns and commit to releasing both focused and strict evaluators.

Extensive empirical evaluation and analysis: agentic and non-agentic baselines, multiple LLM families, difficulty stratification, dialect analyses, and an informative error taxonomy; the rebuttal adds concrete commitments with timelines for manual adjudication, retrieval baselines, standardized value-linking resources (column sketches), unified input-length controls/schema pruning, and Spider-Agent component ablations.

Multiple complementary settings (Spider 2.0 agentic and 2.0-lite/2.0-snow) facilitate both realistic workflow evaluation and easier benchmarking; authors commit to a local-only subset (DuckDB/SQLite snapshots), cost-aware runbooks, and releasing prompts/truncation scripts to improve accessibility and reproducibility.

The results convincingly expose a sizable capability gap (e.g., ~21% SR for Spider 2.0 with o1-preview; ~5–6% EX on 2.0-lite and ~2% on 2.0-snow for top baselines), underscoring high significance for the community.
weaknesses: Several key improvements (strict EX and adjudication results, retrieval baselines, column-sketch value resources, unified input-length controls, and Spider-Agent ablations) are promised with clear timelines but are not yet reflected in the current results, leaving some fairness and rigor questions pending.

Despite mitigations, practical hurdles remain: dependence on cloud warehouses and associated costs; DBT tasks are still a minority; difficulty is primarily measured by token length, and additional complexity indicators (e.g., CTE depth, joins, nested/partitioned schema flags, external-doc dependency) would enhance interpretability.
reviewers.summary_comment: This work introduces Spider 2.0, a timely and ambitious benchmark that substantially elevates text-to-SQL evaluation by incorporating multi-dialect systems (BigQuery, Snowflake, etc.), massive and complex schemas, external documentation, and realistic project contexts (including DBT). The dataset construction is carefully documented, and the experiments are broad and revealing: even strong systems struggle (e.g., o1-preview ~21% SR on Spider 2.0; best text-to-SQL baselines ~5–6% EX on 2.0-lite and ~2% on 2.0-snow). The rebuttal addresses earlier concerns convincingly, clarifying value-level matching in the focused evaluator and committing, with timelines, to release strict evaluators and report both metrics, conduct a manual adjudication study, add retrieval baselines and standardized value-linking resources, enforce unified input-length controls with schema pruning, provide a local-only subset and cost guidance, and perform Spider-Agent ablations. While these enhancements are not yet reflected in the submitted results and cloud/cost constraints and limited DBT coverage remain practical concerns, the core contribution is substantial, well-motivated, and likely to have strong impact. With the planned additions, rigor and fairness will further improve. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: The rebuttal provides concrete, scoped commitments that directly address fairness and rigor: three standardized value-linking tracks (no resources, column sketches/dictionaries, full linking with budget caps), dual evaluators (focused and strict) with released per-task configurations, and a stratified human adjudication study.

Reproducibility is strengthened via a local-only subset (DuckDB/SQLite snapshots where licensing permits), containerized DBT environments, per-task cost estimates and runbooks (dry-run, partition pruning, caching), and mirrored, versioned artifacts with DOIs on GitHub/Zenodo.

Methodological clarity is improved with planned uniform input-length controls, schema-pruning baselines, and comprehensive Spider-Agent ablations (schema exploration, SampleRows, external-doc access, self-reflection, planning variants, pass@k/temperature, step limits) plus released prompts, truncation scripts, and trajectories.

The paper already offers strong empirical evidence of challenge (e.g., o1-preview agent around 21% SR on Spider 2.0; best text-to-SQL baselines around 5–6% EX on Spider 2.0-lite), and the rebuttal’s multi-track reporting and strict evaluator will make these gaps more interpretable and comparable.

Clear scoping of the human adjudication (n=180, stratified by dialect and difficulty, three annotators, Fleiss’ kappa, FP/FN rates vs. focused and strict EX) demonstrates a thoughtful plan to validate metrics and reduce evaluation bias.
weaknesses: Many improvements (multi-track value linking, strict evaluator reporting, adjudication results, local snapshots, expanded ablations) are promised for the camera-ready/leaderboard but are not yet available; impact depends on timely and complete delivery.

Cloud dependence and licensing constraints may still limit full replication for some heavy tasks, and small strata (e.g., Postgres, ClickHouse) in adjudication could yield wide confidence intervals despite stratification.

Uniform schema-pruning and input-length controls are useful baselines but may not fully capture the diversity of retrieval/value-linking strategies; requesting at least a pilot strict-evaluator score and a representative agent ablation in the camera-ready would help substantiate the planned improvements.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors have substantively and concretely addressed prior concerns. They commit to multi-track value-linking protocols (including cost-capped full linking), release both focused and strict evaluators with per-task configs, and conduct a stratified human adjudication (n=180) to quantify FP/FN rates and agreement. Reproducibility is improved through local-only subsets, containerized DBT environments, detailed cost runbooks, and mirrored artifacts with DOIs. Planned uniform input-length controls and extensive Spider-Agent ablations, alongside released prompts and trajectories, will add methodological clarity. While these enhancements are commitments pending delivery, their specificity and scope are convincing and, if implemented, will materially strengthen fairness, rigor, and accessibility. Given the benchmark’s clear novelty and significance and the robust plan to shore up evaluation and reproducibility, I maintain an accept recommendation with a high evaluation score.

strengths: Introduces a highly realistic enterprise benchmark that extends beyond traditional text-to-SQL to include agentic workflows with codebases, external documentation, and diverse SQL dialects across BigQuery, Snowflake, SQLite, DuckDB, Postgres, and ClickHouse.

Clear empirical gap demonstrated: the best code-agent setup (Spider-Agent + o1-preview) achieves ~21.36% success rate on Spider 2.0 (Table 6), while leading text-to-SQL baselines achieve only ~5.68% EX on Spider 2.0-lite and ~2.20% on Spider 2.0-snow (Table 5), underscoring difficulty and significance.

Robust methodology and analysis: multi-step annotation with surface/semantic rewrites, multi-round validation, and detailed error taxonomy (e.g., nested-schema linking failures and document-grounding errors illustrated in Figs. 29 and 23); thoughtful agent action space and ablation-ready framework.

Author rebuttal substantially strengthens evaluation and reproducibility: commitments to release a strict evaluator alongside the focused EX, a stratified human adjudication study (180 items, three annotators, Fleiss’ κ), a Spider 2.0-local subset (DuckDB/SQLite) with materialized golds where feasible, containerized DBT environments, and mirrored artifacts (GitHub/Zenodo).

Planned multi-faceted difficulty annotations (joins, CTE counts and nesting depth, window functions, advanced dialect functions, nested/partitioned tables, cross-schema access, external-document requirements, multi-step planning, and project context) plus stratified reporting will enable more nuanced, fair comparisons.
weaknesses: Several key enhancements (strict evaluator with side-by-side reporting, human adjudication, multi-faceted difficulty taxonomy, local-only subset, and expanded Spider-Agent ablations) are promised for the camera-ready and leaderboard release but are not yet reflected in the current results.

Despite cost-aware guidance and local subsets, full-benchmark accessibility still depends on cloud warehouses and may incur non-trivial costs; not all tasks can be mirrored locally due to licensing/data constraints.

Current ablation depth for the agent is limited; isolating the contribution of components such as schema exploration, retrieval of dialect docs, execution-feedback loops, and planning strategies would further clarify what capabilities matter most.
reviewers.summary_comment: Spider 2.0 is a timely and impactful benchmark that pushes text-to-SQL toward realistic enterprise settings with large, complex schemas, diverse dialects, external documents, and project-level workflows. The paper clearly demonstrates the performance gap of current systems, e.g., ~21.36% SR for the best agentic model on Spider 2.0 (Table 6) and ~5.68%/~2.20% EX for strong baselines on Spider 2.0-lite/snow (Table 5). The methodology is careful (surface/semantic rewrites, multi-round validation) and the analysis is thorough, highlighting concrete failure modes like nested-schema linking errors and document-grounding issues. The authors’ rebuttal convincingly addresses prior concerns by committing to release a strict evaluator with per-task configs, a stratified human adjudication of 180 items, a local-only subset with containerized DBT, cost-aware runbooks, mirrored artifacts, expanded difficulty annotations, and component ablations (including retrieval baselines). While some of these improvements are slated for the camera-ready/leaderboard release and cloud costs remain a practical hurdle, the overall contribution is substantial, well-motivated, and likely to catalyze progress on robust, dialect-aware, workflow-grounded agents. I recommend acceptance.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: ICML explicitly welcomes evaluation methodology, replicability/validity studies, ML systems, and application-driven ML; Spider 2.0 is a data-centric benchmark that evaluates LLM agents and text-to-SQL across multi-dialect databases and real enterprise workflows, aligning with ICML’s evaluation and systems topics.

Contribution fit: The work contributes a benchmark (Spider 2.0, 2.0-lite, 2.0-snow), an agent framework, execution-based evaluators, and an error taxonomy—matching ICML interests in evaluation methodology and ML systems rather than solely algorithms.

Novelty fit: Reviewers consistently rate novelty and significance high (novelty=5, significance=5), and highlight the strong empirical finding of a large capability gap (e.g., ~21% SR on Spider 2.0, ~5–6% EX on 2.0-lite), indicating appropriate ambition for a top venue.

Rigor fit: The paper details multi-stage annotation/QC, execution-based evaluation, and—per reviews—commits to strict evaluators, human adjudication, value-linking tracks, and schema-pruning baselines, strengthening fairness and rigor.

Presentation fit: Clarity is rated positively (clarity=4), and the authors commit to releasing prompts, truncation scripts, trajectories, and cost-aware runbooks, aiding reproducibility—an ICML priority for evaluation papers.
why_it_does_not_fit: Contribution expectations: ICML often favors methodological or theoretical advances beyond benchmarks; reviewers note several improvements (strict evaluator, retrieval baselines, adjudication, ablations) were promises at review time, which may raise concerns about completeness for a main ICML track.
Reviewer risk: Pending additions and cloud dependence/costs (noted by reviewers) introduce risk for fairness/replicability at submission time, which can make acceptance at ICML less certain.

Scope balance: The work is systems- and benchmark-heavy (DBT, BigQuery/Snowflake, agent action space), which may resonate more strongly with venues/track structures that explicitly emphasize datasets and benchmarks compared to some ICML areas.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: NeurIPS lists Data-centric aspects of AI, SysML Infrastructure, General ML, Evaluation, and has a well-established Datasets & Benchmarks ecosystem; Spider 2.0’s data-centric, systems-grounded evaluation of LLM agents for text-to-SQL maps directly to these areas.

Contribution fit: A comprehensive benchmark plus agent framework, evaluators (focused and strict), extensive analyses, and planned ablations aligns strongly with NeurIPS’s Datasets/Benchmarks and Systems interests.
Novelty fit: Reviewers rate novelty and significance at the top end and emphasize the paper’s exposure of a sizable capability gap; NeurIPS D&B often seeks precisely such impactful findings.

Rigor fit: Multi-annotator QC, execution-based scoring, and—per reviews—commitments to strict evaluators, multi-track value linking with budget caps, stratified human adjudication, and uniform input-length controls match NeurIPS D&B rigor and fairness expectations.

Presentation fit: Reviewers praise methodological clarity and reproducibility plans (local-only subset, containerized DBT, mirrored artifacts with DOIs), which align with NeurIPS reproducibility policies.
why_it_does_not_fit: Reviewer risk: Several key elements were promised for camera-ready/leaderboard (strict evaluator reporting, adjudication results, expanded ablations); NeurIPS D&B requires timely delivery, so slippage would introduce acceptance risk.
Practical constraints: Cloud dependence and licensing limitations may hinder exact replication of some heavy tasks despite local subsets—reviewers flag this as a residual concern.

Track alignment detail: If submitted to the main research track rather than D&B, the benchmark-centric nature (vs. algorithmic/theoretical contributions) could face higher bar; selecting the correct NeurIPS track is important for success.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: ICLR explicitly welcomes deep learning evaluation, datasets/benchmarks, and applications involving LLMs/agents; Spider 2.0 evaluates LLM-based code agents and text-to-SQL within complex, real-world contexts—squarely within ICLR’s modern scope.

Contribution fit: Beyond the dataset, the paper provides an agent framework, execution/evaluation methodology, extensive error taxonomy, and ablation-ready design—elements that ICLR commonly accepts for impactful benchmarking work.
Novelty fit: Reviewers rate novelty/significance at the top end and emphasize the revealed capability gap; the contribution is timely for the deep learning/agent community ICLR serves.
Rigor fit: The construction pipeline, multi-round validation, and commitments to strict evaluators and human adjudication address rigor and fairness; reviewers found the plans concrete and scoped.

Presentation fit: Clarity and breadth of experiments are rated positively; the paper’s framing around real enterprise workflows, dialect diversity, and agentic tasks is well articulated and aligns with ICLR’s appetite for analyses that drive research directions.
why_it_does_not_fit: Pending additions: As reviewers note, some promised improvements (strict evaluator reporting, adjudication, expanded ablations) were not in the initial results, which could have been a concern absent clear timelines.
Practical hurdles: Cloud dependence and cost considerations can impact reproducibility for some readers, even with planned local subsets.

Systems emphasis: While acceptable at ICLR (and indeed the paper is already accepted), the strong systems/benchmark orientation could, in other contexts, face questions from reviewers seeking algorithmic novelty; in this case, the significance overcame that risk.

strengths: SWR is a clear, simple per-step, per-layer rescaling that preserves proportionality via explicit bias scaling (Theorem 1) with negligible overhead; the algorithm handles normalization layers and separates feature extractor/classifier via λ_f and λ_c.

Theory within scope is solid: boundedness under an empirically supported O(√t) growth assumption (Appendix B, Theorem 2) and improved inter-layer balance (Appendix C, Theorem 3). The rebuttal further clarifies BN training-mode invariance, promises a lemma, and introduces a logit-preserving C=1 variant to avoid calibration changes.

Empirically, the method shows promising gains: Table 1 reports ~+4.36 points on TinyImageNet with VGG-16 (0.4348 vs. 0.3912 vanilla), and Table 5 with LR decay shows SWR up to 0.5221 vs. 0.4126 vanilla; Figures 3–4 indicate SWR surpasses S&P in mid/late continual stages without performance drops.

The rebuttal commits to substantially strengthening the evaluation: more seeds (VGG-16 from 3 to 5; small models to 10), broader and fairer baselines (full S&P, spectral norm, weight clipping, Niehaus 2024, RIFLE, and continual baselines like LwF/EWC/SI/ER), denser hyperparameter sweeps with matched tuning budgets, optimizer-agnostic analyses, spectral norm trajectories, BN recalibration ablations, and code release.
weaknesses: Novelty remains moderate relative to prior rescaling and initialization-aware methods; SWR’s stepwise proportionality is useful but incremental compared to periodic rescaling and L2-to-initial strategies.

Theoretical scope excludes non-homogeneous architectures (residual connections, attention) and provides boundedness under per-step norm-change assumptions without deeper optimizer-specific or spectral analyses in the current manuscript.

As submitted, the empirical evaluation and presentation have gaps: limited seeds for large models, simplified baselines (e.g., S&P variant, limited head reset), coarse hyperparameter sweeps, missing ablations on λ_f/λ_c and rescaling frequency, and un-audited BN running-statistics effects at inference; clarity issues (formatting artifacts, dense pseudo-code) impede reproducibility.
Many strong improvements are promised in the rebuttal but are not yet implemented in the paper, making it difficult to judge the full rigor and robustness at this stage.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper proposes Soft Weight Rescaling, a per-step, proportionality-preserving rescaling method that curbs weight growth and improves inter-layer balance without adding loss terms. Within its stated scope (feed-forward nets with homogeneous activations), the theoretical analysis is sound, and empirical results are encouraging in warm-start and continual learning, with notable gains on VGG-16/TinyImageNet (e.g., +~4.36 points over vanilla; with LR decay SWR reaches 0.5221 vs. 0.4126). The rebuttal thoughtfully addresses normalization-layer nuances, proposes a logit-preserving C=1 variant, and commits to broader baselines, increased seeds, optimizer/spectral analyses, and clarity/code improvements. However, these enhancements are not yet reflected in the submission, and novelty remains moderate. Given the current state, I recommend rejection, with the view that the work could become compelling if the promised additions and analyses are fully integrated and reported.

strengths: SWR is a simple, low-overhead per-step rescaling that preserves proportionality via explicit bias scaling; the rebuttal’s C=1 (logit-preserving) default is a thoughtful refinement to mitigate calibration concerns without extra gradients.

Theoretical analysis (proportionality, boundedness under empirically supported per-step growth, and improved inter-layer balance) is coherent within scope, and the authors commit to validating the bounded-step assumption empirically and to reporting spectral norm trends.

Empirically, SWR shows consistent gains in warm-start and continual settings (Figures 2–4), and in standard training; notably Table 1 reports +4.36% absolute on VGG-16/TinyImageNet (0.3912 → 0.4348), and with LR decay (Table 5) SWR reaches 0.5221 vs. 0.4080 for L2.

The rebuttal lays out concrete revisions: corrected normalization-layer handling and bias scaling, a clear step-by-step algorithm, BN recalibration protocol, broader and fairer baselines (spectral norm, orthogonalization, clipping, AdamW/Path-SGD, and LwF/EWC/SI/ER), increased seeds with confidence intervals, extensive ablations (λ_f vs λ_c, hard vs soft, frequency, C=1 vs unconstrained-C), optimizer ablations, and code release.
weaknesses: Most remedies are promised but not yet integrated: the corrected algorithm/pseudocode for normalization layers and cumulative bias scaling, C=1 implementation and calibration analysis (NLL/ECE), BN inference-time recalibration ablation, broader baselines, matched tuning budgets, increased seeds, and confidence intervals are missing from the current draft.

Theory remains conservative and assumption-dependent (bounded per-step norm growth) and is limited to feed-forward networks with homogeneous activations; residual/attention architectures and interactions with running BN statistics and effective learning rates are not treated in the present manuscript.
Presentation issues persist in the submission as-is (typographical artifacts, ambiguous Algorithm 1 variables and cumulative scaling logic), which impede reproducibility.
reviewers.summary_comment: This work proposes Soft Weight Rescaling (SWR), a per-layer, per-step rescaling that nudges norms toward a convex combination of current and initial values while preserving proportionality via bias scaling. The idea is practical, theoretically supported within its scope (bounded norms and improved inter-layer balance), and yields solid gains in warm-start and continual learning, as well as meaningful improvements on VGG-16/TinyImageNet (e.g., +4.36% absolute; with LR decay, 0.5221 vs. 0.4080 for L2). The rebuttal is strong: it clarifies normalization-layer handling, introduces a logit-preserving C=1 variant, commits to broader baselines and fair tuning, adds BN recalibration, increases seeds with confidence intervals, and provides extensive ablations and code. However, these critical elements are not yet reflected in the paper as submitted; Algorithm 1 remains ambiguous, expanded baselines and statistical rigor are missing, and calibration/BN analyses are not included. Given the incremental novelty and current gaps in exposition and evaluation breadth, I recommend rejection at this stage. If the authors integrate the corrected algorithm with C=1, add the promised baselines with matched tuning budgets (including spectral norm, orthogonalization, clipping, AdamW/Path-SGD, and LwF/EWC/SI/ER), increase seeds and report confidence intervals, include BN recalibration and calibration metrics, and release code, the paper would be substantially strengthened for a future submission.

strengths: Proposes a simple, per-step, per-layer post-update rescaling (SWR) with explicit bias scaling to preserve proportionality, incurring negligible computational overhead.

Provides theoretical analyses within scope (homogeneous activations) including proportionality construction, a boundedness argument under a per-step growth assumption, and an inter-layer balance improvement; the rebuttal further clarifies a logit-preserving (C=1) variant and concrete handling of normalization layers with BN recalibration.
Demonstrates promising empirical gains in warm-start and continual-learning setups across several models and datasets, with particularly notable improvements on VGG-16/TinyImageNet.

Rebuttal commits to substantial enhancements: adding strong baselines (AdamW, spectral norm, Path-SGD, clipping, continual-learning methods), standardized protocols and tuning budgets, expanded ablations (λ_f vs λ_c, hard vs soft scaling, frequency), calibration metrics (ECE/NLL), effective learning rate analyses, overhead reporting, and public code release.
weaknesses: Novelty remains incremental relative to prior weight-rescaling and initialization-distance methods; proportionality for homogeneous networks is established, and the main conceptual advance is a soft, continuous rescaling schedule.

Theoretical guarantees rely on a bounded per-step change assumption and cover a limited architecture class; treatment of normalization layers and non-homogeneous/residual components is only partly addressed and mostly promised for future revision.

Experimental evaluation as submitted lacks several strong contemporary baselines and fully standardized protocols; fairness and generality remain uncertain until the promised additions and wider comparisons are implemented and reported.
Presentation/reproducibility issues in the current draft (formatting artifacts, incomplete references, ambiguous pseudocode) are acknowledged but not yet resolved; code is not yet available.
reviewers.summary_comment: This work introduces Soft Weight Rescaling, a lightweight per-step rescaling that controls weight growth while preserving proportionality, with supportive theory (within homogeneous settings) and encouraging results in warm-start and continual learning. The author response is thoughtful and substantive: it clarifies BN handling and proposes a logit-preserving C=1 variant, details a corrected algorithm, and commits to broadening baselines (including AdamW, spectral norm, Path-SGD, clipping, and established continual-learning methods), standardizing protocols and tuning, increasing seeds, adding calibration and dynamics diagnostics, reporting overhead, and releasing code. These planned changes would materially strengthen the paper. However, the current submission still lacks these expanded comparisons and standardized evaluations, the theoretical scope remains limited, and several presentation/reproducibility issues persist. Given that the rebuttal primarily outlines prospective improvements without new results, I recommend rejection at this stage, with encouragement to resubmit after incorporating the promised experiments, clarifications, and code.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: The paper targets core ICML areas—deep learning optimization/regularization, generalization, and continual learning—by controlling weight growth and improving inter-layer balance.

Contribution fit: A simple training-time algorithm with theoretical support (proportionality, boundedness under O(√t) growth, and improved balance) aligns with ICML interests in optimization and trustworthy ML.

Empirical scope (within current setup): Demonstrated gains in warm-start and continual-learning setups and nontrivial improvements on VGG-16/TinyImageNet indicate practical relevance to the ML community.
Systems/practicality: Negligible computation overhead and per-step applicability fit interest in ML systems and scalable training techniques.
why_it_does_not_fit: Novelty fit: Reviewers judge novelty as incremental relative to prior rescaling and initialization-distance methods (novelty score ~3), which is a poor match for ICML’s high bar for conceptual contribution.

Rigor fit: Theoretical coverage is limited to homogeneous activations and assumes bounded per-step norm changes; there is no treatment of residual/attention architectures that are standard in modern practice.

Evaluation fit: Reviewers flagged missing strong baselines (e.g., spectral norm, clipping, Path-SGD/AdamW, LwF/EWC/SI/ER), limited seeds, coarse hyperparameter sweeps, and absent calibration/BN analyses (evaluation ~3).
Presentation fit: Clarity/reproducibility issues (ambiguous pseudocode, formatting artifacts, incomplete references) were noted (clarity ~3), which is risky for ICML.

Reviewer risk: All reviewers recommend rejection and emphasize that many critical improvements are only promised, not shown; this makes acceptance at ICML particularly risky despite topical alignment.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: NeurIPS welcomes practical deep learning advances, optimization, generalization, and continual learning; SWR sits squarely in these tracks.

Contribution fit: A low-overhead, per-step proportionality-preserving rescaling with simple implementation and supportive (if scoped) theory is the kind of practical method that can attract NeurIPS community interest.
Empirical promise: Reviewers note consistent gains in warm-start/continual settings and meaningful improvements on VGG-16/TinyImageNet (including with LR decay), suggesting real practical value.
Positioning: The rebuttal’s logit-preserving C=1 variant, normalization-layer handling, and plans for broader baselines/ablations map well to NeurIPS expectations for thorough empirical work.
why_it_does_not_fit: Novelty fit: Reviewers assess novelty as moderate/incremental versus existing rescaling/initialization-aware methods, which is often insufficient for NeurIPS unless the empirical breadth is very strong.

Rigor fit: Theory is assumption-heavy and limited to homogeneous networks; no analysis for residuals/attention or deeper optimizer-specific behavior, which NeurIPS often expects for core training methods.
Evaluation fit: As submitted, baselines are incomplete, seeds are limited, tuning budgets are not matched, and calibration/BN effects are not fully audited—reviewers scored evaluation ~3.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: ICLR’s focus on deep learning, optimization, and training dynamics matches the paper’s aim to control weight growth and recover plasticity via a simple training intervention.

Contribution fit: A deep-learning–centric training technique with supportive analysis (proportionality construction, boundedness, inter-layer balance) fits ICLR’s interest in practical methods that improve training behavior.

Empirical relevance: Reported gains in warm-start, continual learning, and standard classification (notably VGG-16/TinyImageNet) suggest applicability to representation learning scenarios that ICLR values.

Community expectations: ICLR often welcomes training heuristics if broadly validated and clearly presented; the promised additions (broader baselines, ablations, BN recalibration, calibration metrics, code) align with ICLR’s reproducibility norms.
why_it_does_not_fit: Novelty fit: Reviewers view the idea as incremental relative to prior rescaling/initialization-distance approaches, which remains borderline for ICLR without stronger breadth.
