Presentation fit: Reviewers cite clarity/reproducibility issues (ambiguous Algorithm 1, formatting artifacts) and missing code at submission time.
Reviewer risk: All reviewers recommend rejection due to gaps not yet integrated; despite better topical alignment with ICLR’s deep-learning emphasis, the current manuscript remains risky.

strengths: The authors provide substantive theoretical clarifications in the rebuttal: invertibility of the σ-regularized conditional map ϕ_{t,x1}(x0)=(1−t+σt)x0+tx1 via det=(1−t+σt)^d (resolving the t=1 degeneracy), Lipschitzness of the discretized velocity vσ^dis as a softmax of affine functions, and a Grönwall-based prototype error bound combined with self-normalized importance sampling (SNIS) consistency (v̂−vσ=Op(N^{-1/2})).

Clear articulation of a comprehensive evaluation plan: adding CIFAR-10 (32×32) and CelebA 64×64 with FID@50k/KID/IS, fair comparisons to Rectified Flow, OT-CFM, and one-step/distillation baselines, and ablations over σ, buffer size N, solver tolerance, top-k/ANN truncation, and reporting effective sample size (ESS), NFEs, wall-clock, and memory.

Methodologically appealing core idea remains: compute prototypes by integrating the explicit FM velocity (Eq. (1)) backward using a buffer-based SIS estimator (Alg. 1/2), then train a direct one-step map vθ(x0)→x1, enabling ODE-free inference; extension to conditional generation and a color transfer pipeline are described with implementation details.

The paper and rebuttal discuss practical stability knobs and scaling strategies (e.g., tol targeted below the injected noise scale set by σ∈[1e−3,1e−2], numerically stable softmax, top-k/ANN neighbor selection, ESS-based buffer refreshing, optional rejection sampling), indicating awareness of high-dimensional variance issues.

The current draft includes concrete hyperparameters and setups that aid reproducibility: MNIST with DiT and l=15000 steps, n=128, N=6n, σ=1e−2, tol=1e−4 (odeint_adjoint), and for color transfer MLP sizes and n=N=128, which helps readers trace the training and prototype computation pipeline.
weaknesses: Despite strong plans, the submission still lacks the expanded quantitative experiments and baseline comparisons; evidence remains limited to 2D toy data (Fig. 1–2), MNIST samples (Fig. 3) without FID/KID/IS, and qualitative color transfer (Fig. 4) without metrics, making significance and competitiveness unclear.

Practical efficiency and amortization are unproven: training requires inverse-time ODE solves with per-step aggregation over buffer elements (N or top-k), yet no current wall-clock analysis demonstrates a break-even versus standard FM/RF or distilled one-step baselines; scalability in higher dimensions remains hypothetical.

Key empirical claims (e.g., near-monotonic couplings, adequacy of prototypes across modalities) remain unsubstantiated without the proposed measurements; robustness of SNIS with finite N and stability of backward integration in higher dimensions are not demonstrated.

Clarity and presentation issues persist in the current manuscript (broken/placeholder references like 'page-9-10', '059', inconsistent notation between ρ0, ρ^0, etc.), and some application choices (pixel-wise color transfer ignoring spatial structure) understate the task complexity; the promised spatially informed variants and metrics are not yet present.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal meaningfully strengthens the paper’s theoretical underpinnings and offers a detailed, credible plan for comprehensive empirical evaluation and efficiency reporting. In particular, the σ-regularized invertibility at t=1, Lipschitzness of vσ^dis, and prototype error bounds grounded in SNIS consistency improve confidence in soundness. The practical roadmap (FID/KID/IS on CIFAR-10/CelebA, fair baselines, NFEs and wall-clock, ESS diagnostics, top-k/ANN truncation) directly addresses prior concerns. However, the current manuscript still lacks these quantitative results and remains supported primarily by 2D, MNIST, and qualitative color transfer demos, with unresolved questions about training overhead and high-dimensional robustness. I encourage resubmission once the planned experiments and comparisons are incorporated and presentation issues are fixed; in its present state, I recommend rejection.

strengths: Conceptually appealing one-step sampling framework: compute target-to-source prototypes via the explicit FM velocity (Eq. (1)) by integrating the inverse-time ODE (Alg. 1/2), then learn a direct map that enables O(1) inference without ODE solves.

The rebuttal provides concrete theoretical clarifications and additions: invertibility of the sigma-regularized conditional map, Lipschitz/well-posedness of the discretized velocity, SNIS consistency and a kernel-regression view, and a Grönwall-type prototype error bound linking estimator error to endpoint error.

Demonstrated flexibility across tasks (2D toy data with sigma and tolerance sweeps in Fig. 1; MNIST with DiT and 15k steps in Fig. 3; color transfer in Fig. 4). The planned evaluation expands to CIFAR-10 and CelebA with FID/KID/IS, plus strong baselines (Rectified Flow, OT-CFM, one-step/distillation).

Practicality-oriented plans for scalability and efficiency: top-k/ANN truncation of the buffer, ESS monitoring, rejection sampling ablations, vectorized/mixed-precision prototype solves, curriculum on buffer size and ODE tolerance, and explicit amortization break-even analysis with wall-clock and NFEs.
weaknesses: In the current submission, empirical evidence remains proof-of-concept: no quantitative image metrics (e.g., FID@50k), no baseline comparisons (RF, OT-CFM, InstaFlow/KD), no ablations (sigma in 1e-2–1e-3, buffer N, solver tolerance 1e-4 vs 1e-6), and no runtime analyses (training overhead, inference throughput), despite the strong plans outlined in the rebuttal.

Key claims are not yet substantiated within the paper: near-monotonic couplings are not quantified, and prototype stability/accuracy under SNIS and backward ODE integration is not measured (e.g., cycle-consistency error, ESS trends) on higher-dimensional datasets.

Presentation and reproducibility gaps in the current draft (formatting artifacts like stray placeholders, broken references to pages/figures, notation inconsistencies such as ρ0 vs ρ^0) and missing code prevent easy verification; details like n=128, N=6n, DiT configuration, odeint_adjoint tolerance 1e-4 for MNIST need to be integrated systematically.

Training-time cost and scalability are not validated empirically: per-batch complexity O(n×S×N) due to backward ODE and buffered importance sampling is only discussed qualitatively; no wall-clock/memory breakdowns or amortization break-even measurements versus FM/RF or distilled one-step baselines are provided.
reviewers.summary_comment: This work proposes learning a one-step generator by computing prototypes via the explicit flow-matching velocity (Eq. (1)) using inverse-time ODEs (Alg. 1/2) and then training a direct mapping, thereby avoiding ODE solves at inference. The approach is promising and the rebuttal substantially strengthens soundness with concrete theoretical elements (invertibility with σ>0, well-posedness, SNIS consistency, and an error bound) and a thorough empirical plan (CIFAR-10/CelebA FID/KID/IS, baselines like Rectified Flow/OT-CFM/InstaFlow, ablations over σ, buffer N, solver tolerance, top-k/ANN, ESS diagnostics, and comprehensive wall-clock analyses). However, these additions are not yet present in the submission. The current paper still lacks quantitative metrics, baseline comparisons, ablations, and runtime evidence; several claims (e.g., near-monotonic pairings) remain unmeasured; and presentation/reproducibility issues persist. If the promised experiments, proofs, diagnostics, and code are integrated and show competitive quality and efficiency, this could become a compelling contribution. As it stands, I recommend rejection, with encouragement to resubmit after incorporating the planned evidence and fixes.

strengths: The paper proposes a novel and practical idea: compute target-conditioned prototypes via the explicit velocity (Eq. (1)) and an inverse-time ODE (Eq. (2)), then train a one-step mapper (Algorithms 1–2), thereby avoiding ODE solves at inference.

The need for nonzero sigma is clearly motivated and illustrated in Fig. 1 (σ≈1e−5–1e−2 with tol≈1e−4 vs 1e−6), and the method naturally extends to conditional settings (Algorithm 2) and applications such as color transfer (Fig. 4).

The rebuttal substantially strengthens the technical underpinnings with planned proofs (invertibility of ϕ_{t,x1}(x0) = (1 − t + σ t) x0 + t x1, Lipschitzness of v_σ^dis), SNIS consistency discussions, and Grönwall-type bounds linking velocity error to prototype error.

The authors provide a concrete plan for scalability and efficiency: top-k/ANN truncation to reduce the O(n×S×N) cost, ESS monitoring and buffer refresh, curricula on t and σ, and a break-even analysis comparing one-step inference to K-step ODE samplers.

Implementation details currently included (e.g., MNIST: DiT backbone, n=128, N=6n, σ=1e−2, tol=1e−4, 15k steps; color transfer: MLP sizes, n=N=128, 500 steps, default odeint settings) enhance reproducibility and indicate feasibility.
weaknesses: Despite a thorough rebuttal plan, the current manuscript still lacks quantitative evidence and fair baselines: no FID/KID/IS or precision/recall on images (beyond MNIST visuals in Fig. 3), no comparisons to CFM/Rectified Flow/OT-CFM/InstaFlow, and no measured speedups or NFEs. The presented experiments remain toy 2D (Fig. 2), MNIST (Fig. 3), and qualitative color transfer (Fig. 4) only.

Key practical aspects remain unsubstantiated in the paper as submitted: how prototype error scales with buffer size N and dimension d, robustness to ODE solver tolerances (e.g., tol≈1e−4–1e−6) in higher-dimensional image settings, and the empirical break-even point versus multi-step ODE samplers. The training-time overhead of solving inverse ODEs per target sample may be substantial.

Clarity and presentation issues persist in the current draft (e.g., broken/placeholder references like '**059**', '**061**', ambiguous 'page-9-10' citations, and occasional notation inconsistencies between ρ0, ρ^0), which impede readability and replication until the promised fixes are incorporated.
reviewers.summary_comment: This submission introduces a promising one-step sampling approach for Flow Matching: use the explicit velocity (Eq. (1)) to compute prototypes via an inverse-time ODE (Eq. (2)) and train a direct mapper (Algorithms 1–2). The idea is appealing, the σ-regularization experiments (Fig. 1 with σ≈1e−5–1e−2 and tol≈1e−4/1e−6) help motivate invertibility, and the rebuttal outlines substantial theoretical clarifications (invertibility, Lipschitzness, SNIS consistency, Grönwall bounds) and a comprehensive empirical plan (CIFAR-10 and CelebA with FID/KID/IS, comparisons to CFM/RF/OT-CFM/InstaFlow, runtime/throughput, and ablations on σ, buffer size N, and solver tolerance). However, these additions are not yet present in the manuscript. At review time, the paper still lacks quantitative metrics, baseline comparisons, and speed analyses, and it retains clarity issues (broken references and notation). I encourage the authors to incorporate the promised experiments, diagnostics (e.g., cycle-consistency error, MMD/energy distance of prototypes to ρ0), and proofs, and to fix the presentation issues. With these in place, the work could make a compelling case; in its current form, it falls short of the bar.

strengths: The rebuttal directly addresses the core flaw by withdrawing the finite/co-finite claim and replacing it with a finite-state (regular-language) upper bound under fixed precision, NoPE, and stable-softmax, which is more plausible and consistent with prior literature.

A concrete, IEEE-754–aware plan is outlined: formalizing numerically stable softmax, correcting the parameter assumption, and introducing a rounding lemma parameterized by the minimum positive representable value to control exact zeroing of non-target weights.

Constructive lower bounds are made more rigorous and implementation-aligned: head-per-symbol constructions for letter-set languages under NoPE and periodic APE for m-cyclic languages, both relying on logit gaps and stable rounding rather than overflow.

The authors commit to empirical sanity checks across fp16/bf16/fp32 and to a clearer positioning relative to encoder/circuit/logic results, which would strengthen practical relevance and novelty if carried through.
weaknesses: The current manuscript remains unsound and inconsistent: the main claims and proofs in Sections 5–6 still depend on flawed arguments; the proposed finite-state upper bound and precision-aware constructions are not yet integrated into the paper, representing major post-rebuttal changes.

The revised lower bounds hinge on exact rounding to zero under stable-softmax via format-dependent gaps; robustness across IEEE-754 formats, subnormal handling, library implementations of exp/softmax, and fused kernels needs careful, fully rigorous treatment and validation, which is not present yet.

Novelty appears moderate: the finite-state transducer viewpoint under fixed precision and bounded resources is conceptually familiar; the paper needs to delineate its contribution beyond existing encoder and circuit/logic bounds and demonstrate what is uniquely decoder-specific.

Assumptions such as constant decode budget, NoPE, and greedy decoding materially narrow the scope; the significance of results for practical systems remains limited without broader settings or empirical corroboration.
Presentation issues identified earlier (typos, malformed tables/equations, ambiguous notation) persist in the current draft and must be systematically fixed to ensure clarity and confidence.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors’ rebuttal is thoughtful and substantially improves the technical direction: it replaces the untenable finite/co-finite claim with a finite-state upper bound under fixed precision and NoPE, formalizes stable-softmax and IEEE-754 rounding, and proposes constructive lower bounds for letter-set and m-cyclic languages that are better aligned with practical implementations. These changes, along with planned empirical checks, would make the work far more sound and relevant. However, the current manuscript still contains the unsound arguments and lacks the revised proofs and validations; the required corrections are extensive and central to the contribution. Additionally, the precision-dependent rounding conditions demand careful, implementation-robust proofs and experiments that are not yet included, and the novelty relative to existing finite-domain and logic/circuit results remains to be clearly articulated. I recommend rejection at this time, with encouragement to incorporate the proposed revisions, provide full formal proofs and empirical validations, and clearly position the contributions before resubmission.

strengths: The rebuttal substantially improves the technical direction: it abandons overflow-based reasoning, adopts numerically stable softmax, and corrects the assumption to exclude non-finite QK^T values, aligning with practical implementations.

Provides a more convincing path to a regular-language upper bound via a refined state definition that includes clipped histograms of key/value patterns and final-position activations, together with a κ-threshold lemma to ensure attention outputs depend only on finitely many summary counts.

Clarifies IEEE-754 portability for the zeroing/rounding strategy with explicit bounds based on min_pos and ulp, and plans to handle both subnormal-preserving and flush-to-zero regimes; proposes cross-backend empirical checks to validate thresholds.

Outlines concrete, constant-precision constructions for letter-set (NoPE) and m-cyclic (periodic APE) languages that avoid universal approximation and length-dependent scaling, making them more plausible under fixed precision.
weaknesses: The key results remain proposed but not proved in the submission: the DFA reduction hinges on nontrivial lemmas (finite pattern sets, κ-threshold rounding, and especially the right-congruence property s(w) = s(w′) ⇒ s(wσ) = s(w′σ)) that are not yet included; without these, the main upper bound is still unsubstantiated.

The clipped-histogram approach requires careful justification that the finite summaries indeed determine all subsequent attention and FFN computations across layers/heads (including nonlinearities and residuals) under fixed precision; this dependence is asserted but not demonstrated, and the precise definition of the state and update δ needs formalization.

The rounding/underflow conditions, while more principled, depend sensitively on IEEE-754 details (subnormals, FTZ, exp implementations) and chosen Δ; robustness across precisions and backends remains to be established empirically and formally.

Given the scope of revisions (replacing the headline theorems, adding new lemmas, formal proofs, and experiments), the current manuscript does not yet meet the standard for completeness and rigor required for acceptance.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors’ second response addresses prior concerns with a much more rigorous plan: redefining the state to clipped histograms to make the δ transition well-defined under causal attention, proposing κ-threshold and negligible-contribution lemmas in IEEE-754 terms, and outlining constant-precision constructions for letter-set and m-cyclic languages without relying on overflow or universal approximation. These directions are promising and better grounded in practical numerics. However, the crucial components are not yet in the paper: the well-definedness of the DFA reduction (i.e., that the chosen state forms a right congruence under appending symbols) and the rounding lemmas remain to be formally proved and integrated; similarly, the portability of the zeroing strategy across precisions/backends must be demonstrated. Given the magnitude of changes required and the absence of complete proofs and validations in the current draft, I recommend rejection at this time, with encouragement to resubmit once the new theoretical results and supporting experiments are fully developed and incorporated.

strengths: The authors withdraw the unsound overflow-based softmax reasoning and adopt a numerically stable softmax within an explicit IEEE-754 fixed-precision model, correcting the infinity-freeness assumption and aligning the setup with practice.

They substantially refine the finite-state (regular) upper-bound argument by defining a concrete, finite state S(w) comprising query-indexed accumulation tables (unnormalized sums Z_lh(q), U_lh(q)) per layer/head and the final-position activation, together with a right-congruence and well-defined transition δ(S, σ). This directly addresses the earlier concern about causal attention recomputing over all past keys/values.

For lower bounds, they replace underflow-to-zero dependence with a portable ULP-Separation Lemma that guarantees exact outputs under round-to-nearest when non-target contributions are below ulp thresholds, and outline precision-aware constructions for letter-set (NoPE) and m-cyclic (APE) languages.

They plan to add empirical validations (across fp32/fp16/bf16 and common backends) and a cleaned, rigorous presentation (numerical model specification, full proofs, corrected notation), which would materially improve the paper.
weaknesses: The key technical pieces remain promises rather than completed results. The finite-state reduction hinges on nontrivial lemmas (finiteness of Q_lh and table ranges, well-defined δ, and the right-congruence proof under causal attention), as well as the Clipped Accumulation Lemma; these are not yet provided in the manuscript. Until these proofs are presented in full, the main claim remains unverified.

The ULP-Separation-based lower bounds depend on precise control of rounding and value norms across IEEE-754 formats and backend behaviors (round-to-nearest-even, subnormals, FTZ/DAZ). Concrete Δ and ulp bounds, and portability across hardware/software stacks, need to be fully specified and proved; currently they are only outlined.

Novelty and scope are moderate after revision. A decoder-specific, finite-state upper bound under fixed precision and NoPE is plausible but may overlap with existing finite-state intuitions; lower bounds cover letter-set and m-cyclic families, which are relatively limited subclasses. Clearer positioning against prior theorems and a broader characterization would strengthen significance.
The current submission still lacks the promised empirical validation and substantial rewriting for clarity. Accepting on the basis of intended revisions is premature.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal makes strong progress: it corrects the numerical model, retracts the finite/co-finite claim, and proposes a rigorous path to a finite-state upper bound via an explicit, query-indexed state that appears sufficient to define a right-congruent transition under causal attention. It also replaces fragile underflow arguments with a portable ULP-Separation approach for precision-aware lower bounds and commits to empirical checks and a substantial rewrite. These changes address the core issues I raised and, if fully executed, could yield a solid contribution. However, the crucial lemmas and complete proofs are not yet in the manuscript, the portability and parameter bounds for the lower bounds remain to be formalized, and empirical evidence is pending. Given the extent of outstanding work and the modest novelty, I recommend rejection at this stage, with encouragement to resubmit a thoroughly revised version with full proofs, clarified assumptions, and supporting experiments.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: The paper studies the expressivity of Transformers under fixed precision and positional encoding choices, connecting to formal languages, logic, and circuit complexity—well within ICML’s “theory of machine learning” and “general machine learning” topics, and close to prior ICML work on fixed-precision expressivity (e.g., encoder bounds).

Contribution type: A theoretical upper bound (now positioned as a finite-state/regular-language upper bound under fixed precision, NoPE, and numerically stable softmax) plus constructive lower bounds (letter-set under NoPE, m-cyclic under APE) aligns with ICML’s appetite for rigorous ML theory with practical implications.

Practical framing: The rebuttal moves to IEEE-754–aware modeling (stable softmax, infinity-free QK^T), κ-threshold/rounding lemmas, and plans empirical checks across fp16/bf16/fp32; this bridges theory and real-world implementations, which ICML values.
why_it_does_not_fit: Rigor and completeness issues (reviewers): The current manuscript still contains unsound arguments and lacks the integrated revised proofs. The proposed finite-state upper bound and IEEE-754 rounding/threshold lemmas are not yet in the paper; key right-congruence and clipped-histogram/state-transition lemmas remain unproved.

Novelty and scope concerns (reviewers): Reviewers cite moderate novelty and overlap with existing encoder/circuit/logic bounds; the decoder-specific angle and what is uniquely new are not yet clearly delimited.

Assumption narrowness and significance (reviewers): Constant decode budget, NoPE, greedy decoding, and other constraints reduce practical impact; without the promised empirical corroboration, significance for deployed systems is limited.
Presentation (reviewers): Typos, malformed tables/equations, and ambiguous notation undermine clarity; ICML expectations for polish are not met.
Reviewer risk: All reviewers recommend rejection pending major revisions and validations, indicating high risk at ICML’s competitive bar.

strengths: The authors provide concrete methodological clarifications, including corrected and explicit equations for the training objective, probability path, forward-Euler inference, and the inpainting update rule, which strengthen the technical soundness.

They detail data processing and conditioning steps (GEE reprojection/resampling, QA mask dilation, strict t′ < t composites, normalization, dt and N_steps), and commit to disclosing baseline configurations, improving transparency and fairness.

They acknowledge limitations of pseudo-ground truth for dynamic phenology and propose stratified evaluations (by time gap and growth stage) and cross-sensor checks, which would make future evaluations more robust.

They plan to include qualitative real cloud/shadow panels in the revision and provide immediately actionable additions (baseline configs, scripts/pseudocode, corrected figures/notation) to partially bridge current gaps.

The core contribution—CFM-based multi-sensor fusion for high-resolution, gap-free imagery—remains timely and relevant, with existing results indicating improvements over STARFM and a diffusion baseline on held-out years.
weaknesses: Key empirical enhancements (real cloud/shadow and SLC-off quantitative evaluations, extended baselines like ESTARFM/HISTARFM and RePaint, σ sensitivity, leakage ablations) remain prospective and are not present in the current submission.

Reliance on pseudo-ground truth (nearest clear scenes) for real-world protocols risks biasing results away from the targeted dynamic agricultural phenology; proposed mitigations are not yet demonstrated in the manuscript.

Clarity issues (garbled equations, unresolved figure references) and fairness/runtime details are acknowledged but not yet reflected in the submitted version, and reproducibility cannot be verified during review as code/data remain unavailable.
Efficiency claims versus diffusion and classical baselines are not substantiated by matched wall-clock/runtime comparisons in the current paper; these measurements are deferred.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal provides substantial clarifications and a thoughtful plan to strengthen the work: explicit and corrected mathematical formulation, precise data processing and composite safeguards, clearer baseline fairness, and plans for broader real-world evaluations and ablations. These responses increase confidence in the approach’s soundness and future reproducibility. However, the current manuscript still lacks the key empirical validations and extended baselines needed to convincingly establish practical effectiveness, particularly for dynamic agricultural settings. Clarity and reproducibility remain incomplete at review time, and efficiency claims are not yet supported by runtime measurements. Judging the submission as it stands, I recommend rejection, with the view that incorporating the promised experiments, analyses, and materials would likely make this a strong resubmission.

strengths: Introduces Conditional Flow Matching for multi-sensor spatio-temporal fusion and gap-filling, providing a stable and efficient alternative to diffusion for generating high-resolution, gap-free imagery.

Well-motivated integration of MODIS temporal information with Landsat spatial detail, including a practical inpainting-style mechanism (Algorithm 3) that preserves known pixels while reconstructing unknown regions.

Solid quantitative trends: accuracy improves markedly with steps (e.g., SSIM from 0.651 at 1 step to 0.912 at 50 steps; PSNR from 23.3 to 31.8; SID from 0.285 to 0.018), and CFM outperforms a diffusion baseline and STARFM (e.g., SSIM 0.912 vs. 0.891/0.852; SID 0.0186 vs. 0.0271/0.0481; PSNR 31.8 vs. 30.0/28.6).
Demonstrates the value of MODIS conditioning for heavy occlusion: at 75% cloud cover, SSIM improves to 0.812 with MODIS vs. 0.723 without, and SID is substantially lower (0.071 vs. 0.167).

The rebuttal provides concrete and comprehensive plans: adding stronger classical and deep baselines (HISTARFM/STAIR and a learning-based fusion model), real-cloud and Landsat-7 SLC-off evaluations with QA error analyses, runtime and hyperparameter ablations (including σ and conditioning strategies), cross-region/sensor tests, and release of code and preprocessing scripts; it also clarifies immediate fixes to equations, figures, and methodological details.
weaknesses: Key evaluation gaps remain in the current submission: comparisons to stronger classical (HISTARFM/STAIR/ESTARFM) and modern deep spatiotemporal fusion/cloud-removal methods are not yet included; the rebuttal commits to them but provides no results.
Gap-filling is still primarily assessed with synthetic masks; quantitative results on real cloudy/shadowed scenes and systematic QA error analyses are not present, limiting practical validity.

Clarity and reproducibility issues persist in the submitted draft (garbled equations, unresolved figure references, incomplete baseline configurations and preprocessing details); fixes are promised but not yet reflected in the manuscript.

No current runtime or memory comparisons and limited ablations on key choices (e.g., probability path noise σ, MODIS dropout, composite strategies); efficiency claims versus diffusion are not fully substantiated beyond step-count accuracy trends.
reviewers.summary_comment: This paper proposes SatFlow, a Conditional Flow Matching framework that fuses MODIS and Landsat to synthesize high-resolution, gap-free reflectance imagery. The approach is technically sound and well-motivated, with encouraging quantitative results: the method improves substantially with more inference steps (e.g., SSIM 0.651→0.912; PSNR 23.3→31.8), outperforms a diffusion baseline and STARFM on held-out scenes (e.g., SSIM 0.912 vs. 0.891/0.852; SID 0.0186 vs. 0.0271/0.0481), and shows clear benefits from MODIS conditioning at high cloud cover (e.g., SSIM 0.812 vs. 0.723 at 75%). The rebuttal is thorough, toning down claims and detailing concrete additions (stronger baselines, real-cloud and SLC-off evaluations, QA robustness, σ/conditioning ablations, runtime reporting, cross-region/sensor tests) and immediate fixes to clarity and reproducibility (corrected equations, full configs, preprocessing scripts). However, these additions are not yet realized in the current manuscript, which still lacks breadth in baselines, real-world gap-filling validation, and essential clarity/runtime analyses. Given conference standards to judge the submission as-is, I maintain a rejection. If the promised experiments and documentation are incorporated with the stated rigor, the work would be a strong candidate for acceptance in a revised submission.

strengths: The rebuttal provides concrete clarifications to fix malformed math and unify notation (explicit linear Gaussian path, u = x1 − x0, dt = 1/Nsteps) and to correct figure references, which should materially improve readability.

Authors commit to transparent baseline configurations and fairness controls for existing experiments (e.g., diffusion setup with same backbone, optimizer, schedule, and 50 sampling steps), and detail MODIS–Landsat preprocessing, composite construction, and normalization.

Core quantitative results in the current manuscript already indicate gains of CFM over representative baselines: Table 2 shows CFM (SID 0.0186, SSIM 0.912, PSNR 31.8) outperforming diffusion (0.0271, 0.891, 30.0) and STARFM (0.0481, 0.852, 28.6).

The efficiency–accuracy ablation (Table 1) is informative, with SSIM improving from 0.651 (1 step) to 0.912 (50 steps) and SID dropping from 0.285 to 0.018, supporting the claim that linearized flows achieve good quality at moderate step counts.

The plan to broaden baselines (ESTARFM, HISTARFM, STAIR, FSDAF/STFusion-like deep models, RePaint-style inpainting, geostatistical SLC-off filling), expand evaluations to real clouds/SLC-off and domain metrics (SAM, NDVI error, temporal continuity), and provide code/weights/GEE scripts would significantly strengthen the work.
weaknesses: Key additions remain prospective at review time: broader baselines, real-world cloud/shadow and SLC-off evaluations, uncertainty estimates, and robustness analyses are not yet included in the manuscript.

Reproducibility is still pending; despite detailed descriptions, code/weights/configs and exact data splits are not available, and important ablations (σ sensitivity, conditioning strategy effects) and matched runtime comparisons (latency, memory, GPU hours) are not yet evidenced.

Robustness to QA mask errors and to temporally interpolated MODIS under abrupt events is only qualitatively discussed; quantitative stratification by QA categories and case studies for burn/snow/flood scenarios are not provided in the current version.

Results are reported without variance across seeds, and fairness metrics such as parameter counts/FLOPs and mean±std performance are not presented, limiting confidence in generality and efficiency claims.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors provide a thorough and constructive rebuttal, with immediate fixes to equations/notation and detailed methodological clarifications, and they appropriately temper claims. The existing results already show meaningful gains over STARFM and a diffusion baseline (e.g., SID/SSIM/PSNR improvements in Table 2) and a clear efficiency–accuracy profile (Table 1). However, the submission still lacks the expanded state-of-the-art baselines, real-world cloud/SLC-off validations, uncertainty estimates, and robustness analyses that would substantiate the broader claims and practical reliability. Reproducibility artifacts and comprehensive runtime comparisons also remain pending. Judging the paper as submitted, I recommend rejection. A revised version that includes the promised baselines, real-scene evaluations with domain metrics, σ/conditioning ablations with quantitative deltas, matched runtime/latency reporting, multiple-seed statistics, and released code/configs would likely meet the bar for acceptance.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: The work sits squarely in application-driven machine learning for sustainability and Earth observation, a stated ICML interest area; it applies a modern generative modeling technique (Conditional Flow Matching) to remote sensing fusion, super-resolution, and inpainting.

Contribution fit: There is an ML-centric pipeline (CFM with a U-Net backbone, conditional inputs, and an inpainting-style update) and quantitative ML metrics (SSIM/PSNR), and the paper includes comparisons to a diffusion baseline, which aligns with ICML’s focus on ML methodology versus classical remote-sensing-only methods.

Novelty/utility: Reviewers acknowledge that CFM-based multi-sensor fusion is timely and relevant, with encouraging gains over STARFM and a diffusion baseline (e.g., SSIM 0.912 vs. 0.891/0.852; PSNR 31.8 vs. 30.0/28.6), suggesting tangible ML benefits.

Rigor intent: The authors provide concrete clarifications to the objective, probability path, and inference and commit to baseline fairness (same backbone, optimizer, schedule) and to releasing configs/scripts, which aligns with ICML’s emphasis on reproducibility.

Scalability relevance: The claim of faster sampling with linearized flows relative to diffusion (step–accuracy trends) connects to ICML’s interest in practical ML systems and efficient generative modeling.
why_it_does_not_fit: Contribution fit gap: ICML typically expects either clear algorithmic novelty or very strong empirical rigor; here the core contribution is an application of CFM with a straightforward linear Gaussian path (u = x1 − x0) and pipeline engineering, rather than a new ML algorithm or theory.

Rigor concerns: Reviewers highlight missing key baselines (HISTARFM/ESTARFM/STAIR, learning-based fusion, RePaint), limited real-world cloud/shadow and SLC-off evaluations, and lack of runtime/latency/memory comparisons—falling short of ICML’s bar for comprehensive empirical validation.

Novelty risk: Reviewers rate novelty as modest (4/10); applying CFM to this domain, while useful, may be seen as incremental at ICML without stronger ablations (e.g., σ sensitivity, conditioning strategies), uncertainty estimates, or broader insights.

Presentation/reproducibility: Clarity issues (garbled equations, unresolved figures) and unavailable code/data at review time were flagged by all reviewers, which tends to hurt chances at ICML where writing quality and reproducibility are heavily weighted.

Reviewer risk: All reviewers recommend reject due to missing experiments and analyses in the current version; thus, even with topical alignment, the likelihood of acceptance at ICML is low without substantial additions.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: NeurIPS explicitly welcomes AI/ML for sustainability and the physical sciences, and interdisciplinary, data-centric work; SatFlow directly targets Earth observation for environmental monitoring using modern generative models.

Contribution fit: A practical ML system that fuses MODIS temporal signals with Landsat spatial detail via CFM and an inpainting-style mechanism is aligned with NeurIPS’ breadth (general ML, deep learning, probabilistic methods, SysML, and data-centric AI).

Empirical signals: The reported improvements over diffusion and STARFM and the step–quality trade-off (e.g., SSIM 0.651→0.912, PSNR 23.3→31.8) demonstrate method behavior, which NeurIPS values for understanding and practice.

Interdisciplinary appeal: The paper’s integration of generative modeling with remote sensing preprocessing (interpolation, QA masking, composites) and plans for cross-sensor tests match NeurIPS’ encouragement of interdisciplinary submissions and applied insights.

Future-proofing: The authors’ planned additions (stronger baselines, real-cloud/SLC-off, runtime reporting, robustness analyses, release of code/scripts) align with NeurIPS’ emphasis on reproducibility and comprehensive evaluation.
why_it_does_not_fit: Rigor gaps as submitted: Reviewers emphasize that critical empirical components (stronger baselines, real-world gap-filling, QA error analyses) are missing; NeurIPS typically requires complete, as-submitted evidence for practical efficacy.

Novelty limits: The work primarily adapts CFM with a linearized path to a new domain; without deeper methodological advances or new theoretical insights, novelty may be judged as incremental at NeurIPS’ main track.

Efficiency claims unsubstantiated: While step count trends are shown, reviewers note absence of matched wall-clock/runtime/memory comparisons versus diffusion and classical baselines, weakening systems/efficiency claims important for NeurIPS practice-focused audiences.
Presentation/reproducibility: Clarity issues (equations/figures) and unavailable artifacts at review time reduce fit given NeurIPS’ high bar for clarity and artifact availability.

Reviewer risk: All three reviewers recommend rejection due to the same deficiencies; despite topical alignment with sustainability and interdisciplinary tracks, acceptance likelihood remains modest without the promised additions.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: ICLR emphasizes deep learning and representation learning; the paper leverages conditional generative modeling (CFM), U-Nets with attention, and training/inference details that are technically relevant.
Empirical behavior: The step–quality profile and comparisons to diffusion speak to learning dynamics and sampling efficiency, which can interest ICLR’s generative modeling community.
Application relevance: Representation learning for remote sensing and image synthesis are within ICLR’s scope, and the problem (gap-free high-resolution imagery) is meaningful.
why_it_does_not_fit: Contribution mismatch: ICLR typically prioritizes new learning principles, architectures, or analyses; this work applies an existing CFM framework with a simple probability path and focuses on an application pipeline, offering limited methodological novelty.

Rigor and analysis: Reviewers highlight missing ablations (σ sensitivity, conditioning strategies), uncertainty estimates, variance across seeds, and theoretical/diagnostic analysis—elements ICLR often expects in generative modeling papers.
Evaluation scope: Lack of stronger baselines and real-world cloud/SLC-off benchmarks in the submitted version weakens the empirical case relative to ICLR’s standards for convincing evidence.
Presentation/reproducibility: Clarity issues and absence of released code/configs at review time further reduce fit for ICLR, which strongly values clean exposition and artifacts.
Reviewer risk: With all reviewers recommending rejection for similar reasons, and given ICLR’s emphasis on methodological novelty and deep insight, the risk of an unfavorable outcome is high.

strengths: Compelling, realistic benchmark: 1,488 real Upwork tasks totaling $1M with triple-verified end-to-end tests and a unified Docker harness; public Diamond split of 502 tasks worth $500,800 improves reproducibility and community access.

Novel managerial evaluation: SWE Manager tasks grounded in real proposal choices with 99% agreement among experienced engineers, complementing IC tasks to assess both technical and management capabilities.

Economic framing: Performance mapped to actual payouts enables concrete cost and productivity analyses; on Diamond, Claude 3.5 Sonnet reaches 26.2% pass@1 on IC SWE and 44.9% on Manager tasks, earning $208K (Table 1, Figure 6), while pass@k, test-time compute, user-tool, and cost-savings analyses (Figures 7–10) demonstrate headroom and practical value.

Thorough methodology and validation: Triple-verification of tests, automated broken/fixed checks, contamination considerations, and a private holdout split; detailed categorical breakdowns (Tables 2–3, A12) show breadth across task types and difficulty.

Rebuttal-driven enhancements: Concrete commitments to add prompt-aligned acceptance-criteria summaries, a SpecPass subset with re-runs for key models, task-level flags for scope expansions, borderline adjudication sampling, SWE Manager top-2 and NDCG metrics with annotations for multiple acceptable proposals, multi-seed confidence intervals and standardized compute budgets, stratified analyses with rank stability (e.g., Kendall’s tau), an optional Diamond-MM multimodal track, and an add-on split from a second repository to improve external validity.
weaknesses: External validity remains constrained until the add-on split is released; current results are still single-repository and single-platform, which may limit generalization despite planned stratified analyses and rank-stability reporting.

Prompt–grader mismatch mitigation (prompt-aligned criteria, SpecPass metric, scope-expansion flags, adjudication protocol) is planned but not yet reflected in the reported main results; current metrics may still embody thread-driven scope expansion beyond initial prompts.

SWE Manager refinements (top-2 and NDCG metrics, lenient scoring when multiple proposals are acceptable) are not yet incorporated into the reported scores; present evaluation relies on a single ground-truth choice.

Multimodal cues common in real issues are not leveraged in the primary benchmark; while an optional Diamond-MM track is planned, current results may understate achievable performance on visually grounded tasks.

Reporting and reproducibility nitpicks: request consistent model naming (e.g., GPT-4o vs GPT-40), explicit cost-savings formula and assumptions in Section 3.6, Docker image hash or versioning for the public image, a redacted example of an end-to-end test structure to illustrate coverage versus prompt alignment, and per-model user-tool usage statistics to quantify tool invocation and parsing success.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors have substantively addressed prior concerns with clear, concrete commitments that strengthen validity, transparency, and fairness without diluting the benchmark’s core contribution. SWE-Lancer offers a timely and ambitious step beyond unit-test-centric code benchmarks by evaluating real freelance tasks with triple-verified end-to-end tests and adding a novel managerial track tied to real proposal choices. The economic mapping is particularly valuable, and the experimental suite is extensive, spanning pass@k, test-time compute, user-tool ablations, and a practical cost analysis. The rebuttal further commits to prompt-aligned acceptance criteria, a conservative SpecPass metric with re-runs, metadata and adjudication for scope expansion, enhanced manager metrics (top-2 and NDCG) with annotations for multiple acceptable proposals, multi-seed confidence intervals and standardized budgets, stratified analyses with rank stability, an optional multimodal track, and a second-repository add-on split. While some improvements are slated for the camera-ready and not yet reflected in reported results, the paper’s contributions are substantial, clearly articulated, and likely to shape future evaluations of economically grounded, full-stack SWE and management capabilities. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: Authors commit to a single, clearly defined primary metric (text-only pass@1 and earnings in an offline Dockerized environment) and standardized reporting (template, fixed compute/tool budgets, single leaderboard), directly addressing comparability concerns.

Concrete expansions to analysis and metadata: stratified results by surface/category/price with rank-order stability (e.g., Kendall’s tau), per-task tags, and published selection criteria plus a timeline for a v1.1 cross-repo add-on split prioritizing infra/devops tasks.

Improved label fidelity and grading alignment: acceptance-criteria summaries visible to models, a SpecPass metric aligned to the prompt, and metadata flags for scope extensions to reduce prompt–grader mismatch.

Enhanced management-task evaluation: annotations for tasks with multiple acceptable proposals, top-1 and top-2 accuracy, NDCG@k-style ranking, and a lenient metric that credits acceptable alternatives while keeping a clearly labeled primary metric.

Stronger statistical and economic reporting: multi-seed pass@1 with confidence intervals (via bootstrapping), pass@k and compute/token budget tables, cost-savings confidence intervals and sensitivity analyses (pricing, latency, fallback policies, attempts), and release of scripts and logs for reproducibility; plus presentation cleanup (e.g., consistent model naming) promised.
weaknesses: Despite solid plans, the current release remains single-repository and single-platform with underrepresented infra/devops; external validity is still constrained until the cross-repo add-on split is delivered.
Manager-task ground truth, even with new lenient and ranking metrics, ultimately reflects original managerial choices; residual subjectivity may persist and should be quantified in the camera-ready.
Optional multimodal/clarification tracks could still fragment comparisons if not consistently reported; strict adherence to the primary metric and the proposed reporting template will be crucial.

Key improvements (SpecPass, acceptance-criteria summaries, multi-seed CIs, leaderboard/reporting template) are commitments; the camera-ready should include these artifacts and provide concrete identifiers (e.g., Docker image tags/hashes) to ensure reproducibility.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal substantively addresses prior concerns with specific, implementable commitments that enhance validity, comparability, and transparency: a clearly defined primary metric and reporting template, stratified analyses with stability checks, acceptance-criteria summaries and a SpecPass metric for prompt–grader alignment, expanded manager-task metrics including lenient and ranking-based evaluations, and stronger statistical/economic reporting with released scripts and logs. While the dataset still centers on a single repository and management-task subjectivity cannot be eliminated entirely, the planned cross-repo add-on split and explicit annotations/metrics mitigate these limitations. Provided the camera-ready includes the promised artifacts and cleanup, the contribution remains novel, rigorous, and impactful. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: Economically grounded benchmark with 1,488 real freelance SWE tasks totaling $1M in payouts, including a public Diamond split worth $500,800; rigorous, triple-verified end-to-end tests and a unified Docker harness make grading realistic and hard to game.

Two complementary tracks (IC SWE and SWE Manager) with 99% agreement against original manager choices; diverse, full-stack tasks across web, iOS, Android, and desktop with a clear price gradient reflecting difficulty (Figures 3–4).

Comprehensive empirical results: Table 1 reports Claude 3.5 Sonnet at 26.2% IC SWE and 44.9% SWE Manager on Diamond, earning $208k; Figure 7 shows o1’s pass@k curve nearly tripling by k=7; Figure 8 highlights gains from higher test-time compute, especially on higher-priced tasks; Figure 10 demonstrates economic savings up to 33.5% at pass@5; Appendix A2 contamination analysis shows limited cutoff-related uplift.

Author rebuttal adds concrete, time-bound improvements: multi-seed confidence intervals and standardized budgets, SpecPass and acceptance-criteria summaries to align prompts and E2E tests, top-2/NDCG and lenient metrics for management tasks, an optional multimodal Diamond-MM track and clarification tool, tailored scaffolds for open-source models, and a second repository add-on split for cross-repo generalization.
weaknesses: Reliance on a single commercial repository/platform limits external validity and underrepresents infra/devops; the promised cross-repo add-on split and stratified analyses are not yet available in the current draft.

Manager-task ground truth and IC E2E grading can penalize valid alternatives due to prompt–grader drift and single-choice labels; while the rebuttal commits to top-2/NDCG, lenient metrics, SpecPass, and adjudication, these quantitative results and criteria annotations are not yet reported (the zip/postcode validation case underscores the need for prompt-visible acceptance-criteria summaries).

Statistical reporting and baseline elicitation remain limited: many single-seed pass@1 results lack confidence intervals; open-source baselines appear underelicited by a generic scaffold without fairness diagnostics (e.g., user-tool usage and parsing rates, matched compute budgets). Minor presentation issues persist (e.g., GPT-4o naming consistency, cost-savings formula clarity, Docker image/version identifiers), though the authors commit to fixes.
reviewers.summary_comment: SWE-Lancer is a timely, impactful benchmark that ties LLM performance to real economic value through 1,400+ freelance SWE tasks, robust E2E grading, and a management-selection track. Results are unsaturated and informative: on Diamond, Claude 3.5 Sonnet achieves 26.2% (IC) and 44.9% (Manager), earning $208k, with clear improvements from additional attempts and test-time compute, and price-based cost-savings analysis showing up to 33.5% savings at pass@5. The primary limitations—single-repo external validity, potential prompt–grader drift and label subjectivity, and limited statistical reporting/open-source elicitation—are substantively addressed in the rebuttal with concrete, near-term commitments (multi-seed CIs, SpecPass and acceptance criteria summaries, alternative/lenient metrics, multimodal and clarification tracks, tailored scaffolds, and a second repository split). While these additions are not yet reflected in the current results, the core contribution is strong, novel, and significant, and the planned improvements should further strengthen transparency and fairness. I recommend acceptance.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: ICML explicitly welcomes evaluation methodology and application-driven ML; SWE-Lancer is a rigorous evaluation benchmark with triple-verified end-to-end tests, a unified Docker harness, and public splits, which reviewers praised as a compelling, realistic benchmark.

Contribution fit: Strong systems/evaluation contribution (pass@k, test-time compute, tool ablations, offline Dockerized setup) aligns with ICML’s machine learning systems and evaluation tracks; reviewers highlighted thorough methodology and validation.

Rigor/presentation fit: Reviewers scored clarity and evaluation highly (mostly 5s) and noted commitments to multi-seed CIs, standardized budgets, acceptance-criteria summaries, SpecPass, and improved manager-task metrics, which strengthen methodological rigor and reporting.
why_it_does_not_fit: Novelty emphasis: ICML often prioritizes methodological or theoretical advances; this work is primarily a dataset/benchmark plus empirical study rather than new learning algorithms or theory (reviewers’ novelty/soundness mostly 4–5, not breakthrough method novelty).

External validity: Current dataset centers on a single repository/platform with underrepresented infra/devops; reviewers flagged generalization limits until the promised cross-repo add-on split is released, which can be a concern for ICML’s broad ML audience.

Timeline of improvements: Several key refinements (SpecPass, acceptance-criteria summaries, top-2/NDCG and lenient manager metrics, multi-seed CIs, strict reporting template) are promised but not yet in the reported main results, creating some reviewer risk at ICML.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: NeurIPS explicitly welcomes data-centric AI, SysML infrastructure, socio-technical aspects, human interaction in AI systems, and decision-making; SWE-Lancer combines a data-centric benchmark with systems infrastructure and a management (decision) track tied to real-world choices and payouts.

Contribution fit: Benchmark + economic analysis is highly aligned with NeurIPS’ interdisciplinary scope; reviewers emphasized the economic framing, extensive ablations (pass@k, test-time compute, user-tool), and standardized offline evaluation—strong for a data/benchmark contribution.

Novelty and rigor fit: Reviewers unanimously recommended acceptance, citing triple-verified E2E tests, a public Diamond split, a private holdout, contamination analysis, and planned additions (SpecPass, acceptance-criteria summaries, top-2/NDCG, multi-seed CIs). This level of evaluation rigor and breadth fits NeurIPS expectations for impactful benchmarks.
why_it_does_not_fit: External validity and subjectivity: Reviewers noted the single-repo focus and potential subjectivity in manager-task ground truth (even with 99% agreement), though planned lenient metrics and annotations mitigate this.

Multimodality gap: Many issues contain videos/screenshots, yet the primary benchmark is text-only; reviewers flagged a forthcoming optional multimodal track, but current results may understate performance on visually grounded tasks.

Reporting details still pending: Minor presentation/reproducibility items (consistent model naming, explicit cost-savings formulas, Docker image/version identifiers, per-model tool-usage stats) were flagged and should be tidied for NeurIPS camera-ready standards.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: ICLR explicitly includes datasets and benchmarks and broad deep-learning applications; SWE-Lancer targets frontier LLMs/agents with a challenging, unsaturated benchmark and robust E2E evaluation—well within ICLR’s scope.

Contribution fit: Strong empirical/analysis focus (pass@k, test-time compute, cost-savings analysis, contamination and category-stratified results) and release of a public split and Docker environment match ICLR’s interest in reproducible, impactful benchmarks and empirical insights about DL systems.

Rigor/presentation fit: Reviewers rated clarity and evaluation highly and noted concrete commitments to SpecPass, acceptance-criteria summaries, alternative manager metrics, and multi-seed CIs—strengthening credibility and comparability for an ICLR audience.
why_it_does_not_fit: Core-method novelty: ICLR frequently rewards representation/optimization/architectural advances; this paper centers on a benchmark and systems evaluation with limited new learning method contributions (reviewers’ novelty/soundness 4–5).

Scope limits: Single-repo focus and underrepresented infra/devops tasks were flagged by reviewers; although a second-repository add-on split is planned, current results may raise external-validity concerns.

Pending additions: Several improvements (SpecPass, acceptance-criteria summaries, multi-seed CIs, docker image tags) are commitments not yet reflected in the main reported numbers, adding some reviewer risk relative to ICLR’s rigor expectations.

strengths: The authors commit to concrete, substantial additions that directly address attribution and bias: adding ReAct-style and self-reflection/vote-and-verify prompting baselines, UCB-imitation for bandit best-arm, heuristic/Knuth-style solvers for Wordle/Mastermind, and a small-scale online RL baseline with compute reporting.

They will strengthen evaluation integrity with stratified re-evaluation using GPT-4o as env/judge, automated leakage checks, an added 200-sample human audit, and public release of full env/judge transcripts; they will also report programmatic-only aggregates with confidence intervals to isolate gains from LLM-simulated artifacts.

Methodological fixes and ablations improve soundness: exclusion of Minesweeper pairs with mismatched first actions from preference tuning (or use of KTO-like unpaired objectives), SFT-only vs DPO-only vs RPO comparisons under matched budgets, and diversity ablations (Min-p vs top-p).

Curriculum robustness will be examined across UCB(CV) vs UCB(mean/variance) and Thompson sampling, with sensitivity to rollouts and sample sizes, replication on multiple groups, and a derivation sketch linking CV to preference separability (Bradley–Terry margin), improving both rationale and empirical support.

They will provide a systematic analysis of transfer conditions via task descriptors and regression, and show that structurally similar groups (e.g., additional code-breaking variants) improve LOO transfer, adding clarity about when zero-shot generalization should be expected.
weaknesses: Many key improvements remain promised for the camera-ready and are not yet reflected in the current draft; until those results are included (with CIs/significance tests), comparative positioning and attribution are only partially demonstrated.

Curriculum gains reported so far are modest; it remains to be shown that the CV-based UCB policy consistently outperforms simpler alternatives across tasks and metrics, and that the estimator remains reliable at small rollout counts.

Residual risk from LLM-simulated environments persists; while mitigations are planned, the paper should quantify post-mitigation error/hacking rates (with 95% CIs), stratified by task group and seed, and emphasize programmatic-task results in the main narrative.
Clarity around Minesweeper pairing should include how many pairs were excluded and, if using a KTO-like objective, a brief comparison to confirm robustness.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors present a thorough and credible plan to address the core concerns: stronger baselines (agentic prompting, algorithmic imitation, and a small online RL comparison), clearer component ablations, reinforced evaluation integrity (deterministic env/judges, leakage checks, human audits, transcript release), and a more principled and robust curriculum study with comparisons and confidence intervals. They also commit to programmatic-only reporting and a systematic analysis of transfer conditions, which should clarify when zero-shot generalization is expected. While many additions are slated for the camera-ready and curriculum gains remain modest, the contribution remains compelling: a scalable, sampling-centric framework that instills multi-turn information-seeking and decision-making behaviors that often transfer across diverse tasks without degrading standard benchmarks. I recommend acceptance, with the expectation that the promised analyses and quantitative reporting (including CIs/significance tests, post-mitigation hack rates, per-group transfer matrix, and explicit counts for Minesweeper exclusions) are incorporated into the final version.

strengths: Clear, scalable framework (PAPRIKA) for multi-turn decision-making using self-generated diverse trajectories and sequential DPO/RPO on action tokens, with solid empirical gains (e.g., bandit best-arm improvement from 42.25% to 62.25%; curriculum +1.4% avg and +3.3% pass@4).

Extensive evaluation across 10 diverse task groups and two base models (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Gemma-3-12B-IT), including pass@k, turns-to-solve, LOO generalization, and ablations (SFT vs RPO), while showing no degradation on standard benchmarks (e.g., MT-Bench, AlpacaEval, GPQA, Math Hard, MMLU-Pro, IFEval).

Author rebuttal strengthens credibility with concrete commitments: programmatic-only breakdowns with 95% CIs, stratified re-evaluations using GPT-4o as environment/judge, automated leakage checks, a human audit of additional successes, and public release of full env/judge transcripts.

Planned stronger baselines and attribution: ReAct/self-reflection prompting baselines, UCB imitation for bandit best-arm, heuristic/Knuth-style references for Wordle/Mastermind, a small online RL pilot, and expanded ablations (SFT-only vs DPO-only; Min-p vs top-p).

Curriculum robustness and rationale to be deepened: comparisons of UCB(CV) vs UCB(mean/variance) and Thompson sampling, sensitivity to rollout/sample sizes with bootstrap CIs, tests with automatic vs randomized clusters, and analysis linking CV to preference separability under sequential DPO.
weaknesses: Residual reliance on LLM-simulated environments/judges can introduce noise and bias; while planned audits and programmatic-only aggregates mitigate this, final conclusions depend on executing these additions and reporting post-mitigation error rates with confidence intervals.

Task suite remains largely synthetic/text-game-like; even with planned MINT/tool-use and retrieval-augmented evaluations, evidence of transfer to real-world or high-stakes tool pipelines is still limited and should be quantified.

Methodological caveat in Minesweeper (first-move dependency vs DPO pairing) requires exclusion of mismatched pairs or pairing-agnostic objectives (e.g., KTO); the camera-ready should report how many pairs were excluded and demonstrate robustness with/without these data.
reviewers.summary_comment: This work proposes PAPRIKA, a practical approach to instill general, in-context RL-like exploration and decision-making in LLMs using synthetic multi-turn tasks and preference-based fine-tuning. The paper shows consistent gains across 10 tasks and two models, improved efficiency (fewer turns), meaningful zero-shot generalization (e.g., bandit best-arm from 42.25% to 62.25% without bandit-specific training), and no degradation on standard benchmarks. The authors’ rebuttal addresses key concerns with concrete commitments: programmatic-only analyses with CIs, stronger GPT-4o-based audits/leakage checks and a human audit, transcript release, tool-use and retrieval evaluations, stronger baselines (ReAct/self-reflection, UCB imitation, heuristic solvers) and a small online RL pilot, plus deeper curriculum robustness and justification. These additions materially strengthen evaluation credibility and attribution. Remaining risks are the residual dependence on LLM-as-environment, the primarily synthetic scope, and the Minesweeper pairing caveat; the camera-ready should include post-mitigation hack rates stratified by task group, programmatic-only effect sizes with CIs, significance tests across seeds, and reporting of excluded Minesweeper pairs. With these in place, the contribution is solid and timely. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: Presents PAPRIKA, a scalable self-generated multi-turn training framework using sequential DPO/RPO on action tokens to instill general information-gathering and decision-making; shows consistent gains across 10 heterogeneous, partially observable task groups and two base models.

Demonstrates cross-task generalization and efficiency improvements, including leave-one-out transfer (e.g., bandit best-arm 42.25% to 62.25% without bandit-specific training) and reduced turns-to-solve; ablations indicate RPO adds value over SFT alone; standard benchmarks show no degradation.

Introduces a lightweight curriculum (coefficient of variation + UCB) that yields modest but consistent improvements (+1.4% average, +3.3% pass@4 on twenty questions) and directly targets the main bottleneck: sample collection.

Strong reproducibility and engineering: open-source code, datasets, and released checkpoints; careful environment design with programmatic verifiers when feasible; transparent discussion of environment hacking with a measured ~1.5% manual hack rate.

Author rebuttal substantially strengthens attribution and robustness with concrete additions: programmatic-only aggregates with confidence intervals; stronger judges (GPT-4o), automated leakage checks, and human audits with hack-rate–adjusted lower-bound reporting; richer baselines (ReAct/self-reflection, UCB imitation, KTO/RRHF, a small online RL pilot); curriculum robustness (UCB variants, Thompson sampling, sensitivity analyses, embedding-based clustering); strict handling of Minesweeper pairing (exclude mismatches, SFT-only/KTO); and external validity checks via tool-use (MINT) and retrieval-augmented multi-turn QA, plus transcript release for community auditing.
weaknesses: Residual evaluation uncertainty remains for LLM-simulated tasks: while audits and stronger judges are promised and a conservative lower-bound will be reported, the current draft’s results still partially rely on potentially noisy labels and susceptibility to environment hacking on open-ended tasks.

Curriculum gains are relatively small and the CV heuristic, though scale-invariant and practical, remains heuristic and depends on small rollout estimates and task clustering; theoretical justification is light (authors plan to add intuition) and robustness evidence is promised rather than shown.

Baselines and broader applicability are still limited in the current draft: many comparisons (ReAct/self-reflection, KTO/RRHF, UCB-imitation, online RL pilot) and external generalization (tool-use, retrieval) are commitments for the camera-ready; negative/minimal transfer for some LOO cases (e.g., Wordle) and limited bandit-regret improvements with more arms highlight remaining gaps.
Scope centers on synthetic/text environments; even with planned tool-use and retrieval evaluations, evidence of transfer to real-world pipelines will remain preliminary.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper introduces a practical and scalable post-training methodology (PAPRIKA) that improves multi-turn information-seeking and decision-making abilities by fine-tuning on diverse self-generated trajectories with sequential DPO/RPO. Empirical gains are broad, spanning 10 task groups and two base models, with notable zero-shot transfer (e.g., bandit best-arm 42.25% to 62.25%) and improved efficiency (fewer turns). A lightweight curriculum based on the coefficient of variation and UCB yields modest but consistent benefits (+1.4% average, +3.3% pass@4 on twenty questions). My main concerns were the reliance on LLM-simulated environments and judges (label noise and hacking), modest curriculum gains and heuristic motivation, limited baselines, a Minesweeper pairing caveat, and external validity beyond toyish text tasks. The rebuttal directly addresses these with concrete commitments: programmatic-only breakdowns with uncertainty, stronger audits and transparency (transcripts), expanded baselines (including KTO/RRHF and an online RL pilot), curriculum robustness and clustering analyses, hack-rate–adjusted lower bounds, Minesweeper pairing fixes, and added tool-use and retrieval evaluations. While these additions are promised rather than shown, the core contribution remains compelling and well-supported, and the planned extensions substantially mitigate key concerns. I recommend acceptance.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Central ML themes (RL/decision-making with LLMs, curriculum learning, preference optimization) align closely with ICML’s core areas (reinforcement learning, optimization, trustworthy/evaluation). Reviewers highlighted the scalable, sampling-centric framework and cross-task generalization as solid ML contributions.

Contribution fit: Method + empirical framework with sequential DPO/RPO on action tokens, a CV-based UCB curriculum, and extensive ablations across 10 task groups and two base models. Reviewers praised the breadth (pass@k, turns-to-solve, LOO transfer), the planned stronger baselines (ReAct/self-reflection, UCB imitation, heuristic solvers), and a small online RL baseline—exactly the type of methodological attribution ICML values.

Novelty fit: While building on DPO/RPO, the sequential trajectory-level preference setup, sampling-centric training, and curriculum using coefficient of variation are positioned as new in combination; reviewers gave novelty/soundness strong marks and welcomed the planned derivation sketch linking CV to preference separability.

Rigor/presentation: Clear problem setup (POMDP framing), reproducibility (open code/data/checkpoints), and reviewers endorsed added rigor for camera-ready (programmatic-only metrics with CIs, stronger judges, human audits, leakage checks). ICML typically rewards this level of methodological care.
Community interest: Application-driven ML and multiturn evaluation benchmarks are explicitly within ICML’s scope; results do not degrade standard benchmarks—important for ICML’s broad ML audience.
why_it_does_not_fit: Synthetic/LLM-simulated environments: Reviewers flagged residual risk from environment hacking/noise; although mitigations are planned, reliance on LLM envs could raise concerns about evaluation validity versus fully programmatic settings ICML often prefers.

Modest curriculum gains to date: The reported +1.4% average/+3.3% pass@4 are incremental; reviewers asked for stronger evidence that CV-based UCB consistently beats simpler policies across tasks and rollout budgets.

Some key analyses are promised: Several robustness checks, stronger baselines, and statistical reporting are slated for camera-ready. ICML panels sometimes penalize papers where a portion of critical evidence is pending.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: NeurIPS welcomes interdisciplinary AI/ML, agents, decision-making, optimization, data-centric AI, and systemization of evaluation. PAPRIKA’s multi-turn agent framing, curriculum over task groups, and analysis of evaluation artifacts match NeurIPS themes.

Contribution fit: Strong empirical scope (10 task groups, two models), leave-one-out generalization, and efficiency (fewer turns) fit NeurIPS’s emphasis on broad benchmarks and reproducible resources. Reviewers highlighted commitments to programmatic-only breakdowns with CIs, stronger judges, human audits, and transcript release—aligned with NeurIPS’s rigor and transparency expectations.

Novelty fit: The sequential preference-tuning on action tokens plus the CV-based task-selection heuristic offer a practical algorithmic package; reviewers scored novelty/soundness well and valued the curriculum rationale to be expanded.

Rigor/presentation: Planned stronger baselines (ReAct/self-reflection, UCB imitation, heuristic solvers) and a pilot online RL comparison improve attribution—an important NeurIPS criterion. Open-source artifacts enhance fit to NeurIPS’s community norms.
why_it_does_not_fit: External validity still limited: Reviewers noted the task suite is largely synthetic/text-game-like. Even with planned tool-use/retrieval evaluations, evidence of transfer to real-world tool pipelines remains preliminary compared to many NeurIPS application tracks.

Evaluation dependencies: Residual reliance on LLM-as-environment/judge introduces potential bias despite planned audits; NeurIPS reviewers often probe label noise and confounding in agentic evaluations.

Some baselines and analyses are pending: Several key additions (e.g., significance tests, curriculum variants, explicit Minesweeper pairing counts) are commitments rather than fully included, which increases acceptance risk at NeurIPS if not delivered convincingly.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: ICLR is strong on deep learning post-training, preference optimization, and RL with transformers. PAPRIKA’s sequential DPO/RPO and in-context RL framing plus curriculum are aligned with ICLR’s focus on representation and optimization for reasoning/acting.

Contribution fit: Broad empirical validation, ablations (SFT vs RPO), and zero-shot transfer across task groups suit ICLR’s empirical standards; reviewers report no degradation on standard DL benchmarks, which ICLR values.

Novelty fit: The action-token-only sequential preference learning and CV-based UCB curriculum offer a practical twist on alignment/post-training methods—consistent with ICLR’s interest in scalable training recipes for reasoning agents.
why_it_does_not_fit: Heavier method than representation/theory: Compared to typical ICLR papers, theoretical novelty is modest (derivation sketch for CV is planned but light), and the contribution leans more toward engineering + evaluation than core representation/learning theory.

Synthetic evaluation and LLM envs: ICLR reviewers often focus on clean, programmatic benchmarks; dependence on LLM-simulated environments and promised audits may be viewed as higher-risk until fully substantiated with CIs and stratified analyses.

Pending revisions: Several critical robustness pieces (KTO or pair exclusion for Minesweeper, stronger baselines, significance across seeds) are promised for camera-ready, which can be risky under ICLR’s timeline and review norms.

strengths: Fairness and compute parity are convincingly clarified: all methods use identical architectures and PPO hyperparameters under a 3B-step cap per condition, with forthcoming main-text and appendix tables detailing step budgets, wall-clock hours, and FCP/E3T population construction; an ablation increasing FCP snapshot density under the same cap is promised.

Mechanism-oriented analyses are planned to substantiate why environment diversity induces partner-robust norms (equilibrium-breaking and implicit meta-learning): conditional mutual information I(agent action; partner action | state), partner-identity probes from recurrent activations, and a stylized coordination analysis on the Dual Destination family.

Human-study rigor will be strengthened with effect sizes, confidence intervals, Holm–Bonferroni and FDR corrections, mixed-effects models, and nonparametric tests, plus data/code release; scope extensions include a third held-out layout and longer episodes to assess stability of reward and preferences.

Environment generator clarity is improved: explicit solvability criteria moved to the main text, difficulty calibration via plan-length/chokepoint/handoff buckets with performance reported by bucket, and a careful statement of the 1.16×10^17 count as an upper bound alongside actual sampled layout counts; train/test distribution diagnostics are promised.

Empirical results remain compelling across settings: CEC achieves near-oracle performance in the toy task (e.g., normalized reward ≈0.931 vs. oracle 0.955 in Fig. 3) and strong XP on both the five canonical (Fig. 6) and 100 procedurally generated layouts, while human studies show higher subjective cooperation and lower collision rates (e.g., Fig. 11) even without training on test layouts.
weaknesses: Several critical additions (compute/wall-clock tables, statistical corrections, new diagnostics, difficulty calibration) are commitments for the camera-ready and are not yet reflected in the manuscript; acceptance is contingent on these being delivered as stated.

Human-study external validity remains limited: evaluations focus on Overcooked with primarily English-speaking Prolific users and a small number of layouts; broader generalization across tasks and more diverse populations is still untested despite partial mitigation.

Conceptual novelty is moderate—CEC leverages domain randomization with standard IPPO and recurrence—and formal theory remains limited; further sample-efficiency ablations and clearer diagnostics explaining baseline failures on procedural tests would strengthen the contribution.

Combining partner and environment diversity (e.g., CEC-E3T) currently underperforms and appears sensitive to training noise and capacity; more systematic exploration of architectures, curricula, and tuning is needed.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors’ rebuttal substantially addresses prior concerns about fairness and clarity by confirming equal step budgets and identical architectures, promising explicit compute/wall-clock accounting and a PBT snapshot ablation, and outlining concrete mechanism-level diagnostics (CMI, partner-identity probes, stylized analyses). Human-study rigor will be improved with comprehensive statistical reporting, longer episodes, and an extra held-out layout, alongside data/code release. Generator details and difficulty calibration will be made explicit, and the large layout-count claim will be reframed as an upper bound with actual sampling counts reported. While the core idea remains conceptually simple and some additions are pending, the current empirical evidence—AI–AI ZSC, procedural generalization, cross-algorithm analyses, and human studies—combined with the planned clarifications and analyses, is compelling. I recommend acceptance, conditioned on inclusion of the promised additions in the camera-ready.

strengths: The authors provide strong fairness and compute-parity clarifications (3B environment steps per condition, shared PPO hyperparameters and architectures) and will add explicit main-text/appendix tables with snapshot counts, step splits for FCP, unique layout counts, per-layout exposure, and wall-clock/FLOP estimates.

They address the combined partner+environment baseline with concrete plans: partner-randomness schedules, CEC warm-start curricula, capacity-adjusted variants at approximately constant FLOPs, gradient-variance diagnostics, and a clearly labeled exploratory longer-training check.

Human-study rigor and ecological validity are bolstered via an added held-out procedural layout, longer interactions for a subset, preregistered analyses, effect sizes with CIs, multiple-comparisons control, nonparametric and ordinal mixed-effects models, order-effect controls, per-round trajectory analyses, and public release of data and code.

Exposure normalization and efficiency analyses will be added (unique layouts encountered, per-layout exposure, cross-play per steps per unique layout, AUC normalization), alongside controlled exposure ablations (CEC-K for K ∈ {1,5,20}, and a multi-layout PBT baseline across 5 layouts under the same step cap) and coverage–performance trade-off plots.

Generator transparency is strengthened with explicit solvability constraints in the main text, train/test distribution diagnostics (joint-plan lengths, handoff counts, object distances, chokepoints, rotations), and reframing the 1.16×10^17 figure as an upper bound with an appendix derivation and actual sampled counts; additional mechanism-level diagnostics (conditional mutual information and partner-identity probes) further substantiate partner responsiveness.
weaknesses: The CEC–E3T combination remains underexplored; while planned sweeps and curricula are promising, conclusions about the best way to integrate partner and environment diversity still depend on future results.
