Even with expanded human-study reporting and a small replication, ecological validity remains relatively narrow (few layouts, a crowdsourced population, a single domain); broader cross-domain validation and longer-term interactions are out of scope.

Efficiency comparisons remain confounded by differing exposure profiles across methods; although the authors will add normalization and CEC-K/PBT-multi-layout ablations, the main paper’s current claims would benefit from the execution and inclusion of these analyses.
reviewers.summary_comment: The additional response convincingly addresses the remaining concerns with detailed commitments that enhance fairness, statistical rigor, transparency, and mechanistic insight. Specifically, the authors will (i) document compute parity and population accounting, (ii) conduct sensitivity sweeps and curricula for the CEC–E3T combination with capacity-controlled variants and stability diagnostics, (iii) expand and preregister human-study analyses with effect sizes, multiple-comparisons control, and longer interactions, (iv) normalize for per-layout exposure with exposure-adjusted efficiency metrics and add controlled exposure ablations (CEC-K and multi-layout PBT), and (v) provide clear generator constraints and distribution diagnostics, while reframing the extreme layout-count as an upper bound. These additions substantially strengthen the paper’s claims that environment diversity fosters general cooperative norms that transfer across partners and tasks. Some open questions remain—particularly around the optimal integration of partner and environment diversity and broader ecological validity—but they do not undermine the core contribution. With the promised additions, the work is impactful, thorough, and clearly communicated. I maintain an accept recommendation.

strengths: Compelling core contribution: cross-environment self-play (CEC) yields robust zero-shot coordination with novel partners and novel tasks, supported by concrete results (e.g., toy Dual Destination normalized XP ≈0.931 vs. oracle ≈0.955; strong XP on 5 held-out layouts and 100 procedurally generated layouts in Figure 6).

Substantial engineering: a high-throughput JAX pipeline (~10M steps/min) and a procedural Overcooked generator with solvability checks enable large-scale, reproducible experiments (3B steps/agent), with detailed architectures/hyperparameters (App. A.7) and code release.

Broad and multi-perspective evaluation: toy domains (fully/partially observable and multi-task), Overcooked with 100 held-out layouts, cross-algorithm XP matrices and empirical game-theoretic analysis (Figure 8), and human studies (80 participants) showing better subjective cooperation and fewer collisions (Figure 11) despite no training on the evaluation layouts.

Insight into learned norms and adaptation: qualitative state-coverage analyses (App. A.10) and reduced collision rates align with users’ subjective ratings (Figure 9), and ablations (e.g., recurrence necessity in App. A.5) clarify why environment diversity fosters general cooperative behaviors.

Rebuttal significantly strengthens rigor and fairness: commitments to add Other-Play baselines (toy and Overcooked subset) and a PSRO-lite comparison at parity; sensitivity sweeps for FCP/E3T; rotation/reflection invariance and difficulty-conditioned diagnostics; partner-responsiveness metrics; compute–performance curves at 0.3B/1B/3B steps with wall-clock/FLOP accounting; exposure-normalized efficiency (CEC-K variants, multi-layout PBT baseline); and expanded human-study coverage and statistical treatment (effect sizes/CIs, multiple-comparisons control, mixed-effects, preregistration, broader demographics).
weaknesses: At submission time, some evaluation gaps remain: Other-Play and PSRO-style baselines, diagnostic analyses for FCP/E3T zero-reward failures, and sample-efficiency curves are promised but not yet incorporated; conclusions would be stronger with these realized.

Human evaluations, while valuable, were limited to two layouts, short episodes, and an English-speaking pool; although the authors plan to add a third held-out layout, longer interactions, stronger statistical controls, and broader demographics, these improvements are not yet reflected in the current results.

The approach relies on substantial interaction budgets (3B steps), and although wall-clock feasibility is clarified, results under tighter compute and in additional cooperative domains beyond Overcooked are still outstanding.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper makes a strong, timely case that environment diversity during self-play can induce general cooperative norms that transfer to new partners and tasks. Results span a toy domain (CEC ≈0.931 normalized XP vs. ≈0.955 oracle), large-scale Overcooked evaluations (Figure 6), cross-algorithm empirical game-theoretic analysis (Figure 8), and human studies showing superior subjective cooperation and fewer collisions (Figures 9 and 11). The authors’ rebuttal addresses key concerns with concrete commitments: adding Other-Play and PSRO-lite baselines under matched budgets, performing deeper diagnostics on baseline failures and partner-responsiveness, reporting compute–performance trade-offs and exposure-normalized efficiency, and strengthening and broadening human evaluations with rigorous statistical analysis and preregistration. While these additions are not yet integrated, the current evidence is robust and the planned expansions further increase confidence in the central claim. Overall, the work is significant, well executed, and likely to influence future approaches to human-compatible cooperative AI. I recommend acceptance.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Core ML/RL focus with multi-agent coordination and generalization across tasks/partners aligns with ICML areas (reinforcement learning, general ML, trustworthy ML, application-driven ML).

Contribution fit: Strong empirical ML contribution with broad evaluations (toy ZSC, 5 canonical and 100 procedural Overcooked layouts, cross-algorithm EGTA, human studies) and reproducible infrastructure (JAX pipeline ~10M steps/min, solvable generator) matches ICML’s emphasis on rigorous ML experimentation.

Rigor fit: Reviewers highlight compute parity (3B steps per condition, identical PPO architectures/hyperparameters), planned ablations, and statistical rigor upgrades (effect sizes, CIs, multiple-comparisons control, mixed-effects) as strengths.
Presentation fit: Clarity scores are high; reviewers note improved generator transparency, difficulty calibration, and forthcoming tables/diagnostics, making framing suitable for ICML.

Novelty level is acceptable at ICML for a well-validated training paradigm (environment diversity/self-play) with mechanism-oriented analyses (conditional mutual information, partner-identity probes) and human evidence.

Reviewer sentiment: All three reviewers recommend Accept, citing compelling empirical results (near-oracle toy XP ≈0.931 vs. 0.955; strong XP on held-out layouts; better subjective cooperation and fewer collisions with humans). This lowers risk for ICML.
why_it_does_not_fit: Novelty concerns: Reviewers note the conceptual novelty is moderate (domain randomization with standard IPPO + recurrence), with limited formal theory—some ICML tracks may favor deeper algorithmic/theoretical advances.
Pending additions: Several promised analyses/tables (compute/wall-clock, snapshot density ablation, exposure normalization) are camera-ready commitments, introducing risk if not delivered.
External validity: Human studies are in Overcooked with primarily English-speaking crowd workers and few layouts; reviewers flag limited ecological breadth.
Integration gap: Combining partner and environment diversity (CEC–E3T) underperforms and is underexplored, which may invite questions on completeness of the study.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Squarely within NeurIPS areas—decision-making/RL/control, human interaction in AI systems, socio-technical aspects, optimization, generalization, and SysML infrastructure.
Interdisciplinary appeal: Empirical game-theoretic analysis, human-AI studies, and reproducible systems tooling resonate with NeurIPS’ multi-track and interdisciplinary focus.

Contribution fit: Strong systems + empirical paper with broad benchmarks and human evaluations; NeurIPS often welcomes new evaluation paradigms and insights into behavior/limitations of existing methods.
Rigor and clarity: Reviewers commend compute parity, exposure accounting plans, diagnostics (CMI, partner probes), and release of code/data—good alignment with NeurIPS reproducibility expectations.
Results strength: Robust XP gains over PBT/SP on held-out tasks and better human-rated cooperation/low collision rates support impactful claims suitable for NeurIPS.
why_it_does_not_fit: Novelty: Reviewers rate novelty as moderate; NeurIPS can be sensitive to conceptual novelty, and the method largely reframes training distribution rather than proposing a new learning algorithm.
Scope limits: Evaluations center on Overcooked; external validity to other domains and longer-term interactions is limited despite plans—NeurIPS reviewers may push for broader scope.
Open gaps: The partner+environment combination underperforms and needs more systematic exploration (curricula, capacity control), which could draw scrutiny.
Pending additions: Several promised analyses (exposure-normalized efficiency, multi-layout PBT under same cap, additional baselines like Other-Play/PSRO-lite) raise risk if not fully incorporated.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Within ICLR’s RL/representation learning scope (generalization, meta-learning via recurrence, probabilistic/optimization aspects), plus datasets/benchmarks and societal considerations (human-AI cooperation).

Empirical insight: Diagnostics into learned norms (collision avoidance, state coverage), conditional mutual information, and partner-identity probes provide representation-level insights that ICLR values.
Reproducibility: Clear architectures/hyperparameters and code release align with ICLR’s reproducibility and openness standards.
why_it_does_not_fit: Contribution style: Emphasis is on a training paradigm and large-scale empirical evaluation; limited algorithmic/architectural innovation or formal theory may be a weaker match for ICLR’s core deep learning/representation novelty expectations.

Compute-heavy systems focus: Significant engineering and large budgets (3B steps) drive results; ICLR often favors conceptual or representational advances over primarily systems/benchmarking papers unless paired with strong new learning ideas.
External validity: Human studies and domains are narrow (Overcooked, few layouts); ICLR reviewers may expect broader modality coverage or deeper representational analysis.
Pending camera-ready additions and underexplored partner+environment integration create risk given ICLR’s stringent bar on completeness at submission time.

strengths: Well-formulated unsupervised in-context denoising task that concretely extends the attention–associative memory connection beyond exact retrieval, with Bayes-optimal baselines for linear subspaces, spheres, and Gaussian mixtures (Propositions 2–4) and a precise one-step DAM mapping (Eqs. 16–17).

Clear theory showing that a single-layer, single-head attention layer attains Bayes-optimal denoisers under fixed-norm/bounded supports (Theorem 3.1; Eq. 13), along with finite-sample concentration depending on intrinsic dimension d rather than ambient n (Appendix E; Propositions 5–6).

Empirical evidence aligns with theory in controlled settings: trained models learn near scaled-identity weights and approach Bayes-optimal MSE across cases (e.g., n=16, d=8, L=500), including linear subspaces (sigma0^2=2, sigmaZ^2=1), spheres (R=1, sigmaZ^2=0.1), and Gaussian mixtures (p=8, sigma0^2=0.02, sigmaZ^2=0.1); loss landscapes (Fig. 6) elucidate the alpha–beta tradeoff and explain why softmax can emulate linear attention.

Robustness to coordinate changes is addressed via structured optimal weights under invertible prompt transformations (Appendix H; Eq. A.12), and the link to kernelized attention (Eq. 14) clarifies the role of Gaussian-kernel variants.

The rebuttal substantively addresses prior concerns with concrete additions: higher n/d/L experiments and CIs, broader baselines (PCA/shrinkage, ridge, k-NN/RBF, denoising autoencoder), architectural ablations (multi-head, low-rank), feature-embedding benchmarks, extensions to anisotropic supports via preconditioning, non-Gaussian radial noise, and a systematic finite-variance mixture study including Gaussian-kernel attention.
weaknesses: As it stands, experiments remain in synthetic regimes with modest scales (e.g., n=16, L=500) and limited baselines; the broader validations and ablations are promised for the camera-ready but not yet included.

Core results hinge on restrictive conditions (spherical/bounded supports, Gaussian corruption, small-variance mixtures); while the authors outline preconditioning for anisotropy and radial non-Gaussian extensions, these relaxations are not fully demonstrated in the current draft.

In the mixture case, the finite-variance gap remains underexplored: learned temperatures (e.g., beta substantially below 1/sigmaZ^2) and the role of the residual shrinkage term beyond the sigma0 -> 0 limit would benefit from the proposed sweeps and comparisons to the exact posterior mean.
reviewers.summary_comment: This paper offers a clean and insightful account of in-context denoising that tightens the conceptual and mechanistic link between attention and dense associative memory. Bayes-optimal estimators are derived for three task families (Propositions 2–4), and a one-layer transformer is shown to approximate or attain these solutions, with trained attention corresponding to a single gradient descent step on an appropriate energy (Eqs. 16–17). The empirical results in controlled settings (e.g., n=16, d=8, L=500; sigmaZ^2 in {0.1, 1}; sigma0^2 in {0.02, 2}) are consistent with the theory and are complemented by finite-sample analyses (Appendix E) and structured-weight transformations (Appendix H). The rebuttal constructively addresses scope and robustness by committing to higher-dimensional experiments, stronger baselines, non-Gaussian and anisotropic settings, and a careful finite-variance analysis for mixtures (including Gaussian-kernel attention). While these additions are not yet realized and the current empirical scope remains synthetic, the theoretical development and mechanistic insights are solid and significant. I recommend acceptance, with the expectation that the promised empirical extensions and clarifications be incorporated in the camera-ready.

strengths: The rebuttal substantively addresses scope and robustness: concrete additions include feature-embedding benchmarks (e.g., whitened natural image patches, frozen encoder embeddings with RMSNorm-like preprocessing), higher n/d and longer L with more seeds and confidence intervals, multi-head and low-rank (n_attn < n) ablations, and expanded baselines (PCA/shrinkage, ridge, k-NN/RBF, denoising autoencoder) plus an iterative Hopfield baseline.

Theory is clarified and extended: preconditioning for affine images of spheres is tied to Appendix H (Eq. A.12) and the generalized linear shrinkage ΣX(ΣX+ΣZ)^{-1}x̃; the authors also propose handling spherically symmetric, log-concave corruptions by reparameterizing β (cf. Eq. 14), with planned Laplace/sub-Gaussian experiments, and sweeping σ0, component count, separation, and imbalance in mixtures with comparisons to Proposition 4 and a Gaussian-kernel attention variant.

The gradient-flow mapping concerns are addressed with a plan to quantify the conservativity condition from Appendix I.2: measuring the Jacobian asymmetry ε_cons = ||J − J^T||_F / ||J||_F (Eq. A.18), correlating it with one-step MSE and iterative stability (Eq. 17), and testing mild regularization toward the symmetric set (Eq. A.19).

Core contributions remain strong: Bayes-optimal denoisers for subspaces, spheres, and mixtures (Propositions 2–4); a one-layer attention construction that asymptotically matches these solutions (Theorem 3.1 and Eq. 13); interpretability via scaled-identity or structured weights (Appendix H) and loss-landscape analyses (Fig. 6) that explain α–β tradeoffs and the softmax–linear connection via small-argument expansion.

The authors emphasize intrinsic-dimension dependence with concentration bounds (Appendix E, Propositions 5–6) and commit to scaling experiments (e.g., n up to 256–512, d up to 128, L into the thousands), which directly tests the trends evident in Fig. 4(a) beyond the initial n=16, d=8, L=500 demonstrations.
weaknesses: Many enhancements remain prospective. The current paper’s empirical validation is still synthetic and single-head, full-rank, with earlier setups like n=16, d=8, L=500 and σZ^2 in {0.1, 1}; real-data feature embeddings, multi-head/low-rank ablations, and broader baselines are not yet included.

Generalization beyond LLN-friendly settings is still partially theoretical. While the proposed preconditioning and radial-noise extensions are promising, evidence for strongly anisotropic or non-symmetric corruptions and finite-variance mixtures (beyond σ0 → 0) is pending; it will be important to quantify the gap between Eq. 8 and the exact posterior (Proposition 4) across σ0 and separations.

The softmax-as-gradient-flow interpretation relies on the conservativity constraint (Appendix I.2). It remains to be shown empirically how often trained weights satisfy or approximate Eq. A.19 in practice and how sensitive iterative behavior is when ε_cons is non-negligible.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors’ rebuttal is thorough and directly responsive. They commit to broadening the empirical scope with realistic feature embeddings, higher-dimensional regimes (scaling n and d), longer contexts L, more seeds with confidence intervals, multi-head and low-rank ablations, and a richer set of baselines, including an iterative Hopfield baseline to quantify the one-step vs multi-step tradeoff. On the theory side, they clarify that affine-spherical supports can be handled via preconditioning (consistent with Appendix H, Eq. A.12) and propose extending beyond Gaussian corruption to radial log-concave noise by reparameterizing β (Eq. 14), with supporting experiments. They also address the conservativity of the softmax update (Appendix I.2) by proposing to measure the Jacobian asymmetry ε_cons and relate it to both one-step performance and iterative stability (Eq. 17). These additions meaningfully strengthen the paper’s external validity and mechanistic claims while preserving its core contributions: deriving Bayes-optimal denoisers for structured families, constructing one-layer attention solutions that match them asymptotically (Theorem 3.1), and mapping trained attention to a single gradient step on a DAM energy (Eqs. 16–17). Although current experiments remain synthetic and some generalizations are pending validation, the overall package is compelling and well presented. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: A clear in-context denoising framework that sharpens the mechanistic link between attention and dense associative memory, with Bayes-optimal baselines derived for subspaces, spheres, and mixtures.

Solid theoretical underpinnings (posterior mean derivations, Theorem 3.1, concentration bounds) and a precise mapping of trained attention to a single gradient step on a context-aware DAM energy landscape.

Empirical demonstrations align with theory (scaled-identity weights, convergence with context length, structured weights under invertible transformations), and the rebuttal commits to substantial extensions: higher n/d/L with confidence intervals, multi-head and low-rank ablations, and feature-embedding benchmarks.

Thoughtful generalizations and diagnostics in the rebuttal: anisotropy via preconditioning (affine images of spheres), robustness to radial non-Gaussian noise, Gaussian-kernel attention for finite-variance mixtures, and classical baselines (PCA/shrinkage, ridge, k-NN/RBF, small DAEs).

A comprehensive plan to quantify iteration vs one-step behavior (varying steps, step size, momentum, early stopping, tied-weight multi-layer variants) and to relate stability to a conservative-flow criterion through Jacobian symmetry diagnostics.
weaknesses: In the current submission, validation remains limited to synthetic, single-layer/single-head settings; promised real or semi-real embedding tests and multi-head/low-rank ablations are not yet included.

Key optimality arguments rely on asymptotic context length and structural assumptions (fixed-norm supports, isotropy); while concentration bounds are provided, empirical finite-sample robustness and misspecification tests are only partially demonstrated.

For Gaussian mixtures, deviations in learned scaling relative to the ideal remain to be fully characterized across σ0, K, separation, and imbalance; kernelized attention comparisons are proposed but absent.

The insight regarding degradation from multiple denoising steps is not yet comprehensively quantified across tasks and noise regimes; the planned systematic study would materially strengthen this point.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper presents a well-founded and insightful account of how a one-layer attention head can implement Bayes-optimal in-context denoising for specific task families, and it makes the attention–associative memory connection concrete by interpreting attention as a single gradient step on a context-induced energy. The rebuttal is strong: it directly addresses concerns with concrete additions (scaling to higher dimensions and longer contexts with confidence intervals, multi-head/low-rank ablations, feature-embedding benchmarks, anisotropic/preconditioned theory, robustness to non-Gaussian corruptions, kernelized attention for mixtures, and a systematic study of iterative updates with diagnostics). While these enhancements are not yet in the submission and the current evaluation remains confined to controlled synthetic settings with asymptotic assumptions, the core contributions are clear, sound, and meaningful. I recommend acceptance and encourage inclusion of the promised experiments and theoretical clarifications in the final version to bolster robustness and external validity.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: The paper’s focus on in-context learning with one-layer transformers, Bayes-optimal denoising on structured distributions, and a mechanistic link between attention and dense associative memory (Hopfield) sits squarely within ICML’s core areas (general ML, deep learning, probabilistic methods, optimization, and theory).

Contribution fit: Strong theoretical development (posterior-mean derivations; Theorem 3.1; finite-sample concentration depending on intrinsic dimension d) plus controlled empirical validation matches ICML’s valuation of principled ML theory supported by focused experiments.

Novelty/rigor fit (per reviewers): All reviewers recommend accept and highlight solid theoretical underpinnings, a precise one-step DAM mapping (Eqs. 16–17), and consistent empirical alignment—indicating the novelty and rigor levels are appropriate for ICML.

Presentation fit: Reviewers rate clarity highly and praise the clean framing of in-context denoising and the attention–associative memory connection, which aligns with ICML’s expectations for well-structured methodological papers.

Strategic fit: ICML historically welcomes mechanistic analyses of transformers and associative memory connections; the paper’s PMLR-style exposition and theory+experiments balance are well aligned with the venue.
why_it_does_not_fit: Scope risk from reviewers: Experiments are still mainly synthetic and modest-scale (e.g., n=16, L=500) with many promised empirical extensions (higher n/d/L, real embeddings, broader baselines) not yet incorporated—this could be a concern for some ICML reviewers.

Assumption restrictiveness: The current results hinge on fixed-norm/bounded supports, Gaussian corruption, and small-variance mixtures; while preconditioning and radial non-Gaussian extensions are discussed, they are not fully demonstrated in the current draft.

Mixture-case gap and dynamics: Reviewers note finite-variance mixture behavior (e.g., learned β below 1/σZ²) and the gradient-flow conservativity diagnostics (Appendix I.2) are not fully explored; ICML reviewers may desire those quantitative analyses in-paper rather than promised.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: The work bridges deep learning, theory, and neuroscience-inspired associative memory—areas NeurIPS actively solicits (deep learning, probabilistic methods, theory, neuroscience/cognitive science, and general ML).

Contribution fit: An in-depth mechanistic analysis of an existing ML component (attention) and its behavior in a new task (in-context denoising) aligns with NeurIPS’s welcome of analyses that yield new insights into limitations/behavior of methods.

Novelty/rigor (per reviewers): Reviewers see substantive theoretical contributions (Bayes-optimal baselines, Theorem 3.1) and a clear mapping to energy-based retrieval, with empirical results consistent with theory—indicating the novelty and rigor are competitive for NeurIPS.
why_it_does_not_fit: Empirical breadth risk: NeurIPS often emphasizes broader empirical validation and practical scope; reviewers note current results are synthetic, single-head, and modest-scale with many extensions only planned (real-data embeddings, multi-head/low-rank, stronger baselines).

Assumption sensitivity: The restrictive conditions (spherical/bounded supports, Gaussian corruption, small-variance mixtures) and the not-yet-demonstrated extensions to anisotropy and non-Gaussian noise may dampen perceived generality for the NeurIPS audience.

Iterative behavior and conservativity: The softmax-as-gradient-flow interpretation depends on a conservativity condition (Eq. A.19); reviewers flag the lack of empirical quantification of Jacobian asymmetry and multi-step stability in the current draft—often a sticking point for NeurIPS standards.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: The paper analyzes transformers and in-context learning—central to ICLR—connecting self-attention to a single gradient-descent step on a learned energy, fitting ICLR’s focus on deep learning mechanisms and representation learning.

Contribution fit: Theory-driven mechanistic insight with concrete architectures (one-layer attention) and empirical demonstrations is a familiar ICLR pattern (e.g., prior works on attention/Hopfield links and in-context learning-as-optimization).

Novelty/rigor/presentation (per reviewers): Positive accept recommendations emphasize clear theory, Bayes-optimal baselines, and consistent experiments with good clarity—appropriate for ICLR’s standards for analytical deep learning work.
why_it_does_not_fit: Empirical limitations: Similar to NeurIPS, ICLR often requests demonstrations beyond toy/synthetic settings; reviewers note current experiments are modest-scale and many key ablations (multi-head, low-rank) and real-embedding tests are promised rather than included.

Assumption reliance: Optimality is established under fixed-norm/bounded supports and Gaussian noise; while preconditioning and radial-noise extensions are proposed, ICLR reviewers may want evidence within the submission.

Incomplete mixture analysis and iterative dynamics: The finite-variance Gaussian mixture gap and the systematic study of multi-step updates versus one-step (including conservativity diagnostics) are not yet fully quantified, posing acceptance risk if expectations for completeness are high.

strengths: The authors now clearly articulate a split-conformal protocol with the transport map and base predictor trained only on the Train split, which resolves exchangeability concerns and aligns the practical pipeline with the stated guarantees.

Positioning is improved: the contribution is reframed as a principled operationalization of center-outward (OT-based) ranks for scalarizing multivariate scores with distribution-free calibration under approximate maps.

A substantially strengthened evaluation plan is presented, including adding CD/HPD-split and copula-based baselines on the same MQF² backbone, surfacing coverage tables in the main text, and reporting two OT-CP variants to separate scalarization effects from backbone differences.

Concrete scalability strategies are outlined (semi-discrete targets, multi-scale Sinkhorn, multi-resolution low-discrepancy grids with early stopping, Nyström approximations, and test-time pruning), with a commitment to quantify wall-clock, memory, and default schedules for m and ε.
Commitments to an algorithm box detailing the full Train/Calib/Test pipeline, thorough proofreading/notation cleanup, and code release for full reproducibility.
weaknesses: Conceptual novelty remains modest: finite-sample validity follows from standard CP for any fixed scalar score; the main advance is a practical OT-based scalarization leveraging known center-outward ranks.

Key evaluation enhancements (additional baselines, main-text coverage tables with statistical tests, matched backbones) are not yet realized in the current draft; conclusions about superiority remain insufficiently substantiated without these results.

Scalability remains a practical concern in the current version: the proposed efficiency techniques are not yet implemented or empirically quantified, and large grids (e.g., m on the order of 2^15) can be costly at train and test time.
Clarity improvements are promised but not yet reflected in the submission; current typos/formatting inconsistencies and ambiguous notation still hamper readability.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal satisfactorily clarifies the split-conformal protocol and exchangeability, appropriately tempers the claims, and commits to a more rigorous and fair empirical comparison (adding CD/HPD-split and copula-based baselines on the same MQF² backbone), main-text coverage reporting, and quantified runtime/memory analyses. It also outlines practical scalability strategies and promises clearer presentation and reproducibility. These responses alleviate earlier soundness and clarity concerns in principle. However, the current draft still lacks the expanded results and quantified efficiency gains, and the conceptual novelty remains limited to a well-motivated, practical scalarization using center-outward ranks. Given that the key empirical and presentation improvements are not yet incorporated, I recommend rejection at this time; a revised version implementing these changes could be competitive.

strengths: The authors fully clarify the split-conformal protocol: both the base predictor and approximate transport map are fit on Train only; the scalar score s(x,y)=||T_hat(S(x,y))|| is fixed before calibration; the threshold q_alpha is the empirical (1−alpha) quantile on Calib with explicit tie handling. This reduction to standard split CP on scalar scores makes the finite-sample validity unambiguous.

They commit to a formal theorem and proof for coverage with any fixed approximate map, an explicit algorithm box detailing Train/Calib/Test steps, and comprehensive fixes to notation/formatting—addressing prior clarity and rigor issues.

The evaluation plan is substantially strengthened: numerical tables with mean±SE for coverage and size, paired significance tests, matched baselines (including CD/HPD-split and copula-based methods) under identical backbones and budgets, and detailed reporting of splits and hyperparameters.

Computational practicality is addressed with concrete, validity-preserving accelerations (semi-discrete targets with multi-scale Sinkhorn, multi-resolution low-discrepancy grids with early stopping, Nyström approximations, and test-time pruning) plus runtime/memory tables and a dimension-aware default schedule for (m, ε).

They will include model-agnostic size/efficiency metrics (e.g., average L2 and Mahalanobis radii, per-dimension widths, low-d Lebesgue volume via QMC) and test sensitivity to the MQF² backbone by using multiple trainings and comparing against these proxies.
weaknesses: Despite the added accelerations and guidance, the approach can remain computationally heavy in higher dimensions (training O(n_train·m), inference O(m)), and practical scalability beyond the reported regimes may still be challenging.

Hyperparameter selection and robustness in very high-dimensional outputs are addressed via Train-only diagnostics and optional projection-based variants, but these remain heuristic and may require nontrivial tuning and engineering to achieve stable efficiency gains.

The theoretical contribution centers on split CP validity after scalarization; deeper analysis of the statistical efficiency of the OT-based scalar score (e.g., conditions under which it improves region size over simpler norms) is still limited.
reviewers.summary_comment: The second rebuttal thoroughly addresses the remaining practical concerns. The clarified split-conformal setup with a fixed Train-only transport map resolves validity questions and will be formalized with a theorem, proof, and an algorithm box. The expanded empirical plan—adding numerical tables with significance, stronger and matched baselines, model-agnostic size metrics, and comprehensive runtime/memory reporting—substantially improves credibility and fairness. Concrete, validity-preserving accelerations and a dimension-aware default schedule provide actionable guidance for practitioners, and the inclusion of Train-only auto-tuning and stability analyses directly targets hyperparameter sensitivity. While computational overhead and high-dimensional robustness remain nontrivial, the method’s core idea and its now clearly stated protocol are sound and practically meaningful. With the promised revisions and released code, I recommend acceptance.

strengths: The rebuttal provides a clear split-conformal pipeline with a fixed, train-only transport map and a scalar score s(x,y)=||T_hat(S(x,y))||, resolving the key exchangeability concern and improving reproducibility.

A formal split-conformal coverage theorem (to be added) explicitly ties the practical procedure to standard CP guarantees for any fixed approximate map, eliminating the need for consistency of T_hat with the Brenier map.

The authors commit to stronger and fairer experiments: harmonized splits/budgets, added baselines (CD/HPD-split and copula-based methods), train-only hyperparameter selection for (m, ε), and model-agnostic region-size metrics with sensitivity checks.

Concrete, validity-preserving accelerations are outlined (multi-scale/semi-discrete targets, Nyström approximations, test-time pruning, multi-resolution grids) alongside plans to report wall-clock time and memory across dimensions.
Code release and detailed reporting (tables with mean±SE, paired tests, documented hyperparameters) are promised, which would substantially improve transparency and replicability.
weaknesses: The current manuscript still lacks the promised algorithmic summary, formal theorem, and corrected presentation; clarity and reproducibility remain insufficient at review time.
The expanded empirical evaluation (additional baselines, model-agnostic size metrics, fairness controls, cross-fitting) is not yet implemented, so claims of superiority remain partially supported.

Scalability remains a concern without quantitative evidence: the O(n_train·m) Sinkhorn training and O(m) evaluation (e.g., with m=2^15) could be prohibitive; the proposed accelerations need to be demonstrated with concrete runtime/memory and size trade-offs.

Region-size assessment still relies primarily on MQF^2 importance sampling in the current results; alternative, model-agnostic size proxies and stability across independently trained MQF^2 models are not yet shown.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper proposes OT-CP, using optimal-transport center-outward ranks to scalarize multivariate conformity scores for standard conformal prediction. The rebuttal substantially clarifies the methodology: a split-conformal protocol with a fixed train-only transport map, an explicit scalar scoring function, and a formal finite-sample coverage theorem to be added. The authors also promise a stronger, fairer evaluation (added baselines, model-agnostic size metrics, harmonized splits/budgets) and concrete scalability experiments with accelerations, plus code release. These commitments convincingly address the main conceptual and validity concerns. However, the current manuscript still lacks these crucial additions and maintains presentation issues, and the stronger empirical and scalability evidence has not yet been incorporated. Given the gap between the promised revisions and the present submission, I recommend rejection at this stage, with the expectation that a revised version implementing these changes would be significantly stronger.

why_it_fits: Topic alignment: Conformal prediction, optimal transport, and distribution-free uncertainty are core to ICML’s ‘probabilistic methods’, ‘trustworthy ML’, and ‘theory’ tracks; the paper’s center-outward (OT-based) ranks for multivariate CP directly engage these areas.

Contribution type: A methodological framework with finite-sample coverage via split conformal and practical OT-based scalarization, plus empirical benchmarking across multivariate regression tasks, matches ICML’s blend of theory + methodology + applications.

Reviewer support for soundness: Reviewers note the clarified split-conformal protocol with a fixed Train-only transport map and a scalar score s(x,y)=||T̂(S(x,y))||, making the validity unambiguous (R2) and resolving exchangeability concerns (R1, R3).
why_it_does_not_fit: Novelty concerns: Two reviewers characterize the conceptual novelty as modest (R1, R3) since finite-sample validity follows standard CP for any fixed scalar score; ICML’s bar for conceptual advancement is high.

Incomplete empirical substantiation at submission time: The promised stronger baselines (e.g., CD/HPD-split, copula methods), matched backbones, and main-text coverage tables with statistical tests are not yet incorporated (R1, R3), weakening the case for impact at ICML.

Scalability and systems evidence are missing: Although accelerations are outlined, their implementation and quantified runtime/memory benefits are not demonstrated (R1, R3). ICML often expects concrete systems/efficiency evidence when computation is heavy (Sinkhorn with large m).
Presentation issues remain: Typos/notation/formatting noted by reviewers (R1, R3) reduce clarity; ICML typically expects polished exposition.

why_it_fits: Topic alignment: NeurIPS explicitly welcomes probabilistic methods, optimization, SysML infrastructure, and data-centric AI; OT-based scalarization for multivariate CP and entropic OT maps (Sinkhorn) fit well within these topics.

Interdisciplinary/engineering angle: NeurIPS values practical algorithmic contributions and in-depth analyses of existing methods; the paper operationalizes center-outward ranks with concrete accelerations (semi-discrete targets, multi-scale Sinkhorn, Nyström, pruning) and plans comprehensive runtime/memory reporting (R1–R3).

Clarified protocol and coverage: As highlighted by R2 (accept), the split-conformal setup with a fixed Train-only map and a formal coverage theorem (to be added) provides a clean, reproducible pipeline—often appreciated by NeurIPS’s methodological tracks.
why_it_does_not_fit: Execution gap: Key promised empirical upgrades (matched baselines on the same backbone, numerical tables with significance tests, model-agnostic size metrics) are not yet in the draft (R1, R3), risking acceptance in a highly competitive venue.

Modest conceptual novelty: Reviewers emphasize that the main advance is a practical scalarization using known center-outward ranks (R1, R3); NeurIPS typically looks for either clearer theoretical advances or compelling empirical superiority.
Compute concerns without demonstrated mitigation: Training/inference cost with large m remains high; while accelerations are proposed, they are not empirically validated in the submission (R1, R3).

Scope of experiments: Current results focus on tabular multivariate regression; NeurIPS often favors breadth across modalities or stronger demonstrations on modern architectures/tasks unless the methodological novelty is substantial.

why_it_fits: Some topic overlap: ICLR includes probabilistic methods, optimization, and general ML; conformal prediction with OT mappings fits these areas in principle.

Connection to deep models in evaluation: The MQF² normalizing flow backbone appears in experiments (even if used mainly for evaluation), providing a tenuous link to ICLR’s representation learning focus.
why_it_does_not_fit: Venue focus mismatch: The core contribution is statistical methodology for CP and OT-based scalarization, not representation learning or deep architectures—ICLR’s primary emphasis.

Limited deep learning angle: The method is largely model-agnostic and not centered on learning representations; tabular multivariate regression benchmarks and OT map estimation dominate, which is less typical for ICLR acceptances without strong DL contributions.

Execution and clarity gaps: Reviewers flag missing formal theorem/algorithm box, incomplete empirical upgrades, and clarity/notation issues (R1, R3); ICLR’s standards for polished presentation are high.

Modest novelty: As per R1 and R3, novelty is primarily operationalizing known center-outward ranks with standard split CP validity; without stronger deep-learning implications or broader DL experiments, fit is weaker than for ICML/NeurIPS.

strengths: Well-motivated and practical RL objective: a correct-only, per-prompt normalized and sigmoid-bounded length penalty controlled by a single α, enabling smooth accuracy–compute trade-offs and a family of operating points.

Robust methodological clarifications: the rebuttal details warm-starting per-prompt MEAN/STD from base model samples, online EMA with shrinkage to dataset-level priors, ε floors and clipping, and confirms the penalty applies only to correct trajectories with correctness dominance for any α<1.

Insightful stability analysis: clear identification of how advantage normalization can negate the intended effect of α in length-penalized objectives, coupled with a simple PPO+RLOO recipe that works with very few updates.

Expanded evaluation commitments: multi-seed results with CIs, stronger decoding-time and preference-learning baselines, global vs per-prompt penalty ablations, real serving metrics (latency, throughput, KV cache), and cross-domain verifiable tasks.

Operational practicality: per-prompt statistics are used only during training (not at inference), involve minimal memory/time overhead, and the approach integrates with standard PPO pipelines without value networks.
weaknesses: Novelty remains moderate amid concurrent length-penalized RL approaches; the distinctiveness lies in per-prompt normalization, bounded rewards, and the advantage-normalization insight rather than a fundamentally new algorithm.

Evidence beyond math with exact verifiers and concrete serving gains is currently promised rather than shown; acceptance hinges on including the latency/throughput/KV metrics, cross-domain results, and stronger baselines in the final version.
While the authors argue the training-time overhead of per-prompt statistics is negligible, quantitative profiling and ablations on operational costs are needed to substantiate deployment feasibility.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper introduces a simple and effective RL objective that reduces chain-of-thought length while preserving accuracy by penalizing only correct responses with a per-prompt normalized, sigmoid-bounded term. The rebuttal convincingly addresses earlier concerns by detailing stable estimation of per-prompt statistics (warm-start, EMA, shrinkage, ε floors), clarifying that penalties never incentivize incorrect answers for α<1, and committing to a substantially strengthened evaluation: multi-seed CIs, stronger baselines, real serving metrics, and cross-domain verifiable tasks, alongside a data hygiene protocol. The stability insight regarding advantage normalization is valuable in practice. Although novelty is moderate and some additions are pending, the approach’s practicality, controllable trade-offs, and clarified methodology make it a useful contribution. I recommend acceptance, contingent on incorporating the promised expanded evaluation and clarifications in the camera-ready.

strengths: The rebuttal provides concrete methodological clarifications: per-prompt, correct-only length normalization with EMA updates, shrinkage to dataset-level priors, epsilon floors, z-score clipping, and sigmoid bounding, alongside a clear no-correct fallback—together preserving correctness-first incentives for any α < 1.

Useful differentiation beyond a simple length penalty: a single tunable knob yielding a family of accuracy–compute operating points from one checkpoint, and a compelling stability insight explaining how standard advantage normalization can neutralize the intended penalty signal.

Practicality remains a strong point: a value-free PPO+RLOO setup with modest compute; the authors commit to adding pseudo-code, explicit hyperparameters (including KL ablations), and a brief proof sketch of correctness dominance.

Substantive commitments to strengthen the evaluation: multi-seed results with confidence intervals, stronger baselines (decoding-time controls, improved offline preferences, global vs per-prompt penalties), system-level serving metrics (latency/throughput/KV), cross-domain validation with verifiers, and a rigorous de-duplication pipeline.
weaknesses: Most improvements are still prospective: the current manuscript lacks multi-seed statistics, stronger baselines, cross-domain validation, system-level performance metrics, and de-duplication/contamination analysis; thus the empirical evidence remains limited and math-only.
Novelty remains moderate relative to concurrent length-penalized RL; the distinctiveness hinges on the promised broader validation and ablations, which are not yet included.

Operational practicality is not fully quantified: the overhead/sensitivity of maintaining per-prompt statistics (EMA, shrinkage parameters) and their impact on training stability and deployment has not been empirically evaluated.
Presentation artifacts and incomplete figures/tables persist in the current draft, hindering reproducibility despite commitments to fix them.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors’ rebuttal credibly clarifies the reward design and training details—per-prompt, correct-only normalization with bounded penalties and a no-correct fallback—improving confidence in soundness. They also articulate meaningful differentiation (tunable trade-offs, advantage-normalization insight) and commit to substantial enhancements: multi-seed CIs, stronger baselines, system-level metrics, cross-domain tasks, and rigorous de-duplication. If delivered, these additions would address the main concerns and could make the paper competitive. However, in its current form, the manuscript still lacks these results, remains math-only with limited baselines and no statistical rigor or serving metrics, and has unresolved contamination checks and presentation issues. I therefore maintain a reject recommendation at this stage, while viewing the direction as promising contingent on the promised revisions.

strengths: Straightforward RL formulation with a bounded, correct-only length penalty (Eq. (4)/(5)) that yields a tunable accuracy–compute trade-off via a single hyperparameter; easy to integrate into PPO+RLOO pipelines.

The rebuttal provides substantial methodological clarifications: per-prompt MEAN/STD estimation via warm-start, EMA updates, shrinkage to dataset-level priors, ε floors on STD, z-score clipping, and an explicit correctness-dominance guarantee for α < 1; KL regularization is anchored to a frozen reference policy with planned sensitivity ablations.

Empirical results show sizable token reductions with small accuracy changes on multiple math benchmarks along smooth trade-off curves (e.g., Figures 2–3), and the analysis of advantage normalization is insightful for stability.
Computational efficiency: short RL training (≈100 steps), open-weight models, and accessible tooling (OpenRLHF PPO+RLOO, vLLM) make the approach practical.

The authors commit to meaningful extensions: multi-seed runs with confidence intervals, stronger baselines (decoding-time penalties, offline preference optimization, global penalty variant), system-level serving metrics, cross-domain verifiable tasks (e.g., code with unit tests), and data hygiene with de-duplication.
weaknesses: At submission time, the evaluation remains math-only with limited baselines and no statistical significance reporting; the promised multi-seed CIs, stronger baselines, cross-domain tasks, and system-level metrics are not yet present for verification.

Quantitative reporting is still insufficient (lack of comprehensive numeric tables, confidence intervals, and error bars), and there is no analysis of side effects such as premature stopping, robustness to prompt perturbations, or process-quality changes in the current manuscript.

Key implementation specifics (e.g., PPO clip ε, gradient-norm clipping, KL coefficient sensitivity) and data hygiene/de-duplication details are provided only in the rebuttal and need to be integrated into the paper; without them, reproducibility remains limited.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper introduces a simple and practical RL approach to reduce test-time compute of reasoning models by applying a bounded, per-prompt normalized length penalty to correct trajectories. The rebuttal substantially improves confidence in the method’s soundness by detailing robust normalization (warm-start, EMA, shrinkage, ε floors, clipping), clarifying the KL-regularized PPO setup, and committing to stronger evaluation and reporting (multi-seed CIs, decoding-time baselines, offline preference methods, cross-domain verifiable tasks, system-level serving metrics, and rigorous data hygiene). These planned additions would significantly strengthen the work. However, the current submission still lacks the broader empirical evidence, statistical rigor, and comprehensive reporting required for acceptance: evaluation is confined to math with limited baselines, numerical tables and CIs are absent, and system-level metrics and side-effect analyses are not included. I encourage the authors to incorporate the promised experiments and methodological details; with those in place, the contribution could be above the bar. Given the present evidence, I recommend rejection.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: squarely within ICML’s core areas (reinforcement learning, optimization, and ML systems). The work trains LLMs to adapt test-time compute via an RL objective, aligning with ICML interests in scalable ML, evaluation methodology, and application-driven efficiency/sustainability.

Contribution fit: a practical RL objective (per‑prompt, correct‑only, sigmoid‑bounded length penalty) that integrates into standard PPO pipelines without a value network; produces controllable accuracy–compute trade-offs and a family of operating points—useful for deployment and ML systems audiences.

Rigor/presentation strengths per reviews: clear methodological clarifications in the rebuttal (EMA/shrinkage for per‑prompt stats, ε floors, clipping, correctness dominance for α<1) and an insightful analysis that advantage normalization can neutralize the intended penalty signal—valuable to RL practitioners.
why_it_does_not_fit: Novelty fit is moderate: reviewers note concurrent length‑penalized RL approaches; distinctiveness rests on per‑prompt normalization and the advantage‑normalization insight rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic framework.

Rigor/evaluation gaps for ICML standards: current evidence is math‑only with limited baselines, no multi‑seed statistics/CIs, and no system‑level serving metrics (latency, throughput, KV cache), which ICML ML-systems reviewers typically expect.

Presentation risk: reviewers flag incomplete figures/tables and missing quantitative profiling of the per‑prompt statistics overhead; two of three reviewers recommend rejection pending promised additions—raising acceptance risk at ICML.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: directly aligns with NeurIPS tracks in SysML Infrastructure, Optimization, Decision‑making/RL, Data‑centric AI, and Deep Learning. Reducing LLM test‑time compute and improving serving efficiency are highly topical for NeurIPS.

Contribution fit: an easily deployable RL objective yielding a tunable family of accuracy–compute operating points from a single checkpoint, plus a practically relevant stability insight about advantage normalization—strong appeal to the NeurIPS practitioner and systems/optimization communities.
Broader impact framing: explicit motivation around efficiency, latency, and sustainability resonates with NeurIPS’ interest in real‑world deployment and socio‑technical considerations.
why_it_does_not_fit: Novelty remains incremental amid concurrent work; the main technical distinctiveness (per‑prompt normalized, bounded penalty; advantage‑normalization insight) may be viewed as an engineering refinement rather than a fundamentally new ML method.

Rigor/evaluation gaps: as reviewers emphasize, the current submission lacks multi‑seed CIs, stronger decoding‑time and preference‑learning baselines, cross‑domain validation, and concrete serving metrics—common expectations for NeurIPS acceptance.

Reviewer risk: two reviewers recommend rejection due to evidence being pending rather than included; the reliance on promised additions makes acceptance at NeurIPS risky without the expanded empirical section in place.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: falls under ICLR’s RL, optimization, and general deep learning training methods; the PPO+RLOO setup and reward shaping for LLMs is within scope.

Contribution fit: a simple training objective that can be broadly applied to reasoning LMs to control test‑time compute; accessible implementation and open‑weight experiments are positives for the ICLR community.
Clarity of the core idea: reviewers acknowledge the bounded, correct‑only penalty and training stability guidance as easy to integrate into existing deep RL pipelines.
why_it_does_not_fit: ICLR often prioritizes stronger representation‑learning or theoretical contributions; here the novelty is moderate and primarily an RL objective tweak with practical insights rather than new representational or theoretical advances.

Rigor/evaluation shortfalls are pronounced for ICLR expectations: math‑only evaluation, limited baselines, no multi‑seed statistics/CIs, no cross‑domain tasks, and missing system‑level metrics; side‑effect analyses (e.g., premature stopping, robustness) are also absent.

Presentation/reproducibility concerns: reviewers note incomplete figures/tables and missing hyperparameter/implementation details in the manuscript (though provided in rebuttal), which can be a barrier for ICLR acceptance; overall reviewer sentiment is two rejects versus one accept.

strengths: The rebuttal provides concrete clarifications and corrections: explicit squared-error surrogate objective, two-point finite-difference estimator details, corrected support-set definitions, and explicit stopping criteria and query-budget accounting.

Offers a principled rationale for the surrogate objective (triangle-inequality bound) and plans to empirically validate alignment with true error via correlation analyses and local sensitivity estimation.

Commits to broader, fair baselines under matched query budgets and constraints (NES, SPSA, Liu et al. 2024b, and random-sparse variants) and to multi-seed evaluation with confidence intervals and significance testing.
Adds practical detectability analyses (rolling MAD/std coverage, SNR, anomaly detectors) and ablations over ε and τ, directly addressing imperceptibility and trade-offs.

Addresses practicality with query-frugal refinements (successive-halving screening, group-testing for support discovery, adaptive Δ, early stopping) and plans to report efficacy vs. queries curves; also commits to releasing code, prompts, and full configuration details to ensure reproducibility.
weaknesses: Most improvements remain prospective; the current submission still lacks the expanded baselines, statistical robustness, detectability studies, and query–efficacy curves needed to substantiate claims under realistic constraints.

The surrogate objective continues to optimize deviation from the model’s own output; while bounded and motivated, guarantees are limited without verified assumptions (e.g., local Lipschitzness), and practical reliability may vary across models and datasets.

Query costs may remain high in black-box settings; although frugal strategies are proposed, their empirical effectiveness and comparisons to query-efficient baselines under tight budgets are not yet demonstrated.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors have responded constructively with specific clarifications, theoretical framing, and a thorough plan to strengthen evaluation, detectability assessment, query efficiency, and reproducibility. These steps, if fully implemented, would materially improve the paper’s soundness and fairness. However, at review time these additions are not yet reflected in the results, and the empirical case under matched query budgets and realistic detectability constraints remains incomplete. The surrogate objective’s guarantees are still conditional and not yet validated across settings. I recommend rejection for the current version, with the view that a revised submission incorporating the promised experiments, analyses, and artifacts could be competitive.

strengths: The authors provide detailed, concrete revisions: corrected support-set notation, an explicit label-free squared-error surrogate, a two-point central finite-difference estimator, and full pseudocode with defined hyperparameters and stopping criteria.

Evaluation plans are significantly strengthened with matched perturbation budgets (per-step, total L1/L2 energy, and query budgets), added standard black-box baselines (NES, SPSA, and Liu et al. 2024b), random-sparse baselines, multi-seed runs, confidence intervals, and statistical tests.

Defense coverage is broadened to include robust filters (Hampel, Savitzky–Golay, TV denoising, wavelets) and an empirical assessment of the proposed autocorrelation-based backcasting detector, with precision/recall/AUC and downstream error recovery.

Comprehensive query-efficiency accounting and proposed frugal variants (successive halving screening, group-testing for support discovery, adaptive Δ, early stopping) address practicality and provide efficacy–query trade-off curves.

Reproducibility is improved via promised release of prompts/configurations for closed-source APIs (temperature=0, top_p=1, tokenization rules, version notes), code for open-source models, and repeated trials to quantify nondeterminism.
weaknesses: The current manuscript does not yet reflect the promised changes (revised equations, full pseudocode, matched-budget baselines, query distributions, multi-seed statistics, expanded defenses, and detector evaluation), leaving key concerns unverified at review time.

The theoretical connection between the surrogate objective and true forecasting error remains limited (triangle-inequality lower bound dependent on e_clean and local smoothness), providing only a weak guarantee in challenging regimes.

Despite proposed frugal variants, the total query cost may still be substantial for longer inputs or tighter budgets; practicality claims require in-paper evidence through the promised query–efficacy curves and distributions.

Closed-source API reproducibility remains potentially brittle due to version drift and residual nondeterminism; strong in-paper evidence (exact prompts/configs, repeated runs, and stability analyses) is necessary to substantiate claims.
reviewers.summary_comment: The second rebuttal is thorough and proposes concrete, substantive revisions that, if implemented, would address most prior concerns regarding technical clarity, evaluation fairness, defenses, query efficiency, and reproducibility. The additions—explicit surrogate loss, standard two-point finite-difference estimator, full pseudocode and hyperparameters, matched-budget baselines (including NES/SPSA), multi-seed statistics, expanded defenses with detector evaluation, and detailed query accounting with frugal variants—are appropriate and convincing plans. However, these remain commitments rather than demonstrated results in the current manuscript. The theoretical justification linking the surrogate objective to true error is improved but still modest, and practical feasibility hinges on the unreported query–efficacy results. Given the current state, I maintain a reject recommendation, with the view that incorporating the promised revisions could elevate this work to a strong contribution in a subsequent submission.

strengths: The rebuttal provides precise corrections to the core formulation (τ-sparse, label-free objective; corrected support-set; two-point zero-order estimator with projection), which materially improves methodological soundness.

Commits to comprehensive, budget-matched baselines (NES, SPSA, Liu et al. 2024b) and random-sparse ablations, along with multi-seed statistics and confidence intervals, addressing prior concerns about fairness and robustness of evaluation.

Introduces query accounting, stopping criteria, and frugal query variants (successive halving, group testing), plus query–effectiveness curves, which would make the practicality of the attack more credible.

Strengthens theoretical motivation with an explicit lower bound linking the surrogate objective to true error and a restricted sensitivity assumption, and plans empirical correlation analyses to validate alignment.

Promises full reproducibility artifacts (code, prompts, decoding settings, API/version details) and a broader, tuned defense evaluation including an implemented autocorrelation-based detection with proper metrics.
weaknesses: Most improvements are prospective; the current manuscript still contains technical inaccuracies, incomplete algorithmic details, and limited experiments, so the key claims are not yet substantiated.

The surrogate objective, while better justified, still relies on assumptions (restricted sensitivity) that are unverified in the present submission; practical reliability across models/datasets remains to be demonstrated empirically.
Query efficiency and API feasibility are not evidenced in the existing results; without concrete query budgets and performance under tight limits, practical impact is uncertain.
Expanded defenses and detection remain unimplemented in the current version; effectiveness against stronger/tuned defenses needs to be shown to support robustness-related conclusions.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors’ rebuttal is thorough and directly addresses the main concerns with concrete, appropriate revisions: a corrected and clearer attack formulation, a standard zero-order estimation scheme with projection, comprehensive and budget-matched baselines and ablations, explicit query accounting and frugal variants, strengthened theoretical motivation with planned empirical validation, and significantly improved reproducibility and defense evaluations. However, these changes are not yet reflected in the submitted manuscript or results, and thus the central issues of soundness verification, fairness, and practicality remain unresolved at review time. If the promised revisions and experiments are incorporated and demonstrate consistent gains under matched query and perturbation budgets, validated by statistical significance and query–effectiveness analyses, the work would be substantially strengthened. As it stands, I recommend rejection, encouraging resubmission with the updated methodology and full experimental evidence.

strengths: The authors now provide precise, set-based definitions for I_{i,j} and T terms, clarify implementation via BFS layers, and commit to consistent notation, which substantially improves clarity and reproducibility.

They offer concrete computational guidance: per-pair operation counts O(k^2 (m + 2^p)), per-node memory formulas n · k · (4m + 2^p) bytes, and a clear precomputation strategy that avoids materializing the n×n×(k^2+2k) tensor.

A principled estimator analysis is outlined, including MinHash and HLL bias/variance, corrected claims about unbiasedness, and delta-method aggregation for intersection estimates, along with plans to study downstream impact.

They commit to controlled same-backbone comparisons (swapping structural biases only), sketch-parameter ablations (m, p) with both estimator error and downstream performance, and to correcting table highlighting and claims.

Scalability considerations are discussed with concrete mitigation strategies (sparse/top-K attention, blockwise/anchor-based variants, mixed encodings), indicating thoughtful pathways beyond dense attention.
weaknesses: Despite detailed plans and formulas, key empirical additions (runtime/memory measurements, controlled same-backbone comparisons, sketch-parameter ablations) are not yet present in the submission; reliance on future revisions and promised supplements limits current evaluability.
The theoretical enhancements remain limited to estimator properties and a narrow expressivity proposition; there is still no rigorous analysis of expressivity relative to established frameworks.
Even with on-the-fly computation, the approach inherits O(n^2) attention costs; the proposed scalability variants are prospective and not empirically validated here.

Performance gains remain non-uniform across tasks (e.g., GRIT outperforming on Peptides-func), and while the authors will correct presentation, the current manuscript still contains confusing highlights and overstated claims.
reviewers.summary_comment: The second rebuttal substantially improves the paper’s clarity and methodological soundness: definitions are precisely restated, computation and memory costs are quantified with concrete formulas, estimator behavior is analyzed with appropriate caveats, and sensible scalability strategies are described. The authors also commit to controlled comparisons, sketch-parameter ablations, and corrected result presentation, and indicate they will upload supplementary materials. However, these crucial empirical elements are not yet available in the current submission, and the theoretical additions do not extend beyond estimator properties and limited expressivity insights. Given the absence of concrete runtime/memory benchmarks, backbone-controlled studies, and ablations at review time, I maintain a reject recommendation. The method is promising, and with the proposed additions and corrected presentation, it could be competitive in a revised version.

strengths: The authors convert prior commitments into concrete additions: controlled same-backbone comparisons (swapping SPD/LapPE-RWPE/GIST), runtime and memory tables, sketch-parameter ablations (m, p, k), corrected tables/figures, and a full reproducibility package, which meaningfully strengthen fairness and transparency.

They improve theoretical grounding by correcting the estimator claims, stating independence conditions, providing delta-method bias/variance analyses, variance propagation for 2D differencing, and practical guidance on selecting k, m, p; they also specify handling for small negative estimates via clipping and quantify its impact.

They offer clearer expressiveness positioning with a formal proposition that the joint distance histogram refines SPD, supported by examples, and add empirical scalability mitigations (top-K and blockwise attention) with reported accuracy–efficiency trade-offs, plus rules of thumb for practitioner settings.
Overall, the method remains simple and integrates cleanly into attention while achieving strong results on ZINC/ZINC-full and competitive performance on LRGB/MoleculeNet.
weaknesses: Several enhancements (controlled comparisons, overhead tables, sketch ablations, mitigation studies) are described but not yet visible in the current manuscript; acceptance relies on their inclusion as promised.

Expressiveness analysis remains limited relative to WL hierarchies; the new proposition refines SPD but does not establish stronger theoretical guarantees, and the approach remains largely empirically justified.
Despite proposed mitigations, the O(n^2) attention cost persists and large-scale applicability depends on the effectiveness of sparse/blockwise variants, which are only briefly evaluated.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors have addressed the remaining concerns with specific, substantive additions: detailed algorithmic clarifications, corrected and quantified estimator properties, moderated and corrected empirical claims, controlled same-backbone comparisons, runtime/memory reporting, and sketch-parameter ablations. They also add a formal statement about expressiveness relative to SPD and demonstrate practical scalability mitigations. While some elements are not yet present in the current draft, the planned revisions and prepared analyses appear sufficient to resolve prior reservations. Provided these additions are incorporated as described, the work is novel, sound, and practically valuable, and merits acceptance.

strengths: The authors provide a concrete theoretical positioning by adding a formal proposition that GIST’s joint exact-distance histogram strictly refines shortest-path encodings, complemented by planned synthetic experiments, while avoiding overclaims relative to WL hierarchies.

They address estimator correctness and noise propagation rigorously: clarifying MinHash/HLL properties, independence requirements, delta-method error analysis, 2D inclusion–exclusion variance bounds, recommended parameter ranges, and handling of small negative estimates with quantified bias.

Scalability and practicality are strengthened by precise memory/compute formulas, on-the-fly computation that avoids dense tensors, concrete parameter guidance (k, m, p), and planned runtime/memory tables; they also propose and will evaluate top-K and blockwise attention variants for larger graphs.

Independence/correlation assumptions are explicitly stated, including use of independent hash families and seeds and a discussion of cross-hop correlations with conservative variance bounds and plans to report observed covariances.
weaknesses: Expressivity beyond refining SPD remains largely intuitive without formal guarantees relative to WL-style hierarchies; while not strictly required, such results would further solidify the theoretical contribution.

Scalability to very large graphs and larger k still depends on sketch sizes and sparsification strategies; the empirical validation of top-K/blockwise trade-offs and comprehensive overhead profiling are pending the final revision.

Some improvements are commitments for the camera-ready (controlled backbone comparisons, runtime/memory tables, code release), so the final clarity and reproducibility depend on incorporating these additions as promised.
reviewers.summary_comment: The additional rebuttal effectively addresses the remaining concerns by adding a formal separation over SPD, detailing estimator independence and error propagation (including inclusion–exclusion noise and practical parameter settings), and providing concrete memory/compute estimates with mitigation strategies for large-scale scenarios. While formal expressivity relative to WL remains out of scope and some scalability validations are pending, the planned controlled comparisons, overhead tables, and code release directly target the previous gaps. Given the already strong empirical results and the thorough clarifications and additions outlined, I maintain my acceptance recommendation, contingent on incorporating the promised analyses and reproducibility materials.
