why_it_fits: Topic fit: ICLR welcomes optimization for deep learning, infrastructure/hardware, and training algorithms; the paper studies gradient-based training of neural networks on analog hardware, with a new algorithm (RL) and convergence guarantees.

Contribution type: Theoretical insights into training dynamics (implicit penalty) and a bilevel method that improves training stability are relevant to the ICLR community’s focus on learning algorithms for deep networks.

Novelty/rigor: Reviewers rate the novelty and soundness highly; the work generalizes prior linear-response theory and offers exact convergence under (approximate) zero-shift, with clear assumptions and proofs.
Empirical touchpoints: Experiments on CNNs/ResNets/MobileNet and ablations connect the theory to neural network training, fitting ICLR’s emphasis on learning efficacy.
why_it_does_not_fit: Empirical depth vs. expectation: ICLR often favors strong empirical validation on modern, large-scale DL tasks; current results are simulation-only and mostly small-to-mid scale, with broader evaluations promised but not yet included.

Scope relative to representation learning: The primary contribution is theoretical and hardware-centric rather than advancing representation learning per se, which may dampen fit for core ICLR tracks.

Reviewer-flagged risks: Exact convergence relies on near-zero shift; robustness for large bit-lengths/highly state-dependent responses is limited; the lack of on-silicon validation may be viewed critically by ICLR audiences.

Tuning/engineering dependencies: RL v2 introduces practical mechanisms (buffers, thresholds) whose real-world tuning and failure modes (under severe non-idealities) remain to be fully characterized, which could be seen as under-specified for ICLR’s empirical standards.

strengths: The rebuttal meaningfully strengthens external validity and robustness: authors commit to adding CIFAR-10 color and class-conditional replications, complementary semantic-copying measures (CLIP/DINO-v2 embeddings, LPIPS, PRD), and optimizer sweeps (SGD/momentum/Adam/AdamW) with time-rescaling collapses, with code/scripts to be released.

Core empirical findings remain compelling and well-supported: identification of two timescales with concrete anchors (e.g., τ_gen ≈ 1e5 steps largely independent of n; τ_mem collapsing when time is rescaled by n with onset near τ/n ≈ 300; τ_gen ∝ W^{-1} and τ_mem ∝ n W^{-1}), and an (n, p, τ) phase diagram providing actionable guidance for early stopping and capacity control.

The theoretical–empirical bridge is improved: authors will formalize Theorem 3.2 in the main text (with a proof sketch and explicit assumptions for Theorem 3.1), and clarify how fixed-t spectral analysis explains the persistence of the τ_gen–τ_mem gap under integrated-t training by attributing slow modes to low-noise slices; this maps well onto observed training/test loss dynamics and memorization onsets.

Reproducibility and reporting are strong: open code, detailed protocols, error bars/bootstraps, ablations over k in the memorization criterion, and clear statements on compute constraints and scope of added experiments.
weaknesses: Several key enhancements are still promissory at this stage (CIFAR-10 color/conditional validations, semantic memorization metrics, and optimizer sweeps); until incorporated, the main empirical evidence remains centered on 32×32 grayscale CelebA and synthetic/toy data.

The theoretical model (RFNN with sub-Gaussian data at fixed t under gradient flow) remains an abstraction; despite clearer exposition, the connection to full U-Net training over t and to modern high-resolution/text-conditioned pipelines is indirect.
Semantic memorization detection in the current draft primarily uses L2 nearest-neighbor ratios; while the planned CLIP/DINO/LPIPS/PRD additions should address this, they are not yet present.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal addresses prior concerns with concrete, feasible additions that enhance external validity (CIFAR-10 color and class-conditional), metric robustness (CLIP/DINO-v2 embeddings, LPIPS, PRD), and optimizer sensitivity (showing time-rescaled collapses and τ_mem ∝ n invariance across optimizers). Theoretical clarity will improve via formalizing the main theorem and better linking fixed-t spectral analysis to practical integrated-t training, explaining why the slowest modes at low noise preserve the separation between τ_gen and τ_mem. Even before these additions, the manuscript presents strong evidence: stable τ_gen (≈1e5 updates) independent of n, τ_mem scaling linearly with n (collapse around τ/n ≈ 300), inverse scaling with width, and a clear (n, p, τ) phase diagram. While theory remains idealized and some validations are forthcoming, the overall package is rigorous, reproducible, and practically useful. I recommend acceptance, with the expectation that the promised additions are incorporated into the camera-ready.

strengths: Compelling identification of two training timescales with clear quantitative evidence: τ_gen ≈ 100K steps on CelebA and a delayed rise in memorization with a robust collapse when rescaling by τ/n (Fig. 2 inset around τ/n ≈ 300); capacity sweeps further show τ_gen ∝ W−1 and τ_mem ∝ nW−1 with a collapse near W τ_gen ≈ 3×10^6.

A principled theoretical explanation via a random-features model that yields two separated eigenvalue bulks mapping to fast population-driven learning and slow dataset-specific modes, accounting for τ_mem ∝ n; the rebuttal promises to formalize Theorem 3.2 in the main text, list assumptions, and include a proof sketch, improving rigor and readability.

Strong reproducibility and forthcoming extensions: code already released; authors commit to adding color CIFAR-10 (unconditional and class-conditional with classifier-free guidance), modestly higher resolution (CelebA 64×64), optimizer sweeps (SGD/Adam/AdamW), and frequency-domain diagnostics (FFT/DCT of score residuals, band-limited losses) to directly probe spectral bias and training dynamics.

Planned complementary memorization metrics (CLIP/DINO embedding NN retrieval, LPIPS, PRD) address concerns about reliance on L2 NN ratios (k = 1/3, with stated robustness to k = 1/2 and 1/4), strengthening the evidence against perceptual or semantic copying.

Clear articulation of limitations and a tighter bridge from fixed-t theory to practical training over t (memorization originating at small t), alongside a mapping from theoretical bulks to observed U-Net behavior (train/test loss divergence and delayed f_mem rise), enhances interpretability.
weaknesses: Several key empirical extensions are promised for the camera-ready (CIFAR-10 color/conditional, 64×64, optimizer sweeps, spectral diagnostics, semantic/perceptual memorization metrics) but are not yet included in the main submission; until incorporated, the empirical scope centers on CelebA 32×32 grayscale, unconditional.

The theoretical framework remains idealized (random features, fixed diffusion time, sub-Gaussian data, gradient flow); while the rebuttal improves the exposition and mapping to practice, the link to modern U-Nets and integrated-over-time training is still indirect.

Monitoring losses at a fixed diffusion time (e.g., t = 0.01) is sensible given the low-noise irregularity but may bias the timing estimates; although trends match memorization metrics, explicit multi-t monitoring would further substantiate claims.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper presents a timely and well-substantiated account of why diffusion models often avoid memorization despite overparameterization, demonstrating a clear separation between an early generalization time and a later memorization time that scales with n. The empirical evidence is strong (e.g., τ/n and W-based collapses; τ_gen ≈ 100K steps; τ/n ≈ 300 for memorization onset), and the theoretical analysis via random features provides a convincing mechanistic explanation tied to the spectrum of the feature correlation matrix. The rebuttal meaningfully addresses prior concerns with concrete and feasible commitments: broader datasets and conditioning, modest resolution increases, optimizer sweeps, direct frequency-domain diagnostics, formalized theory statements, and complementary semantic/perceptual memorization metrics. While these additions are promissory at review time, the core contributions are solid, the narrative is clear, and the planned extensions will substantially strengthen external validity and the theory–practice bridge. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: Compelling identification of two training timescales (tau_gen and tau_mem) with clear empirical support (tau_mem scaling linearly with n and both times scaling with W^{-1}) and a coherent mechanistic explanation via spectral separation in a random-features model.

Strong alignment between theory and practice: the spectral analysis explains the observed training dynamics and scaling laws; the rebuttal commits to formalizing the main theorem in the paper and surfacing assumptions to the main text, improving accessibility and rigor.

Thorough experimental methodology with dense (n, p) sweeps, error bars, controls (including full-batch), and released code; rebuttal further strengthens external validity via concrete additions (CIFAR-10 color and class-conditional runs, CelebA 64×64, attention-augmented U-Net) and commits to report collapses and deviations transparently.

Robustness of memorization assessment will be improved with planned CLIP/DINO embedding-space metrics, LPIPS-based measures, PRD curves, and additional membership-inference/retrieval diagnostics, which meaningfully address concerns about nearest-neighbor L2 heuristics.

Clearer anchoring and practical guidance: authors will include numeric anchors (e.g., tau_gen and tau/n collapse constants), and analyze optimizer effects and integrated-over-t training, making the results more actionable.
weaknesses: Several key extensions (CIFAR-10 color/conditional, 64×64 runs, attention variants, CLIP/DINO/LPIPS/PRD, membership-inference probes) are promised but not yet incorporated; acceptance assumes these additions will be implemented and replicate the reported scalings.

Even with the planned extensions, the empirical scope remains at modest resolutions and limited architectures; true generalization to substantially higher resolutions or very different architectures/datasets may still be open. The theoretical analysis remains idealized (random features at fixed t with sub-Gaussian data) and indirectly connected to time-conditioned U-Nets.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper offers a clear and well-substantiated account of implicit dynamical regularization in diffusion models: a fast, population-driven regime (tau_gen) followed by a slower, dataset-specific drift (tau_mem) that grows linearly with n, creating a widening early-stopping window. The theory (random-features spectral analysis) cogently explains the emergence and scaling of these timescales and aligns with the experimental observations. The rebuttal meaningfully addresses prior concerns with specific, feasible commitments: broader datasets (CIFAR-10 color and conditional), modest resolution increase (CelebA 64×64), an attention-augmented architecture, stronger memorization/privacy diagnostics (CLIP/DINO, LPIPS, PRD, and a simple membership-inference probe), explicit numeric anchors, optimizer sweeps, and an analysis connecting integrated-over-t training to the fixed-t theory. While these additions are not yet in the paper, they are well specified and, if included as stated, will significantly strengthen robustness and clarity. I recommend acceptance, with the expectation that the promised extensions and clarifications are incorporated.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Central ML contribution on generalization vs. memorization in diffusion models, touching core ICML areas (deep learning, probabilistic methods, optimization, evaluation/replicability).

Contribution fit: Balanced theory–empirics; random-features spectral analysis explains training dynamics, paired with actionable early-stopping/capacity guidance—well aligned with ICML’s theory, optimization, and evaluation tracks.

Novelty fit: Reviewers scored novelty 4/5; concrete new insights (two timescales τ_gen and τ_mem with τ_mem ∝ n and τ_gen ∝ W^{-1}, (n,p,τ) phase diagram) that extend understanding of overparameterized generative models.

Rigor fit: Reviewers rated soundness 4/5 and evaluation 4–5/5; formalized theorems with assumptions and proofs (to be surfaced in main text), strong empirical methodology with error bars/bootstraps and code release.
Presentation fit: Clarity rated 5/5; clear narrative linking theory to practice and practical guidance (early stopping windows, optimizer sensitivity, spectral bias diagnostics).
why_it_does_not_fit: Scope risk: Current empirical core centers on 32×32 grayscale CelebA; some broader validations (CIFAR-10 color/conditional, 64×64, attention variants) are promissory per reviewers and may weigh heavier at ICML.

Theory–practice gap: Theoretical model (RFNN at fixed t with sub-Gaussian data, gradient flow) is idealized; connection to full U-Nets integrated over t is indirect (reviewer concern), which ICML theory reviewers may scrutinize.

Measurement breadth: Reliance on L2 NN ratios for memorization in the submission; more semantic/perceptual metrics (CLIP/DINO/LPIPS/PRD) are promised but not yet integrated, potentially reducing perceived completeness.

Likelihood risk: Given some additions are planned for camera-ready, ICML’s bar for fully completed empirical validation across datasets may introduce moderate reviewer risk despite strong core results.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Squarely within NeurIPS themes (deep learning, probabilistic methods, optimization, data-centric aspects, theory); focuses on training dynamics and generalization in diffusion models.

Contribution fit: NeurIPS explicitly welcomes in-depth analyses of existing methods providing new insights; the two-timescale mechanism and spectral explanation are precisely such insights bridging theory and practice.

Novelty fit: Reviewers (all recommending accept) highlight robust quantitative discoveries (τ_gen ≈ 1e5 steps, τ_mem collapse with τ/n ≈ 300, τ_gen ∝ W^{-1}, τ_mem ∝ nW^{-1}) and an (n,p,τ) phase diagram—fresh and impactful.

Rigor fit: Strong reproducibility (code, protocols, error bars/bootstraps), clear assumptions and proofs (Theorem 3.1/3.2 to be formalized), optimizer sweeps/time-rescaling planned—reviewers rated soundness/evaluation 4–5.

Presentation fit: Clarity 5/5; the theory–empirics bridge and practical guidance (early stopping, capacity control, spectral diagnostics) align with NeurIPS’s interdisciplinary and tutorial-friendly ethos.
Reviewer risk: Low—reviewers already recommend acceptance contingent on integrating promised additions, indicating strong alignment with NeurIPS norms and expectations.
why_it_does_not_fit: Empirical breadth at submission time is limited (CelebA 32×32 grayscale, unconditional); while NeurIPS tolerates such scope for analytical papers, some reviewers may still prefer broader datasets/conditioning at submission.

Idealized theory (random features, fixed t, sub-Gaussian data) does not capture all modern pipelines; though reviewers accept the mapping, a minority could see this as a limitation of external validity.
Semantic memorization metrics (CLIP/DINO/LPIPS/PRD) and multi-t monitoring are promised but not yet included, leaving a small gap in the current experimental completeness.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Deep learning focus on diffusion models and representation/optimization dynamics matches ICLR’s remit (generative models, optimization, learning theory).

Contribution fit: Provides mechanistic understanding (spectral bias, slow vs. fast modes) and practical training guidance (early stopping, capacity scaling), which ICLR values for advancing deep learning practice.
Novelty fit: Reviewers rate novelty 4/5; the identification and scaling of τ_gen/τ_mem and the (n,p,τ) phase diagram offer new perspectives on training overparameterized generative models.

Rigor and presentation: Clear exposition (clarity 5/5), code release, error bars; formal assumptions and proof sketches planned in main text—consistent with ICLR’s standards on clarity and reproducibility.
why_it_does_not_fit: ICLR often prioritizes algorithmic/architectural advances or representation-learning innovations; this work is primarily analytical and diagnostic rather than proposing a new training method or architecture.

Empirical validation remains at modest resolutions and datasets at submission (CelebA 32×32 grayscale), with broader validations (CIFAR-10 color/conditional, 64×64, attention) promised—this can be a higher-risk mismatch for ICLR reviewers.

The theory relies on an idealized RFNN at fixed t; despite a plausible bridge to integrated-over-t U-Net training, ICLR reviewers may demand stronger empirical corroboration across t and architectures.
Reviewer risk: Moderate—the promissory nature of several extensions and the absence of a concrete new method may lead to mixed outcomes at ICLR despite strong clarity and insight.

strengths: Unified symmetry framework (permutation, semi-permutation, orthogonal, invertible) mapped concretely to Transformer components (FFNs, QK/OV circuits, residual stream via RMSNorm), subsuming prior re-basin approaches.

Compelling empirical LMC on Transformers: learned matching attains near-zero barriers on ViT benchmarks (e.g., B ≈ 0.00 ± 0.00 on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny ImageNet) and low barrier on Tiny Shakespeare (≈ 0.02 ± 0.00), with substantial reductions on BookCorpus (≈ 0.42 ± 0.01); multi-model and width-heterogeneous connectivity is demonstrated.

Clear algorithmic instantiations: data-free weight matching and learned matching via projection operators; insightful diagnostics showing residual orthogonal alignment is the main driver (eigen-angles of O_diff concentrated near 0).

Author rebuttal addresses key concerns with concrete additions: RoPE-aware orthogonal subgroup with explicit commutation constraints and a new RoPE experiment; an ImageNet-1k ViT-S/16 study; expanded baselines (OT/Sinkhorn, STE variants, correlation-based) with matched budgets; endpoint-equivalence checks and overhead reporting; systematic endpoint and mid-path performance metrics across λ.
weaknesses: Several crucial additions (RoPE-constrained experiments, larger-scale ViT results, expanded baselines, endpoint/mid-path metrics) are pending; current acceptance hinges on inclusion and quality of these updates.

Reliance on architecture-level reparameterizations (LayerNorm absorption, RMSNorm reformulation, attention circuit canonicalization) remains to be fully quantified in the current version; practical overhead, numerical drift, and implementation constraints must be convincingly demonstrated with the promised diagnostics.

Scalability and compute overhead for larger LMs and vision models are not yet established; it remains to be seen how projection operators and Procrustes steps behave at higher dimensions and with RoPE constraints.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper presents a principled and practical extension of symmetry-aware alignment for Transformers by unifying permutation, semi-permutation, orthogonal, and invertible reparameterizations. The methodology is well grounded in component-wise symmetries (QK/OV invariances, RMSNorm orthogonality) and yields strong empirical evidence of linear mode connectivity for ViTs and small GPT-2 models, including multi-way and width-heterogeneous settings. The rebuttal substantially addresses prior concerns with specific, actionable additions: RoPE-aware formulations and experiments, a larger ViT study, broader Transformer-specific baselines, endpoint-equivalence and overhead quantification, and comprehensive reporting of endpoint and mid-path performance. While these elements are pending and are important to fully validate scalability and practicality, the core contribution is solid and likely to be further strengthened by the planned updates. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: Unified symmetry framework with precise, component-wise mappings (permutation, semi-permutation, orthogonal, invertible) for FFNs, MHA (QK/OV circuits), and residual streams; includes width-heterogeneous alignment via orthogonal symmetry.

Strong empirical LMC results: zero barriers on ViT benchmarks (Table 2 shows 0.00 ± 0.00 across CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny ImageNet), near-zero on Tiny Shakespeare (~0.02), and clear analysis of why BookCorpus remains higher (~0.42); multi-model connectivity surfaces demonstrate shared low-loss manifolds.

Methodology is thorough with complementary data-free weight matching and task-supervised learned matching; ablations on interpolation-coefficient sampling and initialization; analysis pinpoints residual orthogonal map O as the key driver of the gap between methods (eigen-angle analysis).

Author rebuttal substantively strengthens external validity and rigor: RoPE-aware subgroup characterization (commutation with rotary blocks) and a constrained projection operator (block-diagonal 2×2 Procrustes), planned RoPE experiments on small open LMs, and scaling to ImageNet-1k ViT-S/16 with matched reporting (barriers, endpoint and mid-path accuracy, compute).

Additional diagnostics and deployment guidance: endpoint equivalence metrics (max/mean logit deviation, relative L2), complexity and stability reporting for projections/matching, formal exact vs. soft symmetry boundaries (semi-permutation, inter-head mixing), sensitivity analyses and defaults for learned matching, unlabeled consistency/distillation alternatives, improved data-free O estimation, and a federated workflow relying primarily on WM.
weaknesses: Despite planned additions, external validity to large, contemporary RoPE-based LLMs remains open; proposed RoPE experiments are on smaller models and the ImageNet evaluation is at ViT-S/16 scale.

Applicability still hinges on architecture-level reparameterizations (e.g., LayerNorm absorption, attention circuit canonicalization) and subgroup constraints for RoPE; practical deployment at scale may face numerical/stability sensitivities and engineering overhead.

Learned matching requires task or proxy data and some hyperparameter sensitivity (e.g., λ sampling), and although WM is a strong data-free baseline, the full gains rely on this additional training step.
reviewers.summary_comment: This work offers a well-justified generalization of symmetry-aware alignment for Transformers, extending beyond permutations to semi-permutation, orthogonal, and invertible transformations with clear component-level derivations. Empirically, the paper demonstrates compelling LMC: zero barriers on multiple ViT tasks, near-zero on Tiny Shakespeare, and a thoughtful discussion of the remaining barrier on BookCorpus. The analysis convincingly identifies orthogonal alignment of the residual stream as the main factor bridging weight- and learned-matching performance. The rebuttal addresses key concerns by adding RoPE-aware subgroup projections (with explicit commutation conditions and a numerically stable blockwise Procrustes operator), planning RoPE experiments on small open LMs, scaling vision evaluations to ImageNet-1k ViT-S/16, reporting endpoint-equivalence diagnostics and compute, and clarifying exact vs. soft symmetry boundaries. Practical guidance for data-scarce/federated settings and improved data-free O estimation further increases utility. While external validity to very large LLMs remains to be shown and some engineering is required for standard LayerNorm/RoPE stacks, the contributions are sound, significant for understanding Transformer loss landscape geometry, and practically actionable. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: The rebuttal provides concrete, technically precise extensions: a RoPE-aware orthogonal subgroup with an explicit PROJORTH_ROPE operator and a controlled RoPE experiment on small/medium open LMs to validate constraints.

Baseline coverage will be broadened under matched budgets and splits to include Transformer OT fusion, Sinkhorn Re-Basin, STE-based Git Re-Basin (with WM init), REPAIR, and correlation-based matching, improving fairness and context.

Semi-permutation is formalized and operationalized beyond soft relaxations: exact FFN semi-permutation under positively 1-homogeneous activations and sparse column-stochastic maps, plus inter-head OV mixing with clear exactness boundaries and head-similarity-driven ablations.

The authors commit to moving key derivations into the main text and adding endpoint-equivalence diagnostics, as well as reporting compute/memory overhead and scalable projections (randomized/batched SVD, RoPE blockwise Procrustes).

Deeper analysis of the persistent BookCorpus barrier is planned (similarity spectra, residual subspace alignment, clustering by generalization strategy, and brief post-alignment fine-tuning), clarifying whether gaps reflect misalignment or genuinely distinct minima.
weaknesses: Many critical enhancements (RoPE-aware experiments, ImageNet-1k ViT-S/16 evaluation, expanded baselines, exact semi-permutation and OV mixing benefits) are commitments for the camera-ready and not yet empirically demonstrated; scalability and comparative advantages remain to be verified.

The practical benefits of semi-permutation and inter-head OV mixing versus simpler head permutations and orthogonal residual alignment may be modest and task-dependent; their net impact is unclear until the new experiments are reported.

Reliance on reparameterizations (LayerNorm handling, attention circuit canonicalization) and projections (SVD/Procrustes) could pose overheads at larger scales; although mitigations are proposed, concrete large-scale cost profiles and robustness remain to be shown.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper’s core contributions—a unified symmetry framework for Transformers (permutation, semi-permutation, orthogonal, invertible) and strong empirical evidence of low/zero linear barriers for ViTs and GPT-2, including multi-model and width-heterogeneous cases—are compelling. The rebuttal directly addresses key concerns with precise, actionable additions: a RoPE-aware orthogonal subgroup and projection with planned experiments, expanded baselines under matched budgets, exact semi-permutation formalization and targeted tests, endpoint-equivalence diagnostics, and a thorough analysis of BookCorpus barriers and compute overhead. While these are mostly promised improvements, the plan is technically sound and squarely targets the gaps identified. Given the solid existing results and the detailed commitments that strengthen scalability, rigor, and fairness of evaluation, I maintain an accept recommendation.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: The work targets core ML questions about loss landscape geometry, symmetry groups, and optimization, aligning with ICML areas in general ML, theory, optimization, and ML systems. It analyzes Transformers across vision and language, matching ICML’s interest in application-driven ML.

Contribution fit: A unified symmetry framework (permutation, semi-permutation, orthogonal, invertible) with concrete mappings to Transformer components (FFN, QK/OV circuits, residual stream) and practical algorithms (data-free weight matching, learned matching via projection operators). Reviewers praised the principled formalization and practicality.

Novelty fit: Reviewers rated novelty positively (4/5) and highlighted first demonstrations of (near-)zero linear barriers for ViTs and small GPT-2, plus multi-model and width-heterogeneous connectivity—innovations that extend beyond permutation-only re-basing.

Rigor fit: Reviewers scored soundness and evaluation highly (4–5/5), citing thorough ablations (e.g., λ sampling, initialization), diagnostics (eigen-angle analysis of the residual orthogonal map), and planned broader baselines under matched budgets—consistent with ICML’s emphasis on careful methodology.
Presentation fit: Clarity was rated strongly (4–5/5), and the paper includes code and detailed experimental settings, matching ICML’s standards for reproducibility and clear framing.
why_it_does_not_fit: Potential mismatch in theoretical depth: While the framework is principled and includes formal component-wise symmetry derivations, the work is primarily empirical; ICML reviewers sometimes expect stronger formal guarantees or broader theoretical development.

Scale and practicality concerns in reviews: Current experiments focus on ViTs for CIFAR/Tiny ImageNet and small GPT-2 models; reviewers note pending larger-scale validations (e.g., ImageNet-1k ViT-S/16, RoPE-aware LLMs) and the need to quantify overhead and numerical stability—risking perceived external validity at ICML.

Reliance on architectural reparameterizations: Rewriting LayerNorm via RMSNorm and attention circuit canonicalization may be viewed as engineering-heavy; reviewers asked for clearer overhead and endpoint-equivalence diagnostics, which, if not fully delivered, increase risk.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Loss landscapes, mode connectivity, and symmetry-aware alignment are classic NeurIPS themes (deep learning, optimization, theory, data-centric AI). The interdisciplinary coverage (vision and language) and analysis-driven contribution closely match the conference profile.

Contribution fit: Reviewers highlight a unified symmetry framework concretely instantiated for Transformer components, plus pragmatic algorithms (Procrustes-based orthogonal alignment, head assignments, learned matching with projection operators) demonstrating zero/near-zero barriers, multi-model connectivity, and width-heterogeneous alignment—typical of NeurIPS’s blend of conceptual insight and empirical validation.

Novelty fit: Strong novelty signal in reviews (4/5), especially the first-time near-zero linear paths for ViTs and small GPT-2 and the clear identification of the residual orthogonal map as the key driver. NeurIPS explicitly welcomes analyses that give new insights into behavior/limitations of existing methods.

Rigor fit: High soundness/evaluation scores (4–5/5) and thorough ablations. Reviewers note planned additions (RoPE-aware subgroup with blockwise Procrustes, expanded baselines, ImageNet-1k ViT-S/16), which further strengthen rigor and external validity—well aligned with NeurIPS expectations.

Presentation fit and community precedent: Clarity rated highly; detailed reporting and code availability. Prior mode connectivity and re-basin works have strong NeurIPS precedent, increasing likelihood of a positive reception.
why_it_does_not_fit: Pending additions noted by reviewers: Acceptance partly hinges on including new RoPE-aware experiments, larger-scale ViT evaluations, and expanded baselines; if these land weaker than promised, this could be scrutinized.

Scalability and overhead: Reviewers flagged open questions about compute/memory for projection operators at higher dimensions and engineering overhead with RoPE/LN stacks; while addressed conceptually, large-LLM validation remains pending.

Data requirements for learned matching: Although a strong data-free baseline exists (weight matching), the best results use task- or proxy-data, introducing some deployment sensitivity that NeurIPS reviewers may probe.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Deep learning-centric focus on Transformers, representation geometry, and optimization dynamics aligns with ICLR’s scope (representation learning, optimization, theory for deep nets).

Contribution fit: The paper offers a unified deep-learning symmetry perspective with practical alignment procedures and end-to-end learned projections—methods ICLR often features (e.g., analyses that expose deep net behavior).

Novelty and insight: Reviewers endorse the novelty (4/5) and the insight that orthogonal residual alignment dominates the gap between data-free and learned approaches. The framework extends representation alignment beyond permutations (semi-permutation, orthogonal, invertible), which is of interest to the ICLR community.
Rigor/presentation: Soundness and evaluation are strong; the work provides ablations, diagnostics, and code, which ICLR values for empirical DL studies.
why_it_does_not_fit: Emphasis on landscape geometry over representation learning improvements: ICLR often prioritizes advances that improve learning performance or representations. Here, the primary contribution is understanding and enabling low-barrier interpolation rather than boosting task metrics.

Scale and external validity: Reviewers point out that evaluations are on smaller GPT-2 and mid-scale ViTs, with larger/modern RoPE LLMs still pending; ICLR reviewers may push harder for contemporary, large-scale deep learning evidence.

Engineering prerequisites: Reparameterizations (e.g., RMSNorm reformulations, circuit canonicalization) and projection steps may be seen as non-trivial to deploy at scale; learned matching’s data/hyperparameter sensitivity adds risk for ICLR’s standards of practical deep learning rigor.

strengths: Integrated, controlled, end-to-end study across pre-training, CPT, SFT, and RL with final checkpoints and consistent pipelines, yielding stage-interaction insights rather than phase-isolated observations; e.g., Table 1 quantifies when larger models “unlock” gains (4B at 160BT SFT Maj@16 = 26.4% vs. 14.2% for 1B; with RL 34.8% vs. 22.5%).

Actionable, quantified guidance: optimal modest replay during CPT (Table 2: GSM8K-Platinum Pass@1 = 21.01% for 8BT FineWeb + 42BT FineMath vs. 19.27% pure FineMath), delineated SFT epoch/data scaling trade-offs (Figure 6–7), and RL saturation plus Pass@16 collapse tied to response-length growth (Figure 8; Appendix B.2, Figures 11–13).

Robust evaluation design across upstream cloze tasks and downstream generative tasks, covering in-domain and multiple OOD benchmarks with diverse decoding schemes (greedy, Maj@16, RM@16, Pass@16) and standard errors over multiple seeds (Appendix C.3).

Methodological clarity and diagnostics: intermediate checkpoints understate capability compared to fully trained smaller runs (Section 4.1; Table 3), and ORM scores correlate far better with downstream accuracy than validation perplexity for post-trained models (Section 4.2; Figure 10; Appendix B.3, Figure 14).

Strong open-science contribution: 100+ models (1B/4B) trained from scratch, datasets for all stages, and full training/evaluation pipelines. The rebuttal further commits to a 4B replay sweep, PPO ablations (KL/clipping, length penalties), a 1B DPO/IPO baseline, added OOD benchmarks (e.g., MBPP, CommonsenseQA), style-bias diagnostics, and improved artifacts/formatting.
weaknesses: Novelty remains moderate: many core observations (e.g., over-training can hinder fine-tuning; replay mitigates forgetting; RL boosts confidence more than core reasoning) align with recent literature, though the unified and quantified treatment is valuable.

Scope remains limited to small models (1B/4B) and math-centric CPT/SFT/RL; generalization to larger models, alternative CPT domains (e.g., code or multi-domain), and different RL/preference-learning methods or reward models is not yet empirically established (some analyses are promised for the camera-ready).

RL coverage is still centered on PPO with a binary verifiable reward; while the authors plan KL/clipping/length-penalty ablations and a DPO/IPO baseline, results with diverse RL/preference-learning paradigms and reward models are not yet presented, leaving some uncertainty around the robustness of the “confidence-not-reasoning” conclusion.

Practical replication may be compute-intensive for many labs (up to 32 H100 80GB GPUs reported in Section C), and there is residual risk of stylistic bias or contamination from teacher-generated SFT data; the planned style-bias diagnostics and prompt-robustness checks will help but are pending.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal meaningfully strengthens the paper’s positioning and addresses key concerns. It clarifies that the main contribution is a controlled, from-scratch, multi-stage quantification of training-stage interactions with final checkpoints and consistent pipelines, accompanied by actionable guidance. The authors commit to concrete additions—expanded CPT replay methodology including a 4B sweep, broader RL analysis (KL/clipping, length penalties) plus a 1B DPO/IPO baseline, extra OOD benchmarks, explicit length/truncation reporting to explain the Pass@16 collapse, style-bias diagnostics, and clearer separation of ORM usage—that directly target prior weaknesses. Core findings remain well supported: pre-training beyond ~80–160x model size yields diminishing gains and can hinder post-training; modest replay (~5%) best balances retention and adaptation; SFT over-specialization can erode OOD generalization; RL predominantly increases confidence and shows saturation; ORM correlates with downstream accuracy while validation perplexity does not for post-trained models. While novelty is moderate and scope is limited to 1B/4B math-centric settings with PPO-based RL, the study’s breadth, rigor, transparency, and open releases make it a valuable resource. I maintain my recommendation to accept.

strengths: Comprehensive end-to-end analysis across pre-training, CPT, SFT, and RL with 100+ controlled 1B/4B models trained from scratch and full LR decay; clear, quantitative takeaways: pre-training saturation around ~80–160x model size (Section 3.1), the benefit of modest CPT replay (~5%) with GSM8K-Platinum Pass@1 = 21.01% for 8BT FineWeb + 42BT FineMath versus 19.27% for 50BT FineMath (Table 2), and the unlocking of 4B performance at 160BT (SFT Maj@16 = 26.4% vs 14.2% for 1B; SFT+RL Maj@16 = 34.8% vs 22.5%; Table 1).

Well-designed evaluation protocol with multiple decoding schemes (Greedy, Maj@16, RM@16, Pass@16) and reported variability (Section C.3). The paper isolates meaningful phenomena: intermediate checkpoints underperform fully completed short runs (e.g., at 20BT, MATH L1 Pass@16 = 17.85 full vs 11.44 intermediate; Table 3); RL gains saturate around 8–16 epochs and ~150–200K examples with Pass@16 degradation beyond ~4 epochs due to response-length growth (Figures 8, 11–13).

Transparent and reproducible setup: open release of models, data, and pipelines with detailed hyperparameters (Sections 2, C). Clear diagnostic insights such as ORM scores correlating with downstream accuracy better than perplexity post-training (Section 4.2).

Author rebuttal offers concrete, targeted additions: a full contamination audit with exact/fuzzy matching and decontaminated re-evaluations; expanded PPO ablations (KL, clip ranges, length penalties) with response-length/truncation reporting; inclusion of a 1B-scale DPO/IPO preference-learning baseline; ORM robustness checks with an additional ORM and an LM-as-a-judge heuristic; broader inference-only OOD evaluations (e.g., MBPP, CommonsenseQA); and cleaned figures/tables with released data artifacts.
weaknesses: Generalization beyond small (1B/4B) models and math-centric post-training remains uncertain. While the paper includes several OOD tasks and plans additional inference-only OOD evaluations, it does not yet test larger parameter scales or post-training in other domains (e.g., safety/tool-use, instruction-following, code-focused CPT).

Several remedies are prospective: contamination audits, PPO ablations with explicit length control, DPO/IPO baselines, and ORM robustness checks are promised for the camera-ready but not yet executed; conclusions about data leakage, RL method breadth, and ORM dependence will be more convincing once these results are integrated.

RL methodology breadth remains relatively narrow. Even with the planned DPO/IPO baseline at 1B, it is unclear whether alternative RL/preference-learning variants (e.g., GRPO, off-policy approaches, richer reward formulations) would alter the observed confidence-versus-coverage trade-offs.

Minor presentation/practicality: some figure/table artifacts in the submission hinder readability; additionally, while hardware is specified (up to 32 H100 80GB), wall-clock training times per major setting are not reported, which would help practitioners assess replication cost.
reviewers.summary_comment: This paper delivers a rigorous and transparent study of LM training dynamics across pre-training, CPT, SFT, and RL for 1B/4B models, yielding clear, quantitative guidance: pre-training gains saturate around ~80–160x model size (Section 3.1), modest CPT replay (~5%) mitigates forgetting and improves downstream math (GSM8K-P Pass@1 21.01% vs 19.27%; Table 2), larger models ‘unlock’ at higher token budgets (4B at 160BT shows SFT Maj@16 26.4% vs 14.2% for 1B; Table 1), and RL mainly boosts confidence (Maj@16/RM@16) with saturation around 8–16 epochs and ~150–200K examples, while Pass@16 can degrade beyond ~4 epochs due to length growth (Figures 8, 11–13). The demonstration that intermediate checkpoints underperform complete short runs (Table 3) and that ORM scores correlate with downstream accuracy better than perplexity (Section 4.2) are especially useful diagnostics. The authors respond constructively with detailed commitments: comprehensive contamination audits with decontaminated re-evaluations, expanded PPO ablations with explicit length control, a 1B-scale DPO/IPO baseline, ORM robustness checks, additional OOD evaluations, and cleaned figures/tables with released data artifacts. While these additions are not yet executed and the scope remains centered on smaller models and math-centric post-training, the present work is already valuable and reproducible. I maintain an accept recommendation, with the expectation that the promised audits and baselines will be included to strengthen soundness and evaluation breadth.

strengths: The rebuttal directly addresses prior concerns with concrete, camera-ready additions: a comprehensive contamination/overlap audit with decontaminated re-evaluations and released scripts; PPO ablations (KL, clipping) and length-control analyses; and a 1B-scale DPO/IPO baseline to probe confidence-versus-coverage trade-offs.

Strong, quantified stage-wise insights with final checkpoints: e.g., at 160BT pretraining, 4B SFT Maj@16 = 26.4% (SFT) and 34.8% (SFT+RL) versus 1B’s 14.2% and 22.5% (Table 1); CPT replay around ~5% (8BT FineWeb + 42BT FineMath) lifts GSM8K-Platinum Pass@1 to 21.01% over pure math CPT (19.27%) (Table 2).

Nuanced SFT/RL scaling characterizations and failure-mode analysis: SFT epochs saturate for ID around ~8 and harm OOD beyond ~2–4; RL peaks around 8–16 epochs and ~150–200K examples for Greedy/Maj@16/RM@16, with Pass@16 degradation traced to response-length growth and truncation (Figures 8, 11–13).

Useful diagnostics for practitioners: intermediate checkpoints underperform matched full runs (e.g., 40BT full vs intermediate on MATH L1 Greedy 2.97 vs 1.37; Pass@16 17.96 vs 9.38), and ORM scores correlate well with downstream accuracy across tasks while validation perplexity does not, offering a reliable validation proxy.

Clarity and reproducibility are strong and further improved by rebuttal commitments: cleaned figures/tables with released data artifacts; broader inference-only OOD evaluations (e.g., MBPP, CommonsenseQA); explicit separation of RM@16 from accuracy metrics; and robust ORM checks using an additional reward model and an LM-as-a-judge heuristic.
weaknesses: Empirical scale remains limited to 1B/4B; while a new Scope and Generalizability section is planned, the transfer of precise saturation points and trade-offs to ≥7B–13B models remains unverified within the submission.

Several key enhancements (contamination audit results, PPO ablations with length controls, and DPO/IPO baselines) are promised for the camera-ready but are not yet included, so some conclusions about robustness and method breadth are prospective.

Post-training is math-centric; despite broader OOD inference and planned additional evaluations, there are no CPT/SFT/RL experiments in non-math domains yet to validate replay optima and SFT/RL trade-offs under domain shifts.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors respond thoroughly and constructively, committing to contamination audits with decontaminated re-evaluations, expanded RL ablations and a preference-learning baseline, ORM robustness checks, and broader inference-only OOD evaluations. The current paper already provides a controlled, end-to-end study with practical, quantified findings: when larger models ‘unlock’ gains given sufficient pretraining, modest CPT replay (~5%) to balance retention and adaptation, SFT and RL scaling regimes and a clear Pass@16 failure mode due to long responses, the unreliability of intermediate checkpoints, and ORM’s predictive value versus validation perplexity. Remaining limitations concern empirical scale, domain focus, and that some robustness analyses are slated for the camera-ready. Given the rigor, transparency, open releases, and the planned additions that directly address prior concerns, I maintain an acceptance recommendation.

why_it_fits: Strong topic alignment with ICML’s evaluation/meta-studies and ML systems interests: the paper provides a controlled, end-to-end empirical analysis of training dynamics across pre-training, CPT, SFT, and RL, with open models, data, and pipelines that directly support reproducibility and methodology-focused contributions.

Contribution type matches ICML’s appetite for rigorous empirical insights and practical guidance: quantified stage-interaction effects (e.g., 4B ‘unlocking’ at 160BT with SFT Maj@16 26.4% vs 14.2% for 1B; SFT+RL 34.8% vs 22.5%), optimal CPT replay (~5%) improving GSM8K-P Pass@1 to 21.01% vs 19.27%, and clear SFT/RL scaling regimes and failure modes.

Rigor and presentation are praised by reviewers: controlled from-scratch training with complete LR decay, multiple decoding schemes (Greedy, Maj@16, RM@16, Pass@16), standard errors over seeds, and clear diagnostics (intermediate checkpoints underperform full runs; ORM scores correlate better with downstream accuracy than perplexity).

ICML’s scope explicitly includes deep learning, optimization, reinforcement learning, trustworthy ML, and application-driven ML; this work engages all of these via scaling laws, forgetting mitigation, RL post-training behavior, and ID/OOD reasoning evaluations.
why_it_does_not_fit: Reviewers note moderate novelty: several conclusions (e.g., over-training can hinder fine-tuning; replay mitigates forgetting; RL boosts confidence more than core reasoning) are consistent with recent literature, which could be viewed as incremental for ICML.

Scope is limited to 1B/4B models and math-centric CPT/SFT/RL; generalization to larger models or other domains (code, safety/tool use) and broader RL/preference-learning methods is not empirically established, which may narrow the paper’s appeal to ICML’s broad audience.

Some analyses (e.g., PPO ablations, contamination audits, DPO/IPO baselines) are promised for the camera-ready but not yet integrated, posing reviewer-risk at ICML where completeness and breadth often matter.

why_it_fits: Excellent topical fit: NeurIPS explicitly encourages in-depth analyses of existing methods and interdisciplinary studies; this paper delivers a comprehensive, controlled analysis across LM training stages with actionable insights and open assets.

Contribution type aligns with NeurIPS tracks in SysML infrastructure, data-centric AI, optimization, RL, and general ML: 100+ from-scratch 1B/4B models, clear scaling/interaction findings (e.g., pre-training saturation ~80–160x model size; CPT replay ~5% best trade-off; RL saturation and Pass@16 collapse due to response-length growth), and diagnostics (ORM vs perplexity; intermediate checkpoint caveats).

Rigor and reproducibility are strong and explicitly valued by NeurIPS: multiple decoding/eval protocols with standard errors, open pipelines, and detailed hyperparameters; reviewers are positive on clarity and soundness and maintain accept recommendations.
Even with moderate novelty, NeurIPS’s stated call welcomes precisely this kind of in-depth empirical investigation that yields new understanding and practical guidance for the community.
why_it_does_not_fit: Empirical scope remains centered on small models (1B/4B) and math-centric post-training; broader domains and larger scales are not yet tested, which could limit generality for some NeurIPS audiences.

RL coverage focuses on PPO with a binary verifiable reward; despite planned DPO/IPO and PPO ablations, breadth across RL/preference-learning paradigms and reward models is still limited in the current version.
Replication may be compute-intensive (up to 32 H100s), and some promised additions (contamination audits, RL ablations, ORM robustness) are slated for camera-ready, introducing minor reviewer risk.

why_it_fits: Reasonable topical overlap: ICLR lists deep learning, optimization, reinforcement learning, datasets/benchmarks, and infrastructure; the paper studies training dynamics across these areas and releases models/data/pipelines.

Empirical insights into representation-related generalization (ID vs OOD) and post-training behavior (e.g., SFT over-specialization harming OOD, RL mainly improving confidence) can appeal to ICLR’s interest in understanding learned representations and training.
Strong rigor and clarity noted by reviewers (final checkpoints, controlled runs, standard errors, open artifacts) support ICLR’s emphasis on reproducibility.
why_it_does_not_fit: ICLR frequently prioritizes novel representation learning methods or theoretical/conceptual advances; reviewers describe the novelty as moderate and largely confirmatory of recent findings, which may be seen as insufficiently novel for ICLR’s expectations.

The work is primarily an empirical systems/meta-study without a new learning algorithm or theory; the focus on 1B/4B math-centric post-training and PPO-based RL further narrows the perceived contribution for ICLR’s core audience.

Several robustness expansions (e.g., preference-learning baselines, broader RL variants, audits) are promised but not yet included, adding risk for ICLR where completeness and conceptual novelty are often critical.

strengths: The authors provide concrete, targeted revisions: corrected smoothness-based derivation, corrected continuous-time solution, and a reframed LR-ratio result with explicit assumptions and a formal step-size balancing objective on a bilinear surrogate.

They commit to key empirical upgrades with multi-seed/error bars for main results, inclusion of decisive baselines (Grokfast and fixed Fourier features), and ablations (frozen vs. trainable embeddings), which directly test the central claims.
They will add a compact attention-only Transformer study with blockwise Hessian tracking to probe the claimed mechanism’s transfer beyond MLPs, and narrow their broader claims accordingly.

Planned improvements to clarity and reproducibility are substantial: unified notation, fixed citations/figures, clearer captions/axes, compute details, and anonymized code/scripts with exact configs and seeds.
weaknesses: Most improvements remain prospective; the current submission still lacks the promised multi-seed statistics, baseline comparisons, and Transformer validation, so central claims are not yet sufficiently evidenced at review time.

Even with the reframing, the theory is local/approximate (linearization, block-diagonal Hessian assumptions) and primarily motivates a heuristic scaling rule; its value hinges on the expanded empirical validation not yet included.

Generality beyond modular arithmetic remains uncertain; the planned Transformer study is small-scale, and additional baselines (e.g., weight-decay schedules or combinations like Grokfast+Adam-LR) may be necessary to contextualize gains.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal is thoughtful and specific, correcting mathematical issues, clarifying assumptions, and narrowing claims. The proposed empirical extensions (multi-seed/error bars, Grokfast and Fourier baselines, embedding ablations, and a small Transformer study) and reproducibility/clarity fixes would materially strengthen the paper and better support the LR scaling rule derived under local bilinear assumptions. However, these changes are not yet reflected in the submission, and thus the evidence remains insufficient for acceptance at this time. If the authors implement the promised revisions with rigorous statistical reporting and fair, matched-budget comparisons, this work could become a solid and clearer contribution in a future revision.

strengths: The rebuttal directly addresses key theoretical issues with concrete corrections and clarifies assumptions, reframing the learning-rate ratio as a principled step-size balancing heuristic rather than a convergence guarantee.

The paper offers a coherent mechanistic account linking grokking to sparse embedding updates (via token probabilities p_i) and bilinear coupling, supported by diagnostics such as gradient heatmaps, blockwise Hessian spectra, and Fourier analyses.

The proposed intervention—embedding-specific learning rates—remains simple and practical, and the planned ablations (global vs embedding-only LR scaling; layer-wise scaling; frozen/small-init embeddings) would help isolate the mechanism.

The authors commit to substantially broaden the empirical evaluation (multi-seed/error bars, per-token reweighting/sampling, gradient clipping, decoupled regularization, Fourier-feature baselines) and to temper claims with a small Transformer check.
Clear commitments to improve presentation and reproducibility (fixing formatting/numbering, consistent notation, releasing anonymized code/scripts, and reporting compute details).
weaknesses: Most improvements are prospective: the current manuscript still contains mathematical and presentation issues, and the corrected derivations, tempered claims, and proof structure are not yet reflected.

Empirical evidence remains narrow in the present submission (small MLPs, primarily Adam vs. Adam-LR) without multi-seed statistics, error bars, or comparisons to alternative mitigations; the promised baselines and robustness analyses are not yet included.

Claims about broader implications for Transformers remain speculative in the current version; novelty is moderate given prior practice of differential LRs for embeddings and known bilinear coupling effects, and the LR ratio remains a heuristic tuned empirically.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal is constructive and addresses the core concerns by correcting theoretical derivations, clarifying assumptions, and positioning the LR ratio as a balancing heuristic. It also promises a substantially stronger empirical study with multi-seed statistics and relevant baselines, and more cautious claims with an initial Transformer check. However, these changes are not yet present in the submission. As it stands, the paper’s evaluation is limited, some derivations and presentation remain problematic, and the broader claims are still speculative. I recommend rejection at this time, while noting that a revised version incorporating the promised corrections, expanded experiments, and clearer scope could become competitive.

strengths: The paper presents a coherent mechanistic account of grokking centered on embedding update sparsity and bilinear coupling with downstream weights, along with practical interventions (frequency-aware sampling and embedding-specific learning-rate scaling).

The authors’ rebuttal improves theoretical rigor by clarifying the smoothness-based descent argument, correcting the continuous-time approximation, and reframing the LR-balancing rule under explicit local assumptions.

A concrete adaptive sampling algorithm with safeguards (EMA with shrinkage, probability floors, capped importance weights, stratified batching) is proposed, directly addressing concerns about variance and rare-token estimation.

The authors commit to stronger empirical evidence: multi-seed results with CIs, compute/time reporting, code release, added baselines (e.g., Grokfast, Fourier features, LARS/LAMB, Adafactor/Adagrad, freeze–thaw), and a compact Transformer validation with blockwise Hessian diagnostics.
weaknesses: At submission time, the manuscript still lacks the promised additions (multi-seed/error bars, stronger baselines, compute/time reporting, and Transformer experiments), so broader claims remain unsubstantiated beyond small MLPs on synthetic tasks.

Even with clarified assumptions, the theoretical results remain heuristic for grokking-specific behavior (no convergence or generalization guarantees; the LR-ratio balances effective step sizes but does not yield conditions or rates for delayed generalization).
The practicality of the adaptive sampling method (estimation stability for rare tokens, importance-weight variance, and wall-clock overhead) is not yet validated empirically in the current paper.
reviewers.summary_comment: The work advances a plausible and well-motivated perspective that embedding dynamics and bilinear coupling underlie grokking, and it proposes simple interventions that appear effective in the presented settings. The rebuttal substantially strengthens the paper in principle—clarifying derivations and assumptions, detailing an adaptive sampling algorithm with variance controls, narrowing claims, and committing to robust baselines, statistics, compute reporting, and a small Transformer validation. However, these improvements are not yet reflected in the submitted manuscript, and the broader implications remain unverified empirically. I appreciate the thoughtful responses and believe the paper could become competitive with the promised additions and thorough empirical validation, but in its current form I recommend rejection.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: The work studies optimization dynamics and generalization in neural networks (grokking, embeddings, bilinear coupling), which aligns with ICML areas like optimization, deep learning theory, and evaluation/replicability.

Contribution type: A mechanistic analysis plus practical interventions (frequency-aware sampling and embedding-specific learning rates) matches ICML interest in principled improvements to training and generalization.
Community relevance: Understanding grokking and embedding dynamics is of interest to a broad ML audience given widespread use of embeddings in modern models.
why_it_does_not_fit: Rigor/empirical breadth: Reviewers noted the current submission lacks multi-seed statistics, error bars, decisive baselines (e.g., Grokfast, Fourier features), and broader validation beyond small MLPs on synthetic tasks; ICML typically expects stronger empirical evidence and comparison suites.

Theory positioning: Theoretical support is local/approximate (linearization, block-diagonal Hessian assumptions) and primarily motivates a heuristic LR-scaling rule; reviewers flagged that its current value depends on expanded validation not yet included, which weakens ICML’s theory/rigor expectations.

Generality and significance: Claims about transfer to Transformers are largely prospective; the present evidence is limited to modular arithmetic. ICML generally favors contributions with demonstrated breadth or impactful applications.

Presentation and reproducibility: Reviewers cited unresolved clarity and formatting issues in the current version and that many improvements are only promised; ICML places high weight on polished, fully evidenced submissions.
Reviewer risk: All reviewers recommended reject due to insufficient current evidence, making ICML submission high risk despite topic alignment.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: NeurIPS welcomes analysis of existing methods that provide new insights; the mechanistic account linking grokking to sparse embedding updates and bilinear coupling fits tracks like optimization, deep learning, and data-centric aspects of AI.

Contribution type: The paper combines diagnostics (gradient heatmaps, blockwise Hessian spectra, Fourier analyses) with a simple, practical intervention (embedding-specific LR), aligning with NeurIPS’s appetite for insight-driven heuristics with empirical support.
Community interest: Grokking and interpretability/diagnostics have been active themes at NeurIPS; embedding dynamics and bilinear effects in attention are broadly relevant.
why_it_does_not_fit: Empirical evaluation gaps: Reviewers emphasized missing multi-seed/error bars, stronger baselines (e.g., Grokfast, Fourier features, alternative optimizers), and robustness/compute reporting; NeurIPS typically expects these for acceptance.

Novelty positioning: Differential learning rates for embeddings and bilinear coupling effects are known; the LR ratio is framed as a heuristic tuned empirically. Reviewers judged novelty as moderate, increasing bar for rigor and breadth.
Scope and claims: Current evidence centers on small MLPs with synthetic tasks; claims about Transformers remain speculative pending a small, promised but not yet included study.
Presentation: Reviewers reported remaining mathematical and presentation issues in the submitted version despite planned fixes; this weakens NeurIPS presentation/reproducibility expectations.
Reviewer risk: Uniform reject recommendations citing insufficient current evidence make the submission risky at NeurIPS without the promised expansions.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: ICLR emphasizes deep learning, representation learning, optimization, and analyses of learned representations; the focus on embedding dynamics, bilinear coupling, and grokking aligns strongly.

Contribution type: Mechanistic insights into representation/optimization plus a simple optimizer variant (embedding-specific LR) match ICLR’s tradition of accepting phenomenon-centric deep learning studies when well supported.

Precedent: Prior grokking and representation-dynamics works have appeared at ICLR; the discrete Fourier analysis of embeddings and ablations (frozen vs. trainable embeddings) fit ICLR’s experimental style.
why_it_does_not_fit: Rigor/empirical support: As reviewers note, the current submission lacks multi-seed/error bars, compute/time reporting, and comparisons to key baselines; ICLR typically expects robust, well-controlled empirical studies even for phenomenon analyses.

Theory scope: The LR-balancing rule is supported by local/heuristic assumptions and does not provide convergence or generalization guarantees for grokking; reviewers flagged that the theoretical value hinges on empirical validation not yet present.

Generality: Evidence is limited to algorithmic datasets in MLPs; transfer to Transformers is only proposed as a small, future addition, which leaves broader claims under-substantiated for ICLR standards.
Presentation: Reviewers identified clarity and formatting issues and that many improvements are prospective, which may fall short of ICLR’s expectations for a polished submission at review time.
Reviewer risk: All reviewers recommended rejection due to incomplete evidence and presentation, so despite strong topical fit, the current likelihood of success is only borderline.

strengths: The authors now replace the flawed masked-DFT decoder with a principled FFT-based causal convolution (overlap-save), including a streaming decoding variant and clear complexity claims.
Cross-mixing is reformulated as an FFT-based cross-correlation with explicit tensor shapes and padding/cropping rules, resolving earlier dimensional inconsistencies.

They provide a principled treatment of kernel length and receptive field via dilations, including formulas for receptive field growth with depth and discussion of expressivity and frequency resolution.
A causal-friendly parameterization for learned spectral emphasis (time-domain causal filters with optional minimum-phase shaping) is proposed to reconcile learning with strict causality.
The evaluation plan is meaningfully expanded to include strong long-sequence baselines (S4/S5, Hyena, RetNet, Mamba/RWKV), multi-seed confidence intervals, and detailed runtime/memory metrics.
weaknesses: The proposed fixes and expanded experiments are not yet reflected in the manuscript; crucial empirical evidence (runtime/memory benchmarks, multi-seed statistics, new baselines) remains promised rather than demonstrated.

Key design choices (e.g., kernel length L and dilation schedules) and their trade-offs with accuracy, latency, and receptive field are not validated with current results, leaving practical effectiveness unsubstantiated.

While the encoder retains global Fourier mixing, the decoder’s reliance on finite-length causal convolutions raises open questions about preserving global dependencies without the new ablations and analyses.
Positioning against recent state-of-the-art long-sequence models is still absent in the current version, preventing a clear assessment of significance.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal substantively improves the method’s soundness by replacing the unsound masked-DFT with FFT-accelerated causal convolution and by clarifying encoder–decoder cross-mixing via spectral cross-correlation. It also offers a more rigorous design rationale for kernel length and receptive field through dilations and proposes a causal-friendly approach to learned spectral emphasis. Furthermore, the authors commit to adding stronger baselines, multi-seed confidence intervals, and runtime/memory benchmarks. However, these changes are substantial and not present in the current manuscript, and the key empirical evidence needed to support the claims is still missing. Given the lack of updated algorithms, analyses, and results at review time, I recommend rejection, with the view that the promised revisions could make for a strong resubmission.

strengths: Provides concrete, rigorous details for a causal decoder via FFT-accelerated causal convolution (overlap-save with streaming), including complexity and buffer analyses and a receptive-field/dilation discussion.

Reformulates encoder–decoder cross-mixing as an FFT-based linear convolution with explicit padding/cropping indices and tensor shapes, addressing prior dimensional inconsistencies and clarifying complexity.
Clarifies the wavelet branch using a multilevel biorthogonal 5/3 lifting scheme with one-sided causal boundaries and O(N) filtering, improving theoretical soundness and implementability.

Commits to comprehensive empirical updates: stronger long-sequence baselines (e.g., LongT5, RetNet, Hyena/S4, Mamba, BigBird), efficiency metrics (throughput, memory, latency) across 2k–8k contexts, and multi-seed results with confidence intervals.
Plans substantial presentation fixes (pseudocode, tensor-shape tables, corrected figures/notation/sectioning) that should improve clarity and reproducibility.
weaknesses: These are major methodological changes not reflected in the current manuscript, and no updated experiments or efficiency measurements are provided; the subquadratic runtime/memory claims and quality gains remain unverified at submission time.

Key design choices (e.g., block length B and constant factors in overlap-save, dilation schedules and kernel lengths, causal wavelet boundary handling and filter family selection) may significantly impact accuracy and latency but are not yet empirically substantiated.

Replacing softmax attention with FFT cross-correlation and long convolutions changes the inductive biases; the impact on modeling capacity versus strong modern alternatives (state-space/long-convolution models and attention variants) remains unclear without the promised expanded comparisons.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors’ second rebuttal substantially strengthens the technical foundations: the decoder’s masked DFT is replaced by a principled, causal FFT-based convolution with streaming; cross-mixing is redefined as FFT-based linear convolution with clear shapes and indices; and the wavelet branch is specified via causal lifting with biorthogonal 5/3 filters. These address the earlier soundness issues and outline a solid path toward a subquadratic, multi-scale architecture. However, the current submission does not include these corrected algorithms, updated experiments, or efficiency measurements. Given the scale of changes and the absence of corroborating empirical results and fair, expanded baselines at review time, I recommend rejection for now, with encouragement to resubmit once the revised methods and comprehensive evaluations are incorporated.

strengths: The authors provide a technically sound replacement for the flawed masked-DFT decoder via FFT-accelerated causal convolution (overlap-save) with clear complexity statements for training and streaming inference.

They analyze the decoder receptive field vs. latency trade-off, offering a concrete formula for receptive field growth with depth and dilation and a plan to ablate kernel lengths and dilation schedules.

Cross-mixing is clarified as an FFT-based cross-correlation with explicit tensor shapes and proposed stability mechanisms (energy normalization, cosine normalization, temperature scaling, residual gating, and spectral regularization).

The evaluation plan is substantially improved: corrected dataset, stronger and more diverse baselines (e.g., LongT5, RetNet, Hyena/S4, Mamba/RWKV, BigBird/LED), fairness controls, runtime/memory/latency reporting, and statistical significance via bootstrap and multiple seeds.

Scope creep is reduced by explicitly scoping multi-modal fusion to an architectural extension and revising claims to focus on text summarization; reproducibility is addressed with promised pseudocode, dimensionality tables, boundary handling details, and code release.
weaknesses: Despite a strong plan, the current submission still contains the original derivations and limited experiments; the key algorithmic fixes and expanded evaluations are not yet reflected in the manuscript or corroborated by updated results.

The decoder’s expressivity/latency trade-offs (choice of kernel length L and dilation schedules) and the stability/expressiveness of FFT-based cross-correlation versus softmax attention remain unvalidated empirically in the present version.
No concrete runtime, memory, or per-token latency measurements are provided yet, and statistical significance (CIs, multi-seed variance) is still absent from the reported results.
Code and configuration release is deferred to the camera-ready stage, which limits immediate reproducibility and independent validation during review.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal substantially improves the technical soundness and design clarity: the decoder is reformulated as a causal overlap-save convolution with streaming support, encoder–decoder cross-mixing is defined as dimensionally consistent FFT-based cross-correlation with normalization and gating for stability, and the wavelet branch is specified with causal analysis/synthesis. The authors also commit to stronger baselines, fair training protocols, efficiency metrics, statistical rigor, and tighter scoping of claims. However, these are not yet reflected in the submitted manuscript or supported by updated experiments. Critical questions about the decoder’s receptive field vs. latency trade-offs, and the stability/expressiveness of the cross-correlation operator relative to softmax attention, remain unanswered empirically. Given that the present version still exhibits the earlier issues and lacks the expanded evaluation and code, I recommend rejection at this time, with the view that a revised submission incorporating the promised corrections and comprehensive experiments could be competitive.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Core ML architecture and systems efficiency work (subquadratic Transformer via Fourier/Wavelet mixing) aligns with ICML interests in deep learning, optimization, and ML systems.
Contribution type: Algorithmic/methodological advance with complexity analysis and architectural design is in scope for ICML’s ML systems and general ML tracks.

Reviewer feedback notes meaningful soundness improvements planned (replacing masked-DFT with FFT-based causal convolution; clarified FFT cross-mixing), suggesting potential for a rigorous systems/architecture contribution once integrated.
why_it_does_not_fit: Rigor/evaluation gap: Reviewers unanimously flag that the corrected methods and key empirical evidence (runtime/memory, latency, multi-seed CIs, stronger baselines like S4/Hyena/Mamba/RetNet/LongT5) are promised but absent in the current manuscript, which is below ICML’s bar for empirical rigor and reproducibility.

Novelty/significance risk: Reviewers rate novelty and significance low-to-moderate (3–4); given many prior efficient attention/long-sequence models (FNet, Performer, Reformer, Nyströmformer, SSM/long-convolution lines), the incremental value is unclear without the missing comparisons.

Presentation/positioning: Reviewers cite unresolved clarity issues (tensor shapes, boundary handling, pseudocode) and unvalidated design choices (kernel lengths, dilations, overlap-save constants), which lower acceptance likelihood at ICML where methodological clarity and ablation-backed trade-offs are expected.
